> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT523 - FINANCIAL AND COST ACCOUNTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Accounting Concept: Conceptual Framework - Accounting Principles - Accounting Concepts - Accounting Conventions - Capital and Revenue Expenditure: Capital and Revenue Receipts - Capital and Revenue Losses - Deferred Revenue Expenditure - Financial Statements: Understanding and Interpreting Financial Statements - Introduction to Financial Statements: Meaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 1 — FOUNDATIONS OF FINANCIAL ACCOUNTING

## 1.1 What is Accounting? — The Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** *Accounting is the process of recording, classifying, summarizing, analyzing, and interpreting the financial transactions and events of a business entity in a systematic manner, expressed in terms of money, for the purpose of enabling its users to make informed economic decisions.*

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, the discipline is treated as the **"language of business"** — a structured communication system that converts raw business activity into standardized reports called **Financial Statements**.

### 🍕 Intuition (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine a large restaurant kitchen. The Head Chef (Business Owner) needs to know three things every night:
1. How much money came in? (Revenue)
2. How much went out? (Expenses)
3. What is left? (Profit) and what assets/liabilities exist? (Position)

**Accounting is the kitchen's standardized recipe book** — it has rules (Concepts), traditions (Conventions), and formats (Statements) so that any trained accountant, anywhere, can read the same "menu" of numbers and understand the business identically.

### 1.2 The Conceptual Framework — A Bird's-Eye View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Framework of Accounting** refers to the **theoretical structure** of concepts that underlie the preparation and presentation of financial statements. It is essentially a **constitution of accounting** — a set of logical principles, definitions, and standards that guide accountants in recording and reporting financial information.

The framework rests on **four pillars** (as per the IASB / Ind AS / KTU syllabus interpretation):

| Pillar | What It Answers |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Objectives of Financial Reporting** | Why are financial statements prepared? |
| **Qualitative Characteristics** | What makes information useful? |
| **Elements of Financial Statements** | What items are reported? |
| **Recognition & Measurement** | When and how are items recorded? |

---

## 1.3 Accounting Principles, Concepts, and Conventions — The Trinity

A frequently-asked KTU question is the difference between these three. Let us lock the definitions:

> [!NOTE]
> **Accounting Principles** → Broad **general laws / doctrines** that govern the accounting process (e.g., GAAP — Generally Accepted Accounting Principles).
>
> **Accounting Concepts** → **Basic assumptions** on which accounting is based (e.g., Business Entity, Going Concern).
>
> **Accounting Conventions** → **Customary practices** that have gained acceptance over time through usage (e.g., Conservatism, Consistency).

### 🍕 Analogy (Continued)

Think of a courtroom:
- **Principles** = the Constitution (highest law)
- **Concepts** = the Acts of Parliament (specific laws)
- **Conventions** = unwritten customs followed by judges (precedents)

---

## 1.4 Capital and Revenue — The Most Tested Distinction in KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Mis-classification between **Capital** and **Revenue** items is the single largest cause of incorrect profit computation in board exam problems. The examiner tests this in *almost every Part A* of Module 1.

| Term | Meaning | Shown In | Time Benefit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Capital Expenditure** | Spending that creates a long-term asset or improves existing asset | **Balance Sheet (Asset side)** | > 1 year |
| **Revenue Expenditure** | Spending for day-to-day operations | **Profit & Loss A/c (Debit)** | < 1 year |
| **Capital Receipts** | Non-recurring receipts not from operations | **Balance Sheet (Liability side)** | — |
| **Revenue Receipts** | Recurring receipts from normal business | **Profit & Loss A/c (Credit)** | — |
| **Capital Losses** | Non-recurring losses from abnormal events | **P&L A/c (deducted from capital reserve)** | — |
| **Revenue Losses** | Recurring, normal operating losses | **P&L A/c** | — |
| **Deferred Revenue Expenditure** | Revenue in nature but benefits spread over multiple years | **Balance Sheet (Asset side)** | 2-5 years |

> [!WARNING]
> **Deferred Revenue Expenditure** is a *hybrid* — it is **revenue in nature** (not capital) but its benefit **extends beyond the current accounting period**, so it is **deferred** (postponed) and written off over future years. Common KTU examples: heavy advertising campaign expenses, preliminary expenses of a company, research & development costs.

---

## 1.5 Financial Statements — Introduction and Meaning

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** *Financial Statements are structured financial reports that summarize the financial position, financial performance, and cash flows of an entity, prepared in accordance with applicable accounting standards for use by stakeholders — owners, creditors, regulators, and management.*

The **three core financial statements** (also called **Final Accounts** for sole proprietorships / partnerships) are:

1. **Trading and Profit & Loss Account** → shows **performance** (revenue, expenses, profit/loss for the period)
2. **Balance Sheet** → shows **position** (assets, liabilities, capital at a point in time)
3. **Cash Flow Statement** → shows **liquidity** (movement of cash during the period)

> [!TIP]
> For Module 1, KTU focuses only on the **meaning, purpose, and interpretation** of these statements. Detailed preparation of Final Accounts is covered in Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔍 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 The Conceptual Framework — Detailed Layered Architecture

The framework is hierarchical. Let us break it down into operational layers:

### Layer 1: Objectives of Financial Reporting
- Provide information useful for **investment and credit decisions**
- Provide information to help **assess future cash flows**
- Provide information about **economic resources, claims, and changes** in them

### Layer 2: Qualitative Characteristics (Killer Section for Board Exam)
These determine the **"quality"** of accounting information.

| Characteristic | Meaning | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Understandability** | Information must be comprehensible to users with reasonable business knowledge | Avoiding excessive jargon |
| **Relevance** | Information must help decision-making | Reporting contingent liabilities |
| **Reliability (Faithful Representation)** | Information must be free from error and bias | Recording actual transaction price |
| **Comparability** | Information must be comparable across periods and firms | Using same accounting policies year-on-year |

> [!NOTE]
> **Enhancing Qualitative Characteristics** (sub-features that reinforce the four above): *Comparability, Verifiability, Timeliness, Completeness*.

### Layer 3: Elements of Financial Statements
- **Assets** → economic resources controlled by the entity (e.g., machinery, cash)
- **Liabilities** → present obligations (e.g., creditors, loans)
- **Equity** → residual interest of owners
- **Income / Revenue** → inflows / enhancements of assets
- **Expenses** → outflows / depletion of assets

### Layer 4: Recognition and Measurement
- **Recognition** → when to record (e.g., when an asset is acquired)
- **Measurement bases**: Historical Cost, Net Realisable Value, Fair Value, Present Value

---

## 2.2 Complete Catalogue of Accounting Concepts (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme requires students to know **at least 10 core concepts** with definitions and 1-2 line explanations each. The following is the **exhaustive board-ready list**.

| # | Concept | Assumption | Real-World Application |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Business Entity** | Business is separate from owner | Owner's house ≠ Business asset |
| 2 | **Money Measurement** | Only monetary events are recorded | Goodwill of employees NOT recorded |
| 3 | **Going Concern** | Business will continue indefinitely | Depreciate assets, don't value at liquidation |
| 4 | **Accounting Period** | Life of business divided into periods | Quarterly results, annual reports |
| 5 | **Cost** | Assets recorded at acquisition cost | Land bought for ₹10L shown at ₹10L even if market is ₹50L |
| 6 | **Dual Aspect (Duality)** | Every transaction has two aspects: Debit = Credit | Foundation of double-entry bookkeeping |
| 7 | **Revenue Recognition (Realisation)** | Revenue recorded when it is *earned / realised* | Sale on credit recorded at sale, not cash receipt |
| 8 | **Matching** | Expenses matched with revenues of the same period | Cost of goods sold matched with sales revenue |
| 9 | **Full Disclosure** | All material information disclosed | Reporting contingent liabilities in notes |
| 10 | **Consistency** | Same methods used year after year | Same depreciation method every year |
| 11 | **Conservatism (Prudence)** | Anticipate no profit, provide for all losses | Creating provision for doubtful debts |
| 12 | **Materiality** | Only material/significant items reported separately | Pencil for ₹10 debited to stationery, not asset |
| 13 | **Objectivity** | Transactions based on evidence, not personal opinion | Invoice, bill, receipt are primary evidence |
| 14 | **Accrual** | Record income/expense when incurred, not when cash moves | Salary due on 31 March recorded even if paid 5 April |

---

## 2.3 Accounting Conventions — Detailed Explanation

Conventions are **not legally binding** but are followed universally. KTU expects you to know these four:

### Convention 1: Consistency
- **Meaning:** Same accounting method should be used across comparable periods.
- **Why?** To ensure comparability of financial statements year-on-year.
- **Example:** If you used Straight Line Method of depreciation in 2023, continue it in 2024 unless there is a valid reason to change.

### Convention 2: Conservatism / Prudence
- **Meaning:** "Anticipate no profit, but provide for all possible losses."
- **Why?** To ensure the financial position is not overstated.
- **Example:** Provision for doubtful debts, valuing stock at cost **or** NRV (whichever is lower).

### Convention 3: Full Disclosure
- **Meaning:** All material information must be disclosed — even if it is unfavorable.
- **Why?** Stakeholders need complete information for decisions.
- **Example:** Disclosure of pending lawsuits, contingent liabilities, related-party transactions in notes to accounts.

### Convention 4: Materiality
- **Meaning:** Only material / significant items are reported separately. Immaterial items may be aggregated.
- **Why?** To avoid cluttering reports with insignificant details.
- **Example:** A ₹5,000 pen (immaterial) → expensed; A ₹50,00,000 laptop (material) → capitalized as asset.

> [!TIP]
> KTU often asks: *"Materiality and Conservatism are conventions. Discuss."* Use real examples of each.

---

## 2.4 Capital and Revenue Items — Complete Classification Framework

### 2.4.1 Capital Expenditure
**Definition:** Expenditure that results in the **acquisition of a permanent asset** or **improvement in the earning capacity** of an existing asset, the benefit of which **extends beyond the current accounting period**.

**Key Tests (KTU Board Trick Question):**
1. Is the benefit **long-term** (> 1 year)?
2. Does it **add value** to an existing asset, or **create a new asset**?
3. Is it **non-recurring** in nature?
4. Does it **improve earning capacity** or **operational efficiency**?

**Typical Examples:**
- Purchase of land, building, machinery, furniture
- Freight and installation charges of machinery (Capital — because they are part of acquisition cost)
- Legal expenses for acquiring a patent
- Extension / improvement of an existing building
- Cost of purchasing a business

### 2.4.2 Revenue Expenditure
**Definition:** Expenditure incurred for the **day-to-day running of the business**, the benefit of which is **consumed within the current accounting period**.

**Typical Examples:**
- Salaries and wages
- Rent, rates, and taxes
- Repairs and maintenance (to maintain, not improve)
- Selling and distribution expenses
- Office and administration expenses

### 2.4.3 Capital Receipts
**Definition:** A **non-recurring receipt** that is **not from the normal course of business operations** and does **not affect the profit** of the period.

**Examples:**
- Loan raised from bank or financial institution
- Capital introduced by the proprietor / partners
- Proceeds from sale of a fixed asset
- Premium received on issue of shares

### 2.4.4 Revenue Receipts
**Definition:** A **recurring receipt** arising from the **normal business operations** of the entity.

**Examples:**
- Cash sales / credit sales of goods
- Commission received
- Interest received on deposits
- Rent received (from letting out property as business)

### 2.4.5 Capital Losses
**Definition:** Losses arising from **abnormal, non-recurring events** that are not part of regular business operations.

**Treatment:** Shown in the **Profit & Loss A/c on the debit side** but generally **deducted from capital reserve / general reserve** to preserve working capital.

**Examples:**
- Loss on sale of a fixed asset
- Loss due to theft of fixed asset (abnormal)
- Loss from a sudden government nationalization (hypothetical)

### 2.4.6 Revenue Losses
**Definition:** Losses arising from **normal, recurring business operations**.

**Treatment:** Shown in **Profit & Loss A/c on the debit side** as a normal expense.

**Examples:**
- Bad debts
- Loss on sale of stock-in-trade (normal scrap)
- Discount allowed

### 2.4.7 Deferred Revenue Expenditure (DRE)
**Definition:** Expenditure that is **revenue in nature** (not creating an asset) but whose **benefit extends over multiple future accounting periods**. It is therefore **deferred** (postponed) and **written off** over those years.

> [!WARNING]
> **Why is it shown in the Balance Sheet?** Because the *future benefit* is treated as an **intangible asset**. It is *not* a tangible asset.

**Examples (most-tested):**
- **Preliminary expenses** of a newly formed company (legal, registration, etc.)
- **Heavy advertising expenditure** for launching a new product
- **Research and development expenditure** (sometimes)
- **Brokerage / commission** paid to underwriters for raising capital
- **Material losses** in the first year of business (e.g., heavy training costs)

**Treatment Algorithm (Board Pattern):**
- Incur → Capitalize as **Asset (Deferred Revenue Expenditure A/c)** in Balance Sheet
- Each year → **Write off** a portion to **Profit & Loss A/c** until fully written off

**Mathematical Representation (Spread of DRE):**

$$
\text{Amount to be written off per year} = \frac{\text{Total DRE Incurred}}{\text{Estimated benefit period in years}}
$$

For example, if ₹5,00,000 spent on advertising to be spread over 5 years:

$$
\text{Annual write-off} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{5} = 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

---

## 2.5 Financial Statements — Detailed Meaning & Components

### 2.5.1 Definition (Expanded, Board-Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Financial Statements** are the **end product** of the entire accounting process. They are formal, structured documents presented to stakeholders to communicate the financial affairs of an entity. They are prepared from the **Trial Balance** (a summary of all ledger balances) at the end of the accounting period.

### 2.5.2 Components of Financial Statements

| Component | What It Shows | Format |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Trading Account** | Gross Profit / Gross Loss | Revenue from sales − Cost of Goods Sold |
| **Profit & Loss Account** | Net Profit / Net Loss | Gross Profit − Operating Expenses |
| **Balance Sheet** | Financial Position | Assets = Liabilities + Capital |
| **Cash Flow Statement** | Cash Movements | Operating, Investing, Financing activities |
| **Statement of Changes in Equity** | Movement in Owners' Equity | Opening Capital + Profit − Drawings = Closing Capital |
| **Notes to Accounts** | Disclosures | Accounting policies, contingent liabilities, etc. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Note:** The **Cash Flow Statement** is mandatory for **companies** under the Companies Act, 2013 (and Ind AS 7). It is not mandatory for sole proprietorships and partnerships, but most of them prepare a **Statement of Affairs** as a substitute.

### 2.5.3 The Fundamental Accounting Equation

This is the **heart of the Balance Sheet** and the basis of double-entry bookkeeping.

$$
\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Owners' Equity (Capital)}
$$

This can be rearranged as:

$$
\text{Owners' Equity} = \text{Assets} - \text{Liabilities}
$$

Expanded form (with the **accounting period effect**):

$$
\text{Ending Capital} = \text{Beginning Capital} + \text{Net Profit} - \text{Drawings} + \text{Additional Capital}
$$

### 2.5.4 The Matching Equation (For P&L A/c)

$$
\text{Net Profit} = \text{Total Revenue} - \text{Total Expenses}
$$

Expanded for a manufacturing / trading concern:

$$
\text{Gross Profit} = \text{Net Sales} - \text{Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)}
$$

$$
\text{Net Profit} = \text{Gross Profit} + \text{Other Income} - \text{Operating Expenses} - \text{Depreciation}
$$

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

> [!TIP]
> Save this table for the day of the exam. Most KTU numericals in Module 1 are built on these formulas.

| Sl. | Formula | Symbol Form | Purpose |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Balance Sheet Equation | $A = L + C$ | Foundation of double-entry |
| 2 | Ending Capital | $C_{end} = C_{begin} + P - D + I$ | Closing capital after adjustments |
| 3 | Net Profit | $NP = TR - TE$ | Profit & Loss A/c final value |
| 4 | Gross Profit | $GP = S - COGS$ | Trading A/c final value |
| 5 | COGS | $COGS = \text{Opening Stock} + P - \text{Closing Stock}$ | Cost of goods sold in a period |
| 6 | DRE Annual Write-off | $DRE_{annual} = \frac{DRE_{total}}{n}$ | Spreading deferred expenses |
| 7 | Current Ratio | $CR = \frac{CA}{CL}$ | Liquidity test |
| 8 | Quick Ratio | $QR = \frac{CA - \text{Stock}}{CL}$ | Acid test / immediate liquidity |
| 9 | Debt-Equity Ratio | $DE = \frac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{C}$ | Capital structure |
| 10 | Profit Margin | $PM = \frac{NP}{S} \times 100$ | Profitability in % |

*(CA = Current Assets, CL = Current Liabilities, P = Profit, D = Drawings, I = Introduction, S = Sales, n = years)*

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

These principles are **not just textbook knowledge**. In practice:

- **Going Concern** → determines whether a company values assets at cost or liquidation value. Critical during **COVID-19 / economic crises**.
- **Conservatism** → prevents companies from overstating profits (a lesson from the **2008 financial crisis**).
- **Matching Concept** → enables the **accrual basis of accounting** (mandatory under Ind AS / IFRS).
- **Capital vs Revenue** → directly affects **Income Tax Act, 1961** — the tax treatment of an item depends on its classification as capital or revenue.
- **DRE** → affects **how startups and new companies** show their preliminary expenses in the Balance Sheet (relevant for banks when assessing loan applications).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Dual Aspect Concept

> [!NOTE]
> The Dual Aspect Concept is the **foundation of the entire double-entry system**. It is mathematical, not just conceptual. Let us derive it.

### Step 1: Definition
> *Every business transaction has two aspects: a **debit** and a **credit** of equal amount.*

### Step 2: Symbolic Representation

Let $T$ be the value of a transaction. The debit aspect $D$ and credit aspect $C$ are related as:

$$
D = C
$$

### Step 3: Aggregation Over All Transactions
Summing across all transactions in a period:

$$
\sum D = \sum C
$$

### Step 4: Translation to the Balance Sheet Equation
In every transaction, the **debit** affects either an **Asset / Expense**, and the **credit** affects either a **Liability / Equity / Income**.

For any given moment, the total of all debit balances equals total of all credit balances:

$$
\text{Total Debits} = \text{Total Credits}
$$

Translating into the elements of a Balance Sheet:

$$
\text{Assets} + \text{Expenses} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Equity} + \text{Income}
$$

### Step 5: Simplification at Balance Sheet Date
At the end of the accounting period, **expenses and income** are closed to the P&L A/c and transferred to **Equity** (Capital). This reduces to:

$$
\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Equity}
$$

$$
\boxed{A = L + E}
$$

This is the **fundamental accounting equation** — it is *not* assumed; it is *derived* from the Dual Aspect Concept.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** If a KTU Part A question asks *"Derive the Accounting Equation from the Dual Aspect Concept"*, walk through Steps 1 to 5 above. Marks are awarded for the **balanced equation** and the **logical sequence**.

---

## 3.2 Classification Algorithm — Capital vs Revenue Expenditure (Step-by-Step Decision Tree)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU questions often present a **list of 8-10 items** and ask: *"Classify each item as Capital or Revenue Expenditure."* Use this algorithm.

### Step 1: Ask — Does the item create a new asset OR add value to an existing asset?
- **Yes** → Capital Expenditure
- **No** → Move to Step 2

### Step 2: Ask — Is the benefit long-term (> 1 year)?
- **Yes** → Capital Expenditure
- **No** → Move to Step 3

### Step 3: Ask — Is it for day-to-day operations?
- **Yes** → Revenue Expenditure
- **No** → Move to Step 4

### Step 4: Ask — Is it revenue in nature but with multi-period benefit?
- **Yes** → **Deferred Revenue Expenditure**
- **No** → Revenue Expenditure (default)

### Worked Example (Board Pattern)
> *Classify the following:*
> 1. ₹50,000 spent on purchase of machinery
> 2. ₹5,000 spent on repair of machinery
> 3. ₹2,00,000 spent on advertising a new product launch (expected to benefit 5 years)
> 4. ₹30,000 paid as rent for office
> 5. ₹15,000 paid for installation of newly purchased machinery

| Item | Step 1 | Step 2 | Step 3 | Step 4 | Classification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Creates asset (Yes) | — | — | — | **Capital Expenditure** |
| 2 | No (only maintenance) | No | Yes (day-to-day) | — | **Revenue Expenditure** |
| 3 | No | Yes (> 1 yr) | No (not routine) | **Yes** | **Deferred Revenue Expenditure** |
| 4 | No | No | Yes (day-to-day) | — | **Revenue Expenditure** |
| 5 | Part of acquisition (Yes) | — | — | — | **Capital Expenditure** (add to machinery cost) |

---

## 3.3 Treatment of Deferred Revenue Expenditure — Numerical Walkthrough

### Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern)
> A newly incorporated company spent the following in 2023:
> - Preliminary expenses: ₹6,00,000
> - Advertising for product launch: ₹12,00,000 (benefit expected for 6 years)
> - Brokerage on share issue: ₹3,00,000 (benefit expected for 3 years)
>
> Show the treatment in the Balance Sheet as on 31.12.2023 and the amount to be written off in P&L A/c in 2023, 2024, 2025.

### Step 1: Identify the DRE items
All three are **revenue in nature** but have **multi-year benefit** → classify as **Deferred Revenue Expenditure**.

### Step 2: Compute total DRE

$$
DRE_{total} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 21{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 3: Compute annual write-off for each item
Each item has a different benefit period, so they must be written off separately (do **not** average them).

$$
\text{Preliminary Expenses annual write-off} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{6} = 1{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Advertising annual write-off} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{6} = 2{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Brokerage annual write-off} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{3} = 1{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 4: Compute Balance Sheet treatment (as on 31.12.2023, first year)

**Original amounts** (capitalized as DRE asset):

| Item | Original Amount | Written off in 2023 | Balance in B/S (as on 31.12.2023) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Preliminary Expenses | 6,00,000 | 1,00,000 | 5,00,000 |
| Advertising | 12,00,000 | 2,00,000 | 10,00,000 |
| Brokerage | 3,00,000 | 1,00,000 | 2,00,000 |
| **Total DRE Asset** | **21,00,000** | **4,00,000** | **17,00,000** |

### Step 5: P&L A/c Debit (2023)

$$
\text{Deferred Revenue Expenditure Written Off} = 1{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000
$$

This ₹4,00,000 will appear as an **expense in the P&L A/c for 2023**.

### Step 6: 2024 and 2025 P&L A/c
- 2024: Same amount (₹4,00,000) written off
- 2025: Same amount (₹4,00,000) written off
- By 2025-end: Brokerage fully written off
- 2026, 2027, 2028: Only Preliminary + Advertising (₹3,00,000 per year)

### Final Balance Sheet Extract (31.12.2023)

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Assets — Intangible Assets} \\
\text{Deferred Revenue Expenditure} \\
\quad \text{(To be written off in 6 years)} \\
\quad \text{Add: Advertising (12L - 2L)} = 10,00,000 \\
\quad \text{Add: Brokerage (3L - 1L)} = 2,00,000 \\
\quad \text{Add: Preliminary (6L - 1L)} = 5,00,000 \\
\text{Total} = 17,00,000
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often take a **weighted average** of the benefit periods. **Do not** do this. Each DRE item must be **individually** written off over its own benefit period. The KTU examiner allocates 2-3 marks specifically for this.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Gross Profit from Trading Account

### Step 1: Definition
Gross Profit is the **excess of Net Sales over the Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)**.

### Step 2: Formula

$$
GP = \text{Net Sales} - COGS
$$

### Step 3: Expand Net Sales

$$
\text{Net Sales} = \text{Gross Sales} - \text{Sales Returns} - \text{Discount Allowed}
$$

### Step 4: Expand COGS

$$
COGS = \text{Opening Stock} + \text{Purchases} - \text{Purchase Returns} - \text{Closing Stock} + \text{Direct Expenses}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute back

$$
\boxed{
GP = (\text{Sales} - \text{Returns}) - (\text{Opening Stock} + \text{Purchases} - \text{Returns} - \text{Closing Stock} + \text{Direct Expenses})
}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This derivation is the **backbone of Trading Account preparation** which is covered in Module 2. KTU Module 1 may test only the **formula**, but knowing the derivation makes the later module much easier.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Identification of Items

The following table is your **exam-day lifesaver**. It compresses the entire Capital/Revenue framework into a single lookup matrix.

| Item Description | Likely Classification | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Purchase of furniture | **Capital Expenditure** | Creates a new fixed asset |
| Repairs to old furniture | **Revenue Expenditure** | Maintains existing asset, not improvement |
| Legal fees for acquiring a patent | **Capital Expenditure** | Part of patent acquisition cost |
| Legal fees for a court case (defense) | **Revenue Expenditure** | Day-to-day legal expense |
| Wages paid to factory workers | **Revenue Expenditure** | Direct operating cost |
| Wages paid for installation of new machine | **Capital Expenditure** | Part of machine cost |
| Heavy advertising for new product | **Deferred Revenue Expenditure** | Revenue in nature, multi-year benefit |
| Brokerage on issue of shares | **Deferred Revenue Expenditure** | Revenue in nature, multi-year benefit |
| Preliminary expenses of a new company | **Deferred Revenue Expenditure** | Revenue in nature, multi-year benefit |
| Interest paid on loan taken | **Revenue Expenditure** | Recurring operating cost |
| Interest paid on capital borrowed to acquire fixed asset (before use) | **Capital Expenditure** | Capitalized as part of asset cost |
| Sale of old machinery | **Capital Receipt** | Non-recurring, not from operations |
| Cash sales of goods | **Revenue Receipt** | Recurring, from operations |
| Loss on sale of fixed asset | **Capital Loss** | Non-recurring, abnormal |
| Bad debts | **Revenue Loss** | Recurring, normal |
| Compensation received from government for land acquisition | **Capital Receipt** | Non-recurring, not from operations |
| Discount received from suppliers | **Revenue Receipt** | Recurring, from operations |

---

## 3.6 Solving the Trial Balance → Balance Sheet Linkage (Module 1 Theoretical)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Trial Balance** is the *bridge* between the **Ledger** and the **Financial Statements**. The complete flow is:

$$
\text{Journal} \rightarrow \text{Ledger} \rightarrow \text{Trial Balance} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Trading A/c} \rightarrow \text{P\&L A/c} \\ \text{Balance Sheet} \end{cases}
$$

**At the Trial Balance level, accounts are classified as:**

- **Real Accounts** → Asset / Liability → go to **Balance Sheet**
- **Nominal Accounts** → Income / Expense → go to **P&L A/c**
- **Personal Accounts** → Parties / Capital → go to **Balance Sheet** (or P&L for drawings, interest on capital, etc.)

This is the **"Golden Rules of Accounting"** mapped to financial statements:

| Account Type | Rule | Goes To |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Real | Debit what comes in, Credit what goes out | Balance Sheet |
| Nominal | Debit all expenses and losses, Credit all incomes and gains | P&L A/c |
| Personal | Debit the receiver, Credit the giver | Balance Sheet |

> [!TIP]
> KTU may test: *"Why does Drawings appear in the Balance Sheet and not in P&L A/c?"* Answer: Drawings are *not* an expense; they are a *reduction of capital* (personal account).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Conceptual Framework of Accounting (Hierarchical Flow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **KTU 2024 Conceptual Framework** as a top-down hierarchy from objectives to elements.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OBJ OF FINANCIAL REPORTING\nDecision Usefulness\nResource Allocation\nPerformance Assessment] --> B[QUALITATIVE CHARACTERISTICS\nRelevance\nReliability\nComparability\nUnderstandability]
    B --> C[ELEMENTS OF FINANCIAL STATEMENTS\nAssets\nLiabilities\nEquity\nIncome\nExpenses]
    C --> D[RECOGNITION RULES\nWhen to Record\nMateriality Test\nProbability Test]
    D --> E[MEASUREMENT BASES\nHistorical Cost\nNet Realisable Value\nFair Value\nPresent Value]
    E --> F[FINANCIAL STATEMENTS\nP and L A/c\nBalance Sheet\nCash Flow Statement\nStatement of Changes in Equity\nNotes to Accounts]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The hierarchy flows downward: **Why** (Objectives) → **What makes it good** (Qualitative) → **What to report** (Elements) → **When to report** (Recognition) → **How to value** (Measurement) → **Final Output** (Financial Statements).

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Decision Tree for Capital vs Revenue Classification

> [!TIP]
> This is the **most useful diagram for KTU Part A 3-mark questions** where a list of items is given and classification is asked.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[START: Item Received] --> Q{Does it create a new\nfixed asset OR\nadd value to existing asset?}
    Q -- YES --> R[CAPITAL EXPENDITURE]
    Q -- NO --> S{Is the benefit\nmore than 1 year?}
    S -- YES --> T{Is it revenue in nature\nbut multi-period benefit?}
    T -- YES --> U[DEFERRED REVENUE EXPENDITURE]
    T -- NO --> V[CAPITAL EXPENDITURE]
    S -- NO --> W{Is it for day-to-day\noperations?}
    W -- YES --> X[REVENUE EXPENDITURE]
    W -- NO --> Y[DEFERRED REVENUE EXPENDITURE]
    R --> Z[Balance Sheet Asset Side]
    V --> Z
    U --> AA[Balance Sheet Asset Side\nWritten off over years]
    X --> AB[P and L A/c Debit Side]
    Y --> AA
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Components of Financial Statements (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TB[TRIAL BALANCE] --> TR[TRADING ACCOUNT\nGross Profit / Loss]
    TR --> PL[PROFIT AND LOSS A/C\nNet Profit / Loss]
    PL --> BS[BALANCE SHEET\nFinancial Position]
    PL --> CF[CASH FLOW STATEMENT\nLiquidity Position]
    PL --> CE[STATEMENT OF CHANGES IN EQUITY\nCapital Movement]
    BS --> NA[NOTES TO ACCOUNTS\nDisclosures]
    CF --> NA
    CE --> NA
    TR --> NA
    PL --> NA
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Trial Balance** is the **single source** of all financial statements. The **Trading A/c** feeds into the **P&L A/c**, and together they determine the final year-end position shown in the **Balance Sheet**, **Cash Flow Statement**, and **Statement of Changes in Equity**. **Notes to Accounts** are the supporting narrative attached to every statement.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Mapping Accounting Concepts to Real-World Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Entity Concept] --> R1[Owner personal\npurchases not\nin business books]
    C2[Going Concern] --> R2[Fixed assets\ndepreciated over\nyears not liquidation]
    C3[Dual Aspect] --> R3[Every entry has\nequal Debit\nand Credit]
    C4[Matching] --> R4[Expenses matched\nwith revenue of\nsame period]
    C5[Conservatism] --> R5[Stock valued at\ncost or NRV\nwhichever is lower]
    C6[Accrual] --> R6[Outstanding\nexpenses and\nincomes recorded]
    C7[Materiality] --> R7[Small items\nexpensed\nnot capitalised]
    C8[Consistency] --> R8[Same method\nused year\nafter year]
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Classification of Receipts and Losses (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ALL RECEIPTS] --> B{Came from normal\nbusiness operations?}
    B -- YES --> C[REVENUE RECEIPT\nGoes to P and L Credit]
    B -- NO --> D[CAPITAL RECEIPT\nGoes to Balance Sheet Liability]
    E[ALL LOSSES] --> F{Recurring and\nnormal?}
    F -- YES --> G[REVENUE LOSS\nGoes to P and L Debit]
    F -- NO --> H[CAPITAL LOSS\nGoes to P and L Debit\nAdjusted from Reserves]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## 📌 PART A QUESTIONS (Each 3 Marks)

### **Question 1 (3 marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define the following terms:**
(a) Accounting Concept
(b) Accounting Convention
(c) Capital Expenditure

### **Model Answer:**

> **(a) Accounting Concept** → The basic assumptions or conditions on which the entire accounting system is built. They provide the logical foundation for recording and reporting financial transactions.
>
> **Examples:** Business Entity, Going Concern, Money Measurement, Dual Aspect, Matching, Accrual.
>
> **(b) Accounting Convention** → The customary practices, traditions, and conventions that have gained universal acceptance over time through consistent usage in the profession of accounting. They are not legally binding but are universally followed.
>
> **Examples:** Consistency, Conservatism (Prudence), Full Disclosure, Materiality.
>
> **(c) Capital Expenditure** → An expenditure incurred for acquiring a permanent asset, or for improving the earning capacity of an existing asset, the benefit of which extends over more than one accounting period. It is shown as an asset in the Balance Sheet.
>
> **Examples:** Purchase of machinery, land, building; legal expenses for acquiring a patent.

---

### **Question 2 (3 marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]`
**Distinguish between Capital Receipts and Revenue Receipts.**

### **Model Answer:**

| Basis of Distinction | Capital Receipts | Revenue Receipts |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Source** | Not from normal business operations | From regular business activities |
| **Nature** | Non-recurring | Recurring |
| **Effect on Profit** | Does not affect profit | Affects the profit of the period |
| **Treatment** | Shown in Balance Sheet (Liability side) | Shown in P&L A/c (Credit side) |
| **Examples** | Loans, capital introduced, sale of fixed asset | Sales, commission, interest received |

> Capital receipts are not matched with expenses; revenue receipts are matched with revenue expenditure of the same period.

---

### **Question 3 (3 marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model]`
**What is Deferred Revenue Expenditure? Give two examples.**

### **Model Answer:**

> **Deferred Revenue Expenditure (DRE)** is expenditure that is **revenue in nature** (does not create a tangible asset) but whose **benefit extends over several future accounting periods**. Such expenditure is **capitalized** in the Balance Sheet as an intangible asset and **written off** gradually over the period of its benefit.
>
> **Examples:**
> 1. **Preliminary expenses** of a newly formed company (legal, registration, stamp duty on MOA/AOA).
> 2. **Heavy advertising expenses** incurred for launching a new product expected to generate benefits for several years.
> 3. **Brokerage / underwriting commission** paid for raising share capital.
>
> **Treatment:** Capitalized as asset in Balance Sheet → Written off portion-wise to P&L A/c each year.

---

### **Question 4 (3 marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model]`
**List and explain any three accounting concepts.**

### **Model Answer:**

> **(1) Business Entity Concept** — The business is treated as a separate entity distinct from its owner. The personal transactions of the owner are not recorded in the books of the business. *Example: Owner's house is not the business's asset.*
>
> **(2) Going Concern Concept** — It is assumed that the business will continue to operate for an indefinite period and will not be liquidated in the foreseeable future. Hence, assets are valued at cost and depreciated, not at liquidation value.
>
> **(3) Matching Concept** — Expenses incurred in a period should be matched with the revenues earned in that same period to determine the true profit or loss. *Example: Cost of goods sold is matched against sales revenue of the same year.*

---

## 📌 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) format for Module 1 — students must answer **one full 14-mark question** with both sub-parts (a) and (b).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Explain any **seven accounting concepts** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Distinguish between **Capital Expenditure and Revenue Expenditure**. Explain the **treatment of Deferred Revenue Expenditure** in financial statements with an example. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution (a) — Seven Accounting Concepts:**

> **1. Business Entity Concept** — Business is distinct from owner. The owner's personal transactions are kept out of the business books. *Example: Mr. A paying his son's school fees from business cash is not a business expense.*
>
> **2. Money Measurement Concept** — Only transactions measurable in monetary terms are recorded. *Example: The skill of the manager is not recorded; the salary paid to him is.*
>
> **3. Going Concern Concept** — Business is assumed to continue indefinitely. *Example: Buildings are depreciated over 30 years, not valued at scrap value today.*
>
> **4. Accounting Period Concept** — The life of the business is divided into artificial time periods (e.g., 1 year) for reporting. *Example: Annual financial statements for the year ended 31 March.*
>
> **5. Dual Aspect Concept** — Every transaction has two aspects: debit and credit of equal value. *Foundation of double-entry bookkeeping.*
>
> **6. Matching Concept** — Expenses of a period are matched against revenues of the same period. *Example: Depreciation of the year is matched with the year's revenue.*
>
> **7. Conservatism (Prudence) Concept** — Anticipate no profit, but provide for all possible losses. *Example: Creating provision for doubtful debts, valuing stock at cost or NRV whichever is lower.*

### **Model Solution (b) — Capital vs Revenue + DRE:**

> **Distinction Table:**
>
> | Basis | Capital Expenditure | Revenue Expenditure |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Nature** | Creates / improves long-term asset | Day-to-day operating cost |
> | **Benefit** | More than one accounting period | Within the same period |
> | **Recurring** | Non-recurring | Recurring |
> | **Treatment** | Balance Sheet (Asset side) | P&L A/c (Debit side) |
> | **Examples** | Purchase of machinery, building | Salaries, rent, repairs |
>
> **Treatment of DRE:**
>
> DRE is shown as an **asset in the Balance Sheet** (under the head *Miscellaneous Expenditure* or *Intangible Assets*). A portion is **written off each year** to the P&L A/c over the period of benefit.
>
> **Example:** A company spends ₹6,00,000 on preliminary expenses and ₹12,00,000 on product launch advertising (expected to benefit 6 years). Total DRE = ₹18,00,000. Annual write-off = ₹18,00,000 ÷ 6 = ₹3,00,000 per year.
>
> - **Year 1 P&L A/c Debit:** ₹3,00,000
> - **Year 1 Balance Sheet (Asset side):** ₹15,00,000 (₹18,00,000 - ₹3,00,000)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model — Internal Choice Alternative]`

**(a)** What are **Financial Statements**? Explain the **different components** of Financial Statements and their purpose. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Conceptual Framework of Accounting** in detail. Discuss the **qualitative characteristics** that make accounting information useful. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution (a) — Financial Statements:**

> **Definition:** Financial Statements are structured reports that summarize the financial position, financial performance, and cash flows of an entity. They are the end-product of the accounting process and are prepared from the Trial Balance.
>
> **Components:**
>
> | Component | Purpose | What It Shows |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Trading A/c** | Compute Gross Profit | Sales − COGS |
> | **P&L A/c** | Compute Net Profit | Gross Profit − Operating Expenses |
> | **Balance Sheet** | Show financial position at a point in time | Assets, Liabilities, Capital |
> | **Cash Flow Statement** | Track cash movements across operating, investing, financing | Liquidity analysis |
> | **Notes to Accounts** | Disclose additional information | Accounting policies, contingencies |
>
> **Limitations (briefly):** Historical cost basis, ignores price level changes, qualitative factors not captured, may be affected by window dressing, different accounting policies may affect comparability.

### **Model Solution (b) — Conceptual Framework & Qualitative Characteristics:**

> **Conceptual Framework** is the theoretical foundation of financial accounting. It is a coherent system of interconnected objectives, fundamentals, and concepts that prescribes the nature, function, and limits of financial accounting and financial statements.
>
> **Layers:**
> 1. **Objectives** → Decision usefulness, resource allocation, performance evaluation
> 2. **Qualitative Characteristics** → The "quality attributes" of accounting information
> 3. **Elements** → Assets, Liabilities, Equity, Income, Expenses
> 4. **Recognition & Measurement** → When and how to record
>
> **Qualitative Characteristics:**
>
> - **Understandability** — Information should be clear and comprehensible.
> - **Relevance** — Information should help users make decisions.
> - **Reliability (Faithful Representation)** — Information should be free from bias and error.
> - **Comparability** — Information should be comparable across periods and firms.
> - *(Enhancing characteristics: Verifiability, Timeliness, Completeness)*

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — READ CAREFULLY**
>
> 1. **Confusing Concepts with Conventions** — Most students lose 2-3 marks by writing Conservatism as a concept instead of a convention. Memorize: **Consistency, Conservatism, Full Disclosure, Materiality** are **Conventions**.
>
> 2. **Averaging DRE Benefit Periods** — When multiple DRE items have different benefit periods, NEVER take a weighted average. Each item must be written off individually over its own period. Examiner deducts 2-3 marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Equation Derivation** — If asked to derive A = L + C, students often just state the equation. KTU awards marks for the **logical flow from Dual Aspect to the equation**. You must show steps.
>
> 4. **Mixing Capital and Revenue Receipts** — A loan is a **Capital Receipt** (Balance Sheet), not revenue. A commission received is a **Revenue Receipt** (P&L). Common confusion.
>
> 5. **Ignoring "Materiality" in Classification** — In practical problems, students must apply the materiality test: small items (e.g., a ₹2,000 stapler) are expensed (revenue), while large items (e.g., a ₹5,00,000 server) are capitalized.
>
> 6. **Treatment of Drawings** — Drawings are a **deduction from capital**, not a P&L expense. The closing capital formula is: **Capital = Opening Capital + Profit − Drawings + Introduction**.

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> Read this section **30 minutes before the exam** for last-mile preparation.

### 🔹 Core Definitions to Memorize (Verbatim for 2-3 mark questions)
- **Accounting** → Recording, classifying, summarizing, analyzing, interpreting financial transactions.
- **Conceptual Framework** → Theoretical structure of concepts underlying financial statements.
- **Capital Expenditure** → Spending on long-term assets (> 1 year benefit), shown in Balance Sheet.
- **Revenue Expenditure** → Spending for day-to-day operations, shown in P&L A/c.
- **Capital Receipt** → Non-recurring receipt not from operations, shown in Balance Sheet.
- **Revenue Receipt** → Recurring receipt from operations, shown in P&L A/c.
- **Capital Loss** → Non-recurring abnormal loss, charged to P&L but adjusted from reserves.
- **Revenue Loss** → Recurring normal loss, charged to P&L A/c.
- **Deferred Revenue Expenditure** → Revenue in nature, multi-period benefit, written off gradually.
- **Financial Statements** → Structured reports showing financial position, performance, and cash flows.

### 🔹 Top 14 Accounting Concepts (Know Definition + 1 Example Each)
1. Business Entity  2. Money Measurement  3. Going Concern  4. Accounting Period
5. Cost  6. Dual Aspect  7. Revenue Recognition  8. Matching
9. Full Disclosure  10. Consistency  11. Conservatism  12. Materiality
13. Objectivity  14. Accrual

### 🔹 Top 4 Accounting Conventions
1. Consistency  2. Conservatism  3. Full Disclosure  4. Materiality

### 🔹 Key Formulas to Memorize
- $A = L + C$ (Balance Sheet Equation)
- $C_{end} = C_{begin} + P - D + I$ (Closing Capital)
- $GP = S - COGS$ (Gross Profit)
- $NP = TR - TE$ (Net Profit)
- $DRE_{annual} = DRE_{total} \div n$ (DRE Write-off)

### 🔹 DRE Items (Most-Listed in KTU Papers)
- Preliminary expenses of a new company
- Heavy advertising / product launch expenses
- Brokerage and underwriting commission on share issue
- Material abnormal losses in the first year
- Research & development costs (in some cases)

### 🔹 Common Exam Traps
- "Loss on sale of fixed asset" → **Capital Loss** (not Revenue Loss)
- "Brokerage on shares" → **DRE** (not Capital Expenditure)
- "Repairs to existing asset" → **Revenue** (not Capital)
- "Interest on capital borrowed to buy a fixed asset (before use)" → **Capital** (not Revenue)
- "Discount received" → **Revenue Receipt** (not Capital Receipt)

### 🔹 Components of Financial Statements (Memory Aid: **T-P-B-C-N**)
**T**rading A/c → **P**&L A/c → **B**alance Sheet → **C**ash Flow Statement → **N**otes to Accounts

### 🔹 The Golden Rule Mnemonic
- **Real Account:** *D*ebit the **C**oming, **C**redit the **G**oing
- **Nominal Account:** *D*ebit all **E**xpenses, **C**redit all **I**ncomes
- **Personal Account:** *D*ebit the **R**eceiver, **C**redit the **G**iver

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes.** For the next module, focus will shift to **Preparation of Final Accounts** (Trading, P&L, Balance Sheet) and the **Accounting Process Cycle**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types and Techniques of Financial Statement Analysis: Comparative Statement, Common Size Statement, Trend Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT523 - FINANCIAL AND COST ACCOUNTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types and Techniques of Financial Statement Analysis: Comparative Statement, Common Size Statement, Trend Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Financial Statement Analysis (FSA) — The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Financial Statement Analysis is the process of examining and evaluating an organization's financial statements (Balance Sheet, Income Statement, Cash Flow Statement) to understand its financial health, performance, and to make informed economic decisions.*

Financial Statement Analysis involves interpreting numerical data presented in financial statements to assess:
- **Profitability** (How much profit is the firm generating?)
- **Liquidity** (Can the firm pay short-term obligations?)
- **Solvency** (Can the firm survive in the long run?)
- **Efficiency** (How well are assets being utilized?)

### Conceptual Analogy 🧠

Imagine you are a **doctor examining a patient**. You do not just look at one vital sign — you check temperature, blood pressure, heart rate, sugar level, and several other parameters **over multiple visits** to understand whether the patient is healthy, deteriorating, or recovering.

Similarly, a financial analyst is like a **financial doctor**. They don't just glance at one year's profit. They analyze data **across multiple years**, compare it with industry standards, and use various techniques to diagnose the financial health of a business.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Financial Statement Analysis is **NOT** about preparing the statements. It is about **interpreting** them to derive meaningful conclusions.

---

## 1.2 Three Major Techniques of FSA (KTU Module 1 Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 emphasizes THREE specific techniques:**
> 1. **Comparative Statements** (Horizontal Analysis)
> 2. **Common Size Statements** (Vertical Analysis)
> 3. **Trend Analysis** (Time-Series Analysis)

### Intuitive Overview of the Three Techniques

| Technique | Core Question Answered | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Comparative Statement** | "How much did each item change from last year?" | Comparing your **this year's salary** vs **last year's salary** |
| **Common Size Statement** | "What is each item's share of the total?" | Showing what % of your **monthly budget** goes to rent, food, travel |
| **Trend Analysis** | "What has been the **pattern** over the years?" | Tracking your **weight over 10 years** to see the long-term trend |

---

## 1.3 Internal Users vs External Users

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Financial Analysis Matters:**
> - **Internal Users:** Management, employees, owners → for decision-making and planning.
> - **External Users:** Investors, creditors, banks, government, analysts → for investment, lending, and regulatory decisions.

---

## 1.4 Comparative Statement Analysis (Horizontal Analysis)

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparative Statement:** A financial statement that presents the figures for **two or more accounting periods side by side** to facilitate a study of changes in absolute amounts (₹) and in percentages (%) from one period to another.

### Two Sub-Types
1. **Comparative Balance Sheet** — Compares assets, liabilities, and equity across years
2. **Comparative Income Statement (Profit & Loss A/c)** — Compares revenues and expenses across years

### Conceptual Analogy 🧠

Think of it like a **before-and-after fitness comparison photo**. If you were 70 kg last year and 75 kg this year, that's an absolute change of **+5 kg** and a percentage change of **+7.14%**. The Comparative Statement does exactly this — but for every financial line item.

### Key Standard Metric

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Output Columns in Comparative Statements:**
> - **Absolute Change** = Current Year (₹) − Base Year (₹)
> - **Percentage Change** = (Absolute Change ÷ Base Year) × **100**

---

## 1.5 Common Size Statement Analysis (Vertical Analysis)

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Size Statement:** A financial statement in which **each item is expressed as a percentage of a common base** (a single significant total), so that comparisons can be made easily between firms of different sizes or between periods of the same firm.

### Two Sub-Types
1. **Common Size Balance Sheet** — Each item as % of **Total Assets (or Total Liabilities = Total Equity)**
2. **Common Size Income Statement** — Each item as % of **Net Sales (Revenue)**

### Conceptual Analogy 🧠

Imagine two friends — **Arjun earns ₹50,000/month** and **Vikram earns ₹5,00,000/month**. If Arjun saves ₹10,000 and Vikram saves ₹50,000, raw numbers are misleading. But in **percentage terms**, both save **20%**. This normalization is exactly what Common Size Statements do — they remove the effect of **size** and reveal the **structure**.

### Key Standard Metric

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Base Convention:**
> - Balance Sheet → **Total = 100%** (Total Assets or Total of Liabilities & Equity)
> - Income Statement → **Net Sales (Revenue) = 100%**

---

## 1.6 Trend Analysis (Time-Series / Index Number Analysis)

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trend Analysis:** A technique of financial analysis that studies the **movement of financial data over a series of years** (usually 3 to 10 years), where one year is selected as the **base year (= 100)** and the figures of other years are expressed as a percentage (trend percentage) of the base year figure.

### Conceptual Analogy 🧠

Think of the **Sensex or Nifty index**. In 2014, if Sensex = 25,000 (Base = 100), and in 2024 it is 75,000, the trend percentage is **300%**. This tells investors that the market has **tripled** in 10 years. Trend Analysis applies the same logic to a single company's financial data.

### Key Standard Metric

> [!NOTE]
> **Trend Percentage Formula:**
> $$\text{Trend Percentage} = \left( \frac{\text{Current Year Amount}}{\text{Base Year Amount}} \right) \times 100$$
> **Base Year** is always assigned a value of **100**.

---

## 1.7 Summary of the Three Techniques (One-Page View)

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always remember these three keywords — **HORIZONTAL** (Comparative), **VERTICAL** (Common Size), **TIME-SERIES** (Trend).

| Feature | Comparative Statement | Common Size Statement | Trend Analysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Also Known As** | Horizontal Analysis | Vertical Analysis | Index Analysis |
| **Periods Compared** | 2 or more years | 1 year (or compare % across years) | Multiple years (3–10 yrs) |
| **Base Used** | Previous year | Total (Assets / Sales) | Selected base year |
| **Output** | Absolute + % change | Each item as % of base | Trend % (Base = 100) |
| **Best Used For** | Year-on-year comparison | Structural comparison | Long-term pattern detection |
| **Limitation** | Affected by inflation | Ignores absolute size | Sensitive to base year choice |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparative Statement Analysis — Deep Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Comparative Balance Sheet

The Comparative Balance Sheet presents the **assets, liabilities, and equity** of a firm for two or more accounting periods. The analysis involves:

**Step 1:** Arrange the figures of each item for both years.
**Step 2:** Compute **Absolute Change** = Current Year − Previous Year.
**Step 3:** Compute **Percentage Change** = (Absolute Change / Previous Year) × 100.
**Step 4:** Interpret increases (favourable/unfavourable) and decreases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Logic for KTU:**
> - **Increase in Assets** → Generally Favourable (except increase in fictitious assets)
> - **Increase in Liabilities** → Generally Unfavourable
> - **Increase in Reserves/Surplus** → Favourable
> - **Increase in Share Capital** → Indicates fresh capital infusion (Favourable for growth)

### 2.1.2 Comparative Income Statement

Presents the **revenues and expenses** for two or more years. The same formula applies.

**Interpretation Logic:**
- **Increase in Sales** → Favourable
- **Increase in Net Profit** → Favourable
- **Increase in Expenses at a rate higher than sales** → Unfavourable
- **Increase in Operating Expenses** → Could indicate inefficiency

### 2.1.3 Advantages of Comparative Statements
- Helps in **identifying trends** over short periods
- Reveals the **direction** of change (upward/downward)
- Facilitates **inter-firm comparison**
- Helps in **forecasting** future performance

### 2.1.4 Limitations
- Affected by **inflation / price-level changes**
- Comparison limited to **few periods** (usually 2)
- Ignores **qualitative factors**
- Window dressing by management may distort figures

---

## 2.2 Common Size Statement Analysis — Deep Mechanics

### 2.2.1 Common Size Balance Sheet
Every item on the asset side is expressed as a **percentage of Total Assets**. Every item on the liabilities side is expressed as a **percentage of Total Liabilities (or Total of Liabilities + Equity)**.

$$\text{Each Asset Item \%} = \left( \frac{\text{Item Amount}}{\text{Total Assets}} \right) \times 100$$

$$\text{Each Liability Item \%} = \left( \frac{\text{Item Amount}}{\text{Total of Liabilities + Equity}} \right) \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Total Assets (LHS) must always equal Total Liabilities (RHS), and both must sum to **100%** in the Common Size Balance Sheet.

### 2.2.2 Common Size Income Statement
Every item in the P&L is expressed as a **percentage of Net Sales**.

$$\text{Each P\&L Item \%} = \left( \frac{\text{Item Amount}}{\text{Net Sales}} \right) \times 100$$

### 2.2.3 Why Common Size is Powerful

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> A finance manager can compare the cost structure of two companies of vastly different sizes — say, **Reliance (₹8,00,000 crore)** and **a small MSME (₹100 crore)**. By converting both to percentages, we can ask: *"Which firm has a higher raw material cost ratio?"* — the answer is meaningful regardless of absolute size.

### 2.2.4 Interpretation Guidelines
- A **rising % of raw material** → Inefficiency in procurement
- A **rising % of admin expenses** → Inefficiency in administration
- A **declining % of net profit** → Worsening profitability
- A **rising % of long-term debt** → Increasing financial risk

### 2.2.5 Limitations
- Does not reveal **absolute amounts** (a 5% net profit of ₹1000 crore is huge!)
- Depends on a **single base** (Total)
- Ignores **inter-period dynamics** unless multiple years are converted

---

## 2.3 Trend Analysis — Deep Mechanics

### 2.3.1 Procedure
**Step 1:** Collect data for the relevant financial item over multiple years (typically 5–10 years).
**Step 2:** Select a **base year** (usually the earliest year, or a year of normal operations).
**Step 3:** Assign the base year value as **100**.
**Step 4:** Compute trend percentages for all subsequent years using the formula.
**Step 5:** Plot / interpret the trend.

### 2.3.2 Advantages
- Reveals **long-term patterns** (growth, stagnation, decline)
- Helps in **forecasting** future figures
- Useful for **strategic planning**
- Enables comparison of **different items** on the same scale

### 2.3.3 Limitations
- **Base year selection** is subjective — a poor choice distorts the trend.
- Affected by **inflation** — a 300% growth in nominal terms may be much less in real terms.
- Cannot detect **short-term fluctuations** effectively.
- Does not account for **changes in accounting policies**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Trend % > 100" with "increase" and "Trend % < 100" with "decrease". Always remember: **Trend % = 100 means no change**; **> 100 means growth**; **< 100 means decline**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they form the core of all KTU numerical problems.**

| # | Technique | Formula | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Comparative — Absolute Change | $\Delta A = A_{CY} - A_{PY}$ | Amount in ₹ |
| 2 | Comparative — Percentage Change | $\Delta\% = \left( \dfrac{A_{CY} - A_{PY}}{A_{PY}} \right) \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 3 | Common Size (Balance Sheet) | $\text{Item \%} = \left( \dfrac{\text{Item}}{\text{Total Assets}} \right) \times 100$ | Percentage of Total Assets |
| 4 | Common Size (Income Statement) | $\text{Item \%} = \left( \dfrac{\text{Item}}{\text{Net Sales}} \right) \times 100$ | Percentage of Net Sales |
| 5 | Trend Analysis | $\text{Trend \%} = \left( \dfrac{A_{CY}}{A_{BY}} \right) \times 100$ | Index (Base = 100) |
| 6 | Compound Growth Rate (CAGR) | $\text{CAGR} = \left( \left( \dfrac{A_{FY}}{A_{IY}} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1 \right) \times 100$ | Annualized Growth % |

### Symbols Used

> [!NOTE]
> - $A_{CY}$ = Amount in Current Year
> - $A_{PY}$ = Amount in Previous Year
> - $A_{BY}$ = Amount in Base Year
> - $A_{FY}$ = Amount in Final Year
> - $A_{IY}$ = Amount in Initial Year
> - $n$ = Number of years between initial and final

---

## 2.5 Comparison: The Three Techniques at a Glance

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Many exam questions ask: *"Which technique would you recommend for inter-firm comparison?"* — The answer is **Common Size Statement**, because it removes the effect of size.

| Question Answered | Best Technique |
|---|---|
| "Did sales grow last year?" | Comparative Statement |
| "What % of revenue is spent on materials?" | Common Size Statement |
| "How has profit grown over 8 years?" | Trend Analysis |
| "Compare a ₹1000 Cr firm with a ₹10 Cr firm" | Common Size Statement |
| "Is long-term debt rising or falling?" | Trend Analysis (multi-year) |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Comparative Income Statement (Full KTU-Style Solution)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** From the following Income Statement of M/s Kerala Traders, prepare a Comparative Income Statement and analyze the result.

| Particulars | 2023 (₹) | 2024 (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Net Sales | 5,00,000 | 6,00,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 3,00,000 | 3,90,000 |
| Gross Profit | 2,00,000 | 2,10,000 |
| Operating Expenses | 1,00,000 | 1,20,000 |
| Net Profit | 1,00,000 | 90,000 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute Absolute Change for each item.**

$$\text{Absolute Change (Sales)} = 6{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = +1{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Absolute Change (COGS)} = 3{,}90{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = +90{,}000$$

$$\text{Absolute Change (Gross Profit)} = 2{,}10{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = +10{,}000$$

$$\text{Absolute Change (Op. Exp.)} = 1{,}20{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = +20{,}000$$

$$\text{Absolute Change (Net Profit)} = 90{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = -10{,}000$$

**Step 2: Compute Percentage Change using the formula.**

$$\text{Percentage Change} = \left( \frac{A_{CY} - A_{PY}}{A_{PY}} \right) \times 100$$

Let us compute for each item:

$$\text{Sales \%} = \left( \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = +20\%$$

$$\text{COGS \%} = \left( \frac{90{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = +30\%$$

$$\text{Gross Profit \%} = \left( \frac{10{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = +5\%$$

$$\text{Op. Exp. \%} = \left( \frac{20{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = +20\%$$

$$\text{Net Profit \%} = \left( \frac{-10{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = -10\%$$

**Step 3: Prepare the Comparative Income Statement (Final Format).**

| Particulars | 2023 (₹) | 2024 (₹) | Absolute Change (₹) | % Change |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Net Sales | 5,00,000 | 6,00,000 | +1,00,000 | +20% |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 3,00,000 | 3,90,000 | +90,000 | +30% |
| **Gross Profit** | **2,00,000** | **2,10,000** | **+10,000** | **+5%** |
| Operating Expenses | 1,00,000 | 1,20,000 | +20,000 | +20% |
| **Net Profit** | **1,00,000** | **90,000** | **-10,000** | **-10%** |

**Step 4: Interpretation (Mandatory in KTU Answers).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:**
> 1. **Sales grew by 20%** — a healthy sign of business expansion.
> 2. **However, COGS grew by 30%** — a much faster rate than sales, indicating **poor cost control** in procurement or production.
> 3. **Operating expenses grew by 20%** — in line with sales, so operational efficiency is maintained.
> 4. **Net Profit DECLINED by 10%** — a red flag. Despite higher sales, profitability has fallen due to disproportionate rise in cost of goods sold.
> 5. **Recommendation:** Management must investigate the cost structure, renegotiate supplier contracts, and improve production efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Common Size Income Statement

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Using the data from Example 1, prepare a Common Size Income Statement for both years.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the Common Base → Net Sales = 100%**

**Step 2: Compute each item as a percentage of Net Sales for 2023:**

$$\text{COGS (2023) \%} = \left( \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 60\%$$

$$\text{Gross Profit (2023) \%} = \left( \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 40\%$$

$$\text{Op. Exp. (2023) \%} = \left( \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 20\%$$

$$\text{Net Profit (2023) \%} = \left( \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 20\%$$

**Step 3: Compute each item as a percentage of Net Sales for 2024:**

$$\text{COGS (2024) \%} = \left( \frac{3{,}90{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 65\%$$

$$\text{Gross Profit (2024) \%} = \left( \frac{2{,}10{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 35\%$$

$$\text{Op. Exp. (2024) \%} = \left( \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 20\%$$

$$\text{Net Profit (2024) \%} = \left( \frac{90{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 15\%$$

**Step 4: Final Common Size Income Statement**

| Particulars | 2023 (₹) | 2023 (% of Sales) | 2024 (₹) | 2024 (% of Sales) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Net Sales | 5,00,000 | 100% | 6,00,000 | 100% |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 3,00,000 | 60% | 3,90,000 | 65% |
| **Gross Profit** | **2,00,000** | **40%** | **2,10,000** | **35%** |
| Operating Expenses | 1,00,000 | 20% | 1,20,000 | 20% |
| **Net Profit** | **1,00,000** | **20%** | **90,000** | **15%** |

**Step 5: Interpretation.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight from Common Size Analysis:**
> - The cost of goods sold has risen from **60% to 65% of sales** — confirming poor cost management.
> - The gross profit margin has **shrunk from 40% to 35%**.
> - The net profit margin has **dropped from 20% to 15%**.
> - This confirms the conclusion from the Comparative Statement — profitability is eroding despite sales growth.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Trend Analysis (Multi-Year Sales Data)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Compute the Trend Percentages of Sales and Net Profit of M/s KTU Industries from the following data. Take **2020 as the base year**.

| Year | Sales (₹ Lakhs) | Net Profit (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|
| 2020 | 100 | 10 |
| 2021 | 120 | 11 |
| 2022 | 150 | 13 |
| 2023 | 180 | 14 |
| 2024 | 200 | 12 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the Base Year (2020) and assign value = 100.**

$$\text{Sales (2020) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{100}{100} \right) \times 100 = 100$$

$$\text{Net Profit (2020) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{10}{10} \right) \times 100 = 100$$

**Step 2: Compute Trend % for each subsequent year using:**

$$\text{Trend \%} = \left( \frac{\text{Current Year Amount}}{\text{Base Year Amount}} \right) \times 100$$

**For Sales:**

$$\text{Sales (2021) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{120}{100} \right) \times 100 = 120$$

$$\text{Sales (2022) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{150}{100} \right) \times 100 = 150$$

$$\text{Sales (2023) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{180}{100} \right) \times 100 = 180$$

$$\text{Sales (2024) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{200}{100} \right) \times 100 = 200$$

**For Net Profit:**

$$\text{Net Profit (2021) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{11}{10} \right) \times 100 = 110$$

$$\text{Net Profit (2022) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{13}{10} \right) \times 100 = 130$$

$$\text{Net Profit (2023) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{14}{10} \right) \times 100 = 140$$

$$\text{Net Profit (2024) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{12}{10} \right) \times 100 = 120$$

**Step 3: Present the Trend Analysis Table.**

| Year | Sales (₹ Lakhs) | Sales Trend % | Net Profit (₹ Lakhs) | Net Profit Trend % |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2020 (Base) | 100 | 100 | 10 | 100 |
| 2021 | 120 | 120 | 11 | 110 |
| 2022 | 150 | 150 | 13 | 130 |
| 2023 | 180 | 180 | 14 | 140 |
| 2024 | 200 | 200 | 12 | 120 |

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of Trend Analysis:**
> 1. **Sales have grown consistently** — from 100 to 200 (i.e., **doubled** in 5 years) — a healthy growth trend.
> 2. **Net Profit grew from 100 to 140** by 2023, but **declined to 120 in 2024** — indicating a **divergence** between sales growth and profit growth.
> 3. In 2024, sales doubled (200 vs 100) but profit grew only 20% (120 vs 100) — a clear **warning sign**.
> 4. **Recommendation:** Investigate rising costs, pricing strategy, and operational efficiency.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) — A Trend Companion

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Sales in 2020 = ₹100 Lakhs; Sales in 2024 = ₹200 Lakhs. Calculate the CAGR.

**Step 1: Identify Initial and Final Values.**

$$A_{IY} = 100, \quad A_{FY} = 200, \quad n = 4 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2: Apply the CAGR formula.**

$$\text{CAGR} = \left( \left( \frac{A_{FY}}{A_{IY}} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1 \right) \times 100$$

**Step 3: Substitute and simplify.**

$$\text{CAGR} = \left( \left( \frac{200}{100} \right)^{\frac{1}{4}} - 1 \right) \times 100$$

$$\text{CAGR} = \left( (2)^{0.25} - 1 \right) \times 100$$

$$\text{CAGR} = (1.1892 - 1) \times 100 = 18.92\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** Sales have grown at an **average compound rate of 18.92% per year** over 4 years. This single number is more meaningful than saying "sales doubled."

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** When a question says "Prepare a Comparative Statement" — use Horizontal Analysis. When it says "Prepare a Common Size Statement" — use Vertical Analysis. When it says "Compute Trend Percentages" — use Base Year = 100.

| Situation | Recommended Technique | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Single year data available | Common Size Statement | Cannot compare over time |
| Only 2 years of data | Comparative Statement | Trend needs ≥ 3 years |
| Long-term (5+ years) data | Trend Analysis | Detects long-term patterns |
| Inter-firm comparison (different sizes) | Common Size Statement | Normalizes by size |
| Year-on-year change | Comparative Statement | Shows absolute + % change |
| Forecasting future performance | Trend Analysis + CAGR | Uses historical pattern |
| Cost structure analysis | Common Size Income Statement | Shows % of sales per expense |
| Asset composition study | Common Size Balance Sheet | Shows % of total assets |
