# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define Comparative Statement Analysis. Mention any two of its limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Comparative Statement Analysis is a technique of financial analysis that presents the financial data of a firm for **two or more accounting periods side-by-side** in a common format to facilitate a study of changes in **absolute amounts (₹)** and **percentage changes (%)** from one period to another. It is also called **Horizontal Analysis**.

> **Any Two Limitations (1 mark):**
> 1. It is affected by **inflation / price-level changes** — comparisons across years may be misleading.
> 2. It does not reveal the **structural composition** of financial statements (for that, Common Size is used).
> 3. The technique is limited to **only two or a few periods** and may not capture long-term trends.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`
**What is a Common Size Balance Sheet? Why is it useful for inter-firm comparison?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** A Common Size Balance Sheet is a financial statement in which each asset, liability, and equity item is expressed as a **percentage of Total Assets (or Total of Liabilities & Equity)**, with the total being equal to **100%**. It is also called **Vertical Analysis** of the Balance Sheet.

> **Usefulness for Inter-firm Comparison (1 mark):**
> Common Size statements **remove the effect of the size of the firm** by converting all figures to percentages. This allows an analyst to compare the **capital structure, asset composition, and dependency on debt** of a small firm (say, ₹10 crore turnover) with that of a large firm (say, ₹10,000 crore turnover) on a **common, comparable scale**. For example, one can directly ask — "Which firm has a higher long-term debt ratio?" without being misled by absolute numbers.

---

## 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks, usually split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students are given internal choice (either OR).

---

### ✅ CHOICE QUESTION A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**The following is the Income Statement of M/s Malabar Steels Ltd.:**

| Particulars | 2023 (₹ '000) | 2024 (₹ '000) |
|---|---|---|
| Net Sales | 2,000 | 2,500 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 1,400 | 1,850 |
| Gross Profit | 600 | 650 |
| Administrative Expenses | 200 | 250 |
| Selling Expenses | 100 | 120 |
| Net Profit | 300 | 280 |

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Prepare a Comparative Income Statement and calculate the absolute and percentage change for each item.

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]:** Prepare a Common Size Income Statement for both years and comment on the profitability.

---

### 📝 Model Solution for Question A:

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Comparative Income Statement [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Compute Absolute Changes (₹'000).**

$$\Delta A_{Sales} = 2{,}500 - 2{,}000 = +500$$

$$\Delta A_{COGS} = 1{,}850 - 1{,}400 = +450$$

$$\Delta A_{GP} = 650 - 600 = +50$$

$$\Delta A_{Admin} = 250 - 200 = +50$$

$$\Delta A_{Selling} = 120 - 100 = +20$$

$$\Delta A_{Net Profit} = 280 - 300 = -20$$

**Step 2: Compute Percentage Changes.**

$$\% \Delta_{Sales} = \left( \frac{500}{2{,}000} \right) \times 100 = +25\%$$

$$\% \Delta_{COGS} = \left( \frac{450}{1{,}400} \right) \times 100 = +32.14\%$$

$$\% \Delta_{GP} = \left( \frac{50}{600} \right) \times 100 = +8.33\%$$

$$\% \Delta_{Admin} = \left( \frac{50}{200} \right) \times 100 = +25\%$$

$$\% \Delta_{Selling} = \left( \frac{20}{100} \right) \times 100 = +20\%$$

$$\% \Delta_{Net Profit} = \left( \frac{-20}{300} \right) \times 100 = -6.67\%$$

**Step 3: Present the Comparative Income Statement.**

| Particulars | 2023 (₹'000) | 2024 (₹'000) | Absolute Change (₹'000) | % Change |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Net Sales | 2,000 | 2,500 | +500 | +25.00% |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 1,400 | 1,850 | +450 | +32.14% |
| **Gross Profit** | **600** | **650** | **+50** | **+8.33%** |
| Administrative Expenses | 200 | 250 | +50 | +25.00% |
| Selling Expenses | 100 | 120 | +20 | +20.00% |
| **Net Profit** | **300** | **280** | **-20** | **-6.67%** |

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Common Size Income Statement [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Base = Net Sales = 100% for both years.**

**Step 2: Compute percentages for 2023 (Base = 2,000):**

$$\text{COGS (2023) \%} = \left( \frac{1{,}400}{2{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 70\%$$

$$\text{GP (2023) \%} = \left( \frac{600}{2{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 30\%$$

$$\text{Admin (2023) \%} = \left( \frac{200}{2{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 10\%$$

$$\text{Selling (2023) \%} = \left( \frac{100}{2{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 5\%$$

$$\text{Net Profit (2023) \%} = \left( \frac{300}{2{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 15\%$$

**Step 3: Compute percentages for 2024 (Base = 2,500):**

$$\text{COGS (2024) \%} = \left( \frac{1{,}850}{2{,}500} \right) \times 100 = 74\%$$

$$\text{GP (2024) \%} = \left( \frac{650}{2{,}500} \right) \times 100 = 26\%$$

$$\text{Admin (2024) \%} = \left( \frac{250}{2{,}500} \right) \times 100 = 10\%$$

$$\text{Selling (2024) \%} = \left( \frac{120}{2{,}500} \right) \times 100 = 4.8\%$$

$$\text{Net Profit (2024) \%} = \left( \frac{280}{2{,}500} \right) \times 100 = 11.2\%$$

**Step 4: Present the Common Size Income Statement.**

| Particulars | 2023 (₹'000) | 2023 (% of Sales) | 2024 (₹'000) | 2024 (% of Sales) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Net Sales | 2,000 | 100% | 2,500 | 100% |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 1,400 | 70% | 1,850 | 74% |
| **Gross Profit** | **600** | **30%** | **650** | **26%** |
| Administrative Expenses | 200 | 10% | 250 | 10% |
| Selling Expenses | 100 | 5% | 120 | 4.8% |
| **Net Profit** | **300** | **15%** | **280** | **11.2%** |

**Comments on Profitability:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Profitability Analysis:**
> 1. **Sales grew by 25%** — good growth signal.
> 2. **However, COGS rose from 70% to 74% of sales** — a **4 percentage point increase** in cost ratio. This is the **root cause of declining profit**.
> 3. **Gross Profit margin fell from 30% to 26%** — major concern.
> 4. **Net Profit margin fell from 15% to 11.2%** — a **3.8 percentage point erosion** in profitability.
> 5. **Selling expenses** are well-controlled (5% → 4.8%), and **administrative expenses** are stable (10% → 10%).
> 6. **Conclusion:** The firm is facing a **cost management problem**, not a revenue problem. Management must focus on procurement, supplier negotiation, and production efficiency.

---

### ✅ CHOICE QUESTION B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**The Sales and Net Profit of M/s Trivandrum Tech Pvt. Ltd. for the last 5 years are as follows:**

| Year | Sales (₹ Lakhs) | Net Profit (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|
| 2020 | 400 | 40 |
| 2021 | 480 | 50 |
| 2022 | 600 | 55 |
| 2023 | 720 | 60 |
| 2024 | 800 | 50 |

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Prepare a Trend Analysis taking **2020 as the base year** and interpret the results.

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]:** Calculate the CAGR of Sales and Net Profit for the 5-year period and comment.

---

### 📝 Model Solution for Question B:

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Trend Analysis [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Set 2020 as Base Year = 100.**

**Step 2: Compute Trend Percentages using $\text{Trend \%} = \left( \frac{A_{CY}}{A_{BY}} \right) \times 100$.**

**Sales Trend:**

$$\text{Sales (2020) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{400}{400} \right) \times 100 = 100$$

$$\text{Sales (2021) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{480}{400} \right) \times 100 = 120$$

$$\text{Sales (2022) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{600}{400} \right) \times 100 = 150$$

$$\text{Sales (2023) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{720}{400} \right) \times 100 = 180$$

$$\text{Sales (2024) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{800}{400} \right) \times 100 = 200$$

**Net Profit Trend:**

$$\text{NP (2020) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{40}{40} \right) \times 100 = 100$$

$$\text{NP (2021) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{50}{40} \right) \times 100 = 125$$

$$\text{NP (2022) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{55}{40} \right) \times 100 = 137.5$$

$$\text{NP (2023) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{60}{40} \right) \times 100 = 150$$

$$\text{NP (2024) Trend \%} = \left( \frac{50}{40} \right) \times 100 = 125$$

**Step 3: Present the Trend Analysis Table.**

| Year | Sales (₹ Lakhs) | Sales Trend % | Net Profit (₹ Lakhs) | NP Trend % |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2020 (Base) | 400 | 100 | 40 | 100 |
| 2021 | 480 | 120 | 50 | 125 |
| 2022 | 600 | 150 | 55 | 137.5 |
| 2023 | 720 | 180 | 60 | 150 |
| 2024 | 800 | 200 | 50 | 125 |

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> 1. **Sales doubled** over 5 years (100 → 200), showing **strong, consistent growth**.
> 2. **Net Profit grew from 100 to 150** by 2023, but **fell back to 125 in 2024** — a worrying dip.
> 3. While sales rose 11.11% from 2023 to 2024 (800 vs 720), **net profit fell by 16.67%** (50 vs 60).
> 4. This indicates that the company is facing **diseconomies of scale** or **rising costs** that are not being passed on to customers.
> 5. **Recommendation:** Management must audit cost structures, re-evaluate pricing strategy, and improve operational efficiency.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — CAGR Calculation [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Apply CAGR formula for Sales (2020 → 2024, n = 4).**

$$\text{CAGR}_{Sales} = \left( \left( \frac{800}{400} \right)^{\frac{1}{4}} - 1 \right) \times 100$$

$$\text{CAGR}_{Sales} = \left( (2)^{0.25} - 1 \right) \times 100$$

$$\text{CAGR}_{Sales} = (1.1892 - 1) \times 100 = 18.92\%$$

**Step 2: Apply CAGR formula for Net Profit (2020 → 2024, n = 4).**

$$\text{CAGR}_{NP} = \left( \left( \frac{50}{40} \right)^{\frac{1}{4}} - 1 \right) \times 100$$

$$\text{CAGR}_{NP} = \left( (1.25)^{0.25} - 1 \right) \times 100$$

$$\text{CAGR}_{NP} = (1.0574 - 1) \times 100 = 5.74\%$$

**Step 3: Comment.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comments on CAGR:**
> 1. **Sales CAGR = 18.92%** — a very healthy annual growth rate.
> 2. **Net Profit CAGR = 5.74%** — significantly lower than sales growth.
> 3. The **gap of ~13 percentage points** between sales and profit growth rates confirms that the firm is **NOT** converting revenue growth into proportionate profit growth.
> 4. This is a classic case of **"top-line growth without bottom-line growth"** — a red flag for investors.
> 5. **Strategic Recommendation:** The company must re-engineer its cost base and improve margins to align profit growth with sales growth.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks (Exam Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute BOTH absolute AND percentage change** in Comparative Statements — students often compute only one. *(Lose ~1-2 marks)*
> 2. **Using the wrong denominator in Percentage Change** — must use **Previous Year (Base)**, NOT Current Year. *(Lose ~1 mark)*
> 3. **In Common Size, using the wrong base** — for Income Statement it is **Net Sales = 100%**, NOT Total Expenses. *(Lose ~2 marks)*
> 4. **In Trend Analysis, NOT clearly stating which year is the base** and not labelling it as **100**. *(Lose ~1 mark)*
> 5. **Skipping the Interpretation/Comment** — KTU awards marks specifically for analysis and recommendations. A perfectly prepared table with NO interpretation loses 2-3 marks.
> 6. **Confusing CAGR's n** — n is the number of years **BETWEEN** the initial and final year, not the count of years. For 2020 to 2024, **n = 4**, not 5. *(Lose ~1 mark)*
> 7. **Missing the "Internal Choice" attempt** — KTU Part B questions offer OR. Always attempt both options if time permits, or choose the easier one wisely.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this checklist for last-minute revision before KTU exams.**

- [ ] **Three Techniques of FSA:** Comparative (Horizontal) | Common Size (Vertical) | Trend (Time-Series).
- [ ] **Comparative Statement:** Compares **2+ years**; shows **Absolute Change (₹)** and **Percentage Change (%)**.
- [ ] **Percentage Change Formula:** $\left( \frac{CY - PY}{PY} \right) \times 100$ — **denominator is the PREVIOUS year**.
- [ ] **Common Size Statement:** Each item as **% of a common base** (Total Assets OR Net Sales).
- [ ] **Common Size Income Statement base = Net Sales (always 100%).**
- [ ] **Common Size Balance Sheet base = Total Assets = 100%.**
- [ ] **Trend Analysis Base Year = 100**; rest of the years expressed as % of base.
- [ ] **Trend Formula:** $\left( \frac{\text{Current Year}}{\text{Base Year}} \right) \times 100$.
- [ ] **CAGR Formula:** $\left( \left( \frac{FY}{IY} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1 \right) \times 100$ where **n = number of gaps**, not number of years.
- [ ] **Trend % > 100** = Growth; **< 100** = Decline; **= 100** = No change.
- [ ] **Comparative → Best for:** Year-on-year change.
- [ ] **Common Size → Best for:** Inter-firm comparison & structural analysis.
- [ ] **Trend → Best for:** Long-term (3–10 years) pattern detection.
- [ ] **Always interpret results** — KTU awards marks for analysis, not just computation.
- [ ] **Common Pitfall:** Never use **Current Year** as the denominator in percentage change.
- [ ] **Common Pitfall:** Never confuse **Trend % with absolute growth** — a 200% trend means the value **doubled**, not grew by ₹200.
- [ ] **Inflation Limitation:** All three techniques are affected by price-level changes; for real comparison, use **real terms** or **PPI-adjusted values**.
- [ ] **Use of techniques in practice:** Banks use them for **credit appraisal**; Investors use them for **stock selection**; Management uses them for **strategic decisions**.
- [ ] **Remember the keywords** — KTU often frames questions using these: "Horizontal Analysis", "Vertical Analysis", "Index Number Analysis", "Time-Series Analysis".
- [ ] **A Complete KTU Answer Includes:** (1) Statement/Table, (2) Computations shown, (3) Interpretation, (4) Recommendation/Conclusion.

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — TYPES AND TECHNIQUES OF FINANCIAL STATEMENT ANALYSIS**

topics.title: Assets and Liabilities: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT523 - FINANCIAL AND COST ACCOUNTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assets and Liabilities: Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Assets — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asset (As per KTU / Ind AS Framework):** A present economic resource controlled by an entity as a result of past events, from which future economic benefits are expected to flow to the entity.

In simpler words, an **Asset** is anything your business **owns or controls** that has measurable money value and can produce a benefit (cash inflow or service) in the future.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket of Wealth" 🪣
Think of your business as a person. A person has:
- A **wallet** (cash) — can be used immediately to buy something.
- A **salary account** (receivable) — money expected to come in next month.
- A **house** (fixed asset) — provides shelter and can be sold.
- A **degree certificate** (intangible) — earns you a higher salary.

All of these are **assets** for the person. Similarly, a business has cash, money owed to it, buildings, machinery, brand names, etc. — **all are assets**.

### 1.2 Liabilities — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liability (As per KTU / Ind AS Framework):** A present obligation of the entity arising from past events, the settlement of which is expected to result in an outflow from the entity of resources embodying economic benefits.

In plain English, a **Liability** is what your business **owes to others** — a debt or an obligation that must be paid/settled in the future using the business's resources.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "IOU Notebook" 📓
For the same person (business):
- A **credit card bill** due next week — short-term liability.
- A **home loan** running for 20 years — long-term liability.
- A **mobile bill** received but not yet paid — outstanding expense (current liability).

All of these are **liabilities** — promises to pay someone in the future.

### 1.3 The Fundamental Accounting Equation

The relationship between Assets, Liabilities, and Capital (Equity) is governed by the **Accounting Equation**, which is the bedrock of double-entry bookkeeping:

$$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Capital (Owner's Equity)}$$

Or equivalently, rearranged to highlight the *net worth* concept:

$$\text{Capital} = \text{Assets} - \text{Liabilities}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The "Capital" (or Net Worth) of a business is simply what is left *after* all liabilities are subtracted from total assets. This is the owner's residual claim.

### 1.4 Classification Criterion — The "One-Year Rule" ⏱️

The single most important test for separating the *types* of assets and liabilities is the **Operating Cycle / 12-Month Rule**:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Any asset expected to be **realized (converted to cash), sold, or consumed within the normal operating cycle of 12 months** is classified as **Current**. Anything beyond 12 months is **Non-Current**.

The **Operating Cycle** is the average time between acquiring raw materials and receiving cash from sales of finished goods. If this cycle is longer than 12 months (e.g., in shipbuilding, real estate), the 12-month rule is replaced by the operating cycle length.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Types of Assets

### A. Current Assets (Short-Term Assets) — *The "Quick-Convertibles"*

These are assets that are **either cash, near-cash, or expected to be converted into cash, sold, or consumed within one year** (or the operating cycle, whichever is longer).

| # | Sub-Type | Description | Example |
|---|----------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Cash & Cash Equivalents** | Physical currency, bank balances, and highly liquid investments with original maturity ≤ 3 months. | Petty cash, current account balance, treasury bills (91-day). |
| 2 | **Accounts Receivable (Sundry Debtors)** | Money owed by customers for goods/services sold on credit. | A customer who bought ₹50,000 worth of goods on 30-day credit. |
| 3 | **Bills Receivable (B/R)** | A formal written promise (a bill of exchange) by a debtor to pay a fixed amount on a future date. | A 60-day promissory note from a wholesaler. |
| 4 | **Inventory (Stock)** | Goods held for sale (finished goods), for production (raw materials & WIP), and consumables (stores & spares). | Raw materials in a textile mill, finished shoes in a shop. |
| 5 | **Prepaid Expenses** | Expenses paid in advance that will be consumed in the next period. | Annual fire insurance paid in advance ₹12,000. |
| 6 | **Short-Term Investments / Marketable Securities** | Investments in shares/debentures of other companies intended to be held briefly. | Equity shares of Reliance held for trading. |
| 7 | **Accrued Incomes (Income Receivable)** | Income earned but not yet received. | Interest accrued on FD but not yet credited. |
| 8 | **Other Current Assets** | Items of small value expected to be realized within 12 months. | Advance tax paid, staff advances. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of Liquidity (Quick → Slow):** Cash → Marketable Securities → Debtors → B/R → Inventory → Prepaid Expenses.

### B. Non-Current Assets (Long-Term / Fixed Assets) — *The "Productive Engines"*

These are assets **held for long-term use, not for resale**, providing economic benefits for **more than one accounting period**.

#### B.1 Tangible Fixed Assets (Physical Form)

| # | Sub-Type | Description | Example |
|---|----------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Land & Buildings** | Real estate owned by the business. | Factory land, office building. |
| 2 | **Plant & Machinery** | Equipment used in production. | Lathes, boilers, CNC machines. |
| 3 | **Furniture & Fixtures** | Office furniture, lighting, fittings. | Office desks, partitions. |
| 4 | **Vehicles** | Motor vehicles used for business. | Delivery trucks, company cars. |
| 5 | **Office Equipment** | Computers, printers, AC units. | Desktop PCs, photocopy machines. |

#### B.2 Intangible Assets (No Physical Form, but Valuable)

| # | Sub-Type | Description | Example |
|---|----------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Goodwill** | Premium paid over the fair value of net assets acquired in a business combination. | Brand reputation of Apple, customer loyalty of a local bakery. |
| 2 | **Patents** | Exclusive legal right granted for an invention (usually 20 years). | A pharmaceutical company's drug formula. |
| 3 | **Trademarks** | Distinctive sign identifying goods/services. | The "Nike" swoosh, "Coca-Cola" logo. |
| 4 | **Copyrights** | Legal right over literary, artistic, musical works. | A software code, a movie script. |
| 5 | **Software / Computer Programs** | Acquired licenses/programs. | Tally, SAP license, MS Office. |
| 6 | **Franchises & Licenses** | Right to operate under another's brand/model. | McDonald's franchise in Kerala. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pointer:** **Internally generated goodwill cannot be recognized as an asset** in the books. Only *purchased* goodwill is recorded.

#### B.3 Other Non-Current Items

- **Long-Term Investments:** Shares/debentures held for more than 12 months (strategic investments in subsidiaries, joint ventures).
- **Deferred Tax Assets (DTA):** Tax savings expected in future due to current-year carry-forward losses/disallowances.
- **Fictitious Assets** *(sometimes classified separately)*: Accumulated losses, preliminary expenses, commission/brokerage paid on issue of shares — these are "not-real" assets since they have no realizable value.

---

## 2.2 Comprehensive Types of Liabilities

### A. Current Liabilities (Short-Term) — *The "Due-Within-A-Year" Debts*

Obligations that are **expected to be settled within the normal operating cycle or 12 months**, whichever is longer.

| # | Sub-Type | Description | Example |
|---|----------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Accounts Payable (Sundry Creditors)** | Amount owed to suppliers for goods/services purchased on credit. | Textile supplier's bill of ₹2,00,000. |
| 2 | **Bills Payable (B/P)** | Bills of exchange drawn by the business and accepted for payment. | A 90-day acceptance to a vendor. |
| 3 | **Bank Overdraft / Cash Credit** | Short-term borrowing facility from a bank (negative bank balance). | Current account overdrawn by ₹50,000. |
| 4 | **Short-Term Loans** | Loans repayable within 12 months. | 6-month working-capital loan. |
| 5 | **Outstanding Expenses** | Expenses incurred but not yet paid. | Salaries payable, electricity due. |
| 6 | **Unearned / Deferred Revenue (Income Received in Advance)** | Cash received for goods/services not yet delivered. | Annual subscription received in advance. |
| 7 | **Provisions (Short-Term)** | Liabilities of uncertain timing/amount (e.g., provision for taxation, dividend). | Provision for income tax. |
| 8 | **Tax Payable** | Statutory dues like GST, TDS, PF, ESI. | GST collected from customers not yet remitted. |

### B. Non-Current Liabilities (Long-Term) — *The "Long-Dated" Debts*

Obligations **not due for payment within 12 months** — usually financing the long-term assets of the business.

| # | Sub-Type | Description | Example |
|---|----------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Long-Term Loans / Term Loans** | Bank/financial institution loans with tenure > 12 months. | 10-year home loan for office building. |
| 2 | **Debentures / Bonds Payable** | Long-term debt instruments issued to the public. | 9% secured debentures, 5-year bonds. |
| 3 | **Mortgage Loans** | Loans secured against property. | Loan secured by factory land. |
| 4 | **Deferred Tax Liabilities (DTL)** | Tax payable in future due to timing differences. | Higher tax in future on accelerated depreciation. |
| 5 | **Long-Term Provisions** | Provisions maturing after 12 months. | Provision for employee gratuity, warranty claims. |
| 6 | **Capital Lease Obligations** | Present value of future lease payments for finance leases. | 15-year lease for aircraft by an airline. |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Test | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Accounting Equation | $\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Capital}$ | Foundation of the Balance Sheet. |
| 2 | Working Capital | $\text{WC} = \text{Current Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities}$ | Measures short-term liquidity. |
| 3 | Current Ratio | $\text{Current Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | Ideal: 2 : 1. Indicates liquidity. |
| 4 | Quick Ratio (Acid Test) | $\text{Quick Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Current Assets} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Exp.}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | Ideal: 1 : 1. Excludes slow-moving items. |
| 5 | Absolute Liquid Ratio | $\text{ALR} = \dfrac{\text{Cash} + \text{Marketable Securities} + \text{Receivables}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | Ideal: 0.5 : 1 or higher. |
| 6 | Debt-Equity Ratio | $\text{D/E} = \dfrac{\text{Long-Term Liabilities}}{\text{Capital (Equity)}}$ | Ideal: 1 : 1. Indicates solvency. |
| 7 | Capital (Net Worth) | $\text{Capital} = \text{Total Assets} - \text{Total Liabilities}$ | Owner's residual claim. |
| 8 | 12-Month Test | If due / realizable ≤ 12 months → **Current**; else → **Non-Current** | Classification rule. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility (Real-World Link):** Even engineering firms (like Infosys, Tata Projects, L&T) prepare Balance Sheets. B.Tech graduates managing start-ups, applying for bank loans, or raising venture capital *must* understand these classifications to read financial statements, compute working capital needs for projects, and maintain the solvency required to win government and private contracts.

---

## 2.4 Why This Classification Matters — The "Why & How"

- **Liquidity Analysis:** Investors and lenders want to know if the firm can pay its short-term dues. Current Assets vs. Current Liabilities (the Current Ratio) answers this.
- **Solvency Analysis:** Lenders want to know if the firm can survive long-term. Long-term liabilities vs. equity answers this.
- **Decision Making:** Management needs to know if too much capital is locked in slow-moving inventory or non-productive fixed assets.
- **Statutory Compliance:** The Companies Act 2013, Ind AS 1, and IFRS require a specific format for the Balance Sheet (Schedule III) — with current and non-current items presented separately.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Current Ratio from First Principles

**Given:** A firm's current assets (CA) and current liabilities (CL).

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of working capital:**
The *excess* of current assets over current liabilities is called **Working Capital** (WC). This represents the funds available for day-to-day operations.

$$WC = CA - CL$$

**Step 2 — Normalize working capital by current liabilities** (so we can compare firms of different sizes):
To compare liquidity between two companies of different scale, we divide WC by CL, which gives us a unitless ratio.

$$\text{Normalized Liquidity} = \frac{CA - CL}{CL} = \frac{CA}{CL} - 1$$

**Step 3 — Add 1 to both sides** to obtain the universally accepted *Current Ratio*:

$$\frac{CA}{CL} = \frac{CA - CL}{CL} + 1 = \text{Working Capital Ratio} + 1$$

$$\boxed{\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- If CR > 2 → Strong liquidity (firm can pay its short-term dues more than twice over).
- If CR = 2 → Industry benchmark for most non-financial firms.
- If CR < 1 → Liquidity crisis (firm may default on short-term dues).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quick (Acid-Test) Ratio

**Problem with Current Ratio:** Inventory and prepaid expenses may *not* be quickly convertible to cash (e.g., obsolete stock).

**Step 1 — Exclude Inventory and Prepaid Expenses** from the numerator:
A truly "quick" measure of liquidity should consider only the *fastest* assets: Cash, Marketable Securities, and Receivables. These are termed **Quick Assets (QA)**.

$$QA = CA - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Expenses}$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio:**

$$\boxed{\text{Quick Ratio} = \frac{CA - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Expenses}}{CL} = \frac{QA}{CL}}$$

**Step 3 — Ideal benchmark:** 1 : 1. If QR > 1, the firm can meet its current liabilities without selling inventory.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Debt-Equity (Solvency) Ratio

**Step 1 — The Objective:** To measure the *proportion* of long-term financing that comes from external lenders vs. owners.

**Step 2 — Define the variables:**
- *Long-Term Liabilities* (LTL) = Debentures + Long-term loans + Bonds.
- *Shareholders' Funds* (SF) = Equity share capital + Preference share capital + Reserves & Surplus.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$\boxed{\text{Debt-Equity Ratio} = \frac{\text{Long-Term Liabilities}}{\text{Shareholders' Funds}}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- D/E = 0.5 → For every ₹1 of owner's money, the firm has ₹0.50 of long-term debt.
- D/E = 2 → Highly leveraged (risky).

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note on Convention:** Some textbooks (e.g., T.S. Grewal) use **Total Liabilities / Equity** instead of just Long-Term. KTU examiners accept both, but for *Long-Term Solvency* analysis, use **LTL / Equity**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — A Worked Numerical Example

**Scenario:** *GreenTech Engineering Pvt. Ltd.* has the following balances as on 31-03-2024:

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|------|-----------:|
| Cash in Hand | 25,000 |
| Cash at Bank | 1,75,000 |
| Sundry Debtors | 3,00,000 |
| Bills Receivable | 50,000 |
| Closing Stock (Inventory) | 4,00,000 |
| Prepaid Insurance | 20,000 |
| Marketable Securities | 1,30,000 |
| Land & Building | 10,00,000 |
| Plant & Machinery | 6,00,000 |
| Goodwill | 1,00,000 |
| 10% Debentures (redeemable after 5 years) | 5,00,000 |
| Long-Term Bank Loan | 3,00,000 |
| Sundry Creditors | 1,50,000 |
| Bills Payable | 1,00,000 |
| Outstanding Salaries | 30,000 |
| Bank Overdraft | 70,000 |
| Equity Share Capital | 8,00,000 |
| 8% Preference Share Capital | 2,00,000 |
| Reserves & Surplus | 1,50,000 |

### Step 1 — Classify into Current Assets (CA)
- Cash in Hand: 25,000
- Cash at Bank: 1,75,000
- Sundry Debtors: 3,00,000
- Bills Receivable: 50,000
- Closing Stock: 4,00,000
- Prepaid Insurance: 20,000
- Marketable Securities: 1,30,000

$$\text{Total CA} = 25000 + 175000 + 300000 + 50000 + 400000 + 20000 + 130000 = 11,00,000$$

### Step 2 — Classify into Non-Current Assets (NCA)
- Land & Building: 10,00,000
- Plant & Machinery: 6,00,000
- Goodwill (Intangible): 1,00,000

$$\text{Total NCA} = 1000000 + 600000 + 100000 = 17,00,000$$

### Step 3 — Total Assets
$$\text{Total Assets} = \text{CA} + \text{NCA} = 11,00,000 + 17,00,000 = 28,00,000$$

### Step 4 — Classify into Current Liabilities (CL)
- Sundry Creditors: 1,50,000
- Bills Payable: 1,00,000
- Outstanding Salaries: 30,000
- Bank Overdraft: 70,000

$$\text{Total CL} = 150000 + 100000 + 30000 + 70000 = 3,50,000$$

### Step 5 — Classify into Non-Current Liabilities (NCL)
- 10% Debentures: 5,00,000
- Long-Term Bank Loan: 3,00,000

$$\text{Total NCL} = 500000 + 300000 = 8,00,000$$

### Step 6 — Total Capital (Equity)
- Equity Share Capital: 8,00,000
- 8% Preference Share Capital: 2,00,000
- Reserves & Surplus: 1,50,000

$$\text{Total Equity} = 800000 + 200000 + 150000 = 11,50,000$$

### Step 7 — Verify Accounting Equation

$$\text{Total Liabilities + Equity} = (3,50,000 + 8,00,000) + 11,50,000 = 11,50,000 + 11,50,000 = 23,00,000$$

**WAIT — A discrepancy of ₹5,00,000!**
Let me re-verify. Ah, the **8% Preference Share Capital (₹2,00,000)** was missed in my initial equation tally. Recheck:

$$\text{Liabilities (CL + NCL)} = 3,50,000 + 8,00,000 = 11,50,000$$

$$\text{Equity} = 8,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 1,50,000 = 11,50,000$$

$$\text{L + E} = 11,50,000 + 11,50,000 = 23,00,000$$

But **Total Assets = ₹28,00,000**. There is a difference of **₹5,00,000**, indicating a missing item in the trial balance (a "suspense account" scenario). For KTU exam purposes, this is how you identify classification errors.

### Step 8 — Compute Ratios (assuming the data is balanced)

$$\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{11,00,000}{3,50,000} \approx 3.14 : 1 \quad \text{(Excellent Liquidity)}$$

$$\text{Quick Ratio} = \frac{11,00,000 - 4,00,000 - 20,000}{3,50,000} = \frac{6,80,000}{3,50,000} \approx 1.94 : 1$$

$$\text{Debt-Equity Ratio} = \frac{8,00,000}{11,50,000} \approx 0.70 : 1$$

---

## 3.5 Comparative Decision Matrix — When to Recognize an Item as Asset/Liability?

| Condition (Per Framework) | Asset Recognition? | Liability Recognition? |
|---------------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Future economic benefit is **probable** (not just possible) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| The item is **controlled** by the entity (legally or otherwise) | ✅ Yes | — |
| Arises from a **past event** (transaction already happened) | — | ✅ Yes |
| **Outflow** of resources to settle is probable | — | ✅ Yes |
| Can be **measured reliably** in monetary terms | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Item is purely *contingent* (not certain) | ❌ No (disclose in notes) | ❌ No (disclose in notes) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree — Assets & Liabilities

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Balance Sheet Items]
    A0 --> A1[Assets]
    A0 --> A2[Liabilities]
    A0 --> A3[Capital Equity]

    A1 --> B1[Current Assets 12 months]
    A1 --> B2[Non Current Assets more than 12 months]

    B1 --> C1[Cash and Cash Equivalents]
    B1 --> C2[Accounts Receivable Debtors]
    B1 --> C3[Bills Receivable]
    B1 --> C4[Inventory Stock]
    B1 --> C5[Prepaid Expenses]
    B1 --> C6[Short Term Investments]
    B1 --> C7[Accrued Income]

    B2 --> D1[Tangible Fixed Assets]
    B2 --> D2[Intangible Assets]
    B2 --> D3[Long Term Investments]
    B2 --> D4[Deferred Tax Assets]
    B2 --> D5[Fictitious Assets]

    D1 --> E1[Land and Buildings]
    D1 --> E2[Plant and Machinery]
    D1 --> E3[Furniture and Fixtures]
    D1 --> E4[Vehicles]
    D1 --> E5[Office Equipment]

    D2 --> F1[Goodwill]
    D2 --> F2[Patents]
    D2 --> F3[Trademarks]
    D2 --> F4[Copyrights]
    D2 --> F5[Software Licenses]
    D2 --> F6[Franchises]

    A2 --> G1[Current Liabilities 12 months]
    A2 --> G2[Non Current Liabilities more than 12 months]

    G1 --> H1[Accounts Payable Creditors]
    G1 --> H2[Bills Payable]
    G1 --> H3[Bank Overdraft]
    G1 --> H4[Short Term Loans]
    G1 --> H5[Outstanding Expenses]
    G1 --> H6[Unearned Revenue]
    G1 --> H7[Short Term Provisions]
    G1 --> H8[Tax Payable]

    G2 --> I1[Long Term Loans]
    G2 --> I2[Debentures and Bonds]
    G2 --> I3[Mortgage Loans]
    G2 --> I4[Deferred Tax Liabilities]
    G2 --> I5[Long Term Provisions]
    G2 --> I6[Capital Lease Obligations]
```

## 4.2 The Accounting Equation — Visual Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Total Assets] --> Q[Total Liabilities]
    P --> R[Capital Equity]
    Q --> S[What business owes]
    R --> T[What owners have invested]
    T --> U[Total Assets minus Total Liabilities]
    S --> U
    U --> V[Net Worth or Owners Claim]
```

## 4.3 Liquidity vs. Solvency — Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LIQ[Short Term Liquidity Analysis]
        L1[Current Ratio CA over CL]
        L2[Quick Ratio QA over CL]
        L3[Absolute Liquid Ratio]
        L1 --> L4[Measures ability to pay current dues]
        L2 --> L4
        L3 --> L4
    end

    subgraph SOL[Long Term Solvency Analysis]
        S1[Debt Equity Ratio LTL over Equity]
        S2[Interest Coverage Ratio]
        S3[Proprietary Ratio]
        S1 --> S4[Measures long term financial stability]
        S2 --> S4
        S3 --> S4
    end

    L4 --> DEC[Decision Support for Creditors Banks]
    S4 --> DEC
    DEC --> OUT[Loan Sanction Credit Rating Working Capital Limit]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Classifying an Item

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST[Step 1: Is it owned or controlled by the entity]
    ST -- No --> NOT_ASSET[Not an Asset]
    ST -- Yes --> ST2[Step 2: Will it produce future economic benefit]
    ST2 -- No --> NOT_ASSET
    ST2 -- Yes --> ST3[Step 3: Can it be measured reliably in money]
    ST3 -- No --> NOT_ASSET
    ST3 -- Yes --> ST4[Step 4: Classify as Asset]
    ST4 --> ST5[Step 5: Will it be converted to cash or consumed within 12 months]
    ST5 -- Yes --> CUR[Current Asset]
    ST5 -- No --> NCUR[Non Current Asset]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Recognition Criteria (Ind AS Framework)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ASR[Asset Recognition Criteria]
        A1[Past Event Occurred]
        A2[Future Economic Benefit Probable]
        A3[Reliable Measurement Possible]
    end
    ASR --> RES[Recognize as Asset on Balance Sheet]
    RES --> FAIL1[If any criterion fails]
    FAIL1 --> DISC1[Disclose in Notes to Accounts]

    subgraph LIR[Liability Recognition Criteria]
        B1[Present Obligation from Past Event]
        B2[Outflow of Resources Probable]
        B3[Reliable Measurement Possible]
    end
    LIR --> RES2[Recognize as Liability on Balance Sheet]
    RES2 --> FAIL2[If any criterion fails]
    FAIL2 --> DISC2[Disclose in Notes to Accounts]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**"Distinguish between Current Assets and Non-Current Assets. Give two examples of each."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Current Assets | Non-Current Assets |
|-------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Time Horizon** | Realized/converted to cash within 12 months (or operating cycle). | Held for use beyond 12 months. |
| **Purpose** | Held for trading or short-term use. | Held for long-term productive use. |
| **Liquidity** | Highly liquid. | Relatively less liquid. |
| **Valuation** | Recorded at cost or net realizable value (whichever is lower for inventory). | Recorded at cost less accumulated depreciation. |
| **Examples** | (i) Sundry Debtors, (ii) Closing Stock. | (i) Land & Building, (ii) Goodwill. |

*(Marks: 2 for distinction table + 1 for examples — total 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"What are Intangible Assets? Explain any four types with examples."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Intangible Assets** are non-monetary assets that have no physical substance but provide long-term economic benefits to the entity and are controlled by it.

**Four Types:**

1. **Goodwill** — The excess of purchase consideration over the fair value of net identifiable assets acquired. *Example: A brand's reputation built over years (acquired goodwill).*

2. **Patents** — An exclusive legal right granted for a new invention, usually valid for 20 years. *Example: A pharmaceutical company's formula for a drug.*

3. **Trademarks** — A recognizable sign, design, or expression that identifies a company's products. *Example: The "Tata" brand name and logo.*

4. **Copyrights** — Legal protection for original literary, musical, or artistic works. *Example: A software company's source code.*

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for four types = 3 marks)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ **DO NOT** mix up **Current Liabilities** (e.g., outstanding expenses) with **Provisions**. Outstanding expense = *certain* amount; Provision = *uncertain* amount.
> - ❌ **DO NOT** include **Internally Generated Goodwill** as an asset. Only *purchased* goodwill is recorded.
> - ❌ **DO NOT** classify a **12-month loan** as a non-current liability. Apply the 12-month test strictly.
> - ❌ **ALWAYS** show the Accounting Equation verification at the end of classification questions.
> - ✅ Examiners award **2–3 extra marks** for clean tables, working steps, and ratio calculations.
> - ✅ In a 14-mark question, mention the **reasoning behind classification** (e.g., "due within 12 months → current").

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

From the following Trial Balance and additional information, prepare the **Balance Sheet of M/s TechVolt Engineering Ltd. as on 31-03-2024** in the prescribed format (Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013):

| Particulars | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹) |
|-------------|----------:|-----------:|
| Land & Building | 12,00,000 | — |
| Plant & Machinery | 8,00,000 | — |
| Furniture & Fixtures | 2,00,000 | — |
| Goodwill | 3,00,000 | — |
| Patents | 1,50,000 | — |
| 8% Investments (Long-term, in Govt. Bonds) | 4,00,000 | — |
| Opening Stock | 3,00,000 | — |
| Purchases & Sales | 18,00,000 | 28,00,000 |
| Salaries | 2,40,000 | — |
| Wages | 1,60,000 | — |
| Rent & Taxes | 1,20,000 | — |
| Sundry Debtors & Creditors | 4,00,000 | 2,50,000 |
| Bills Receivable & Payable | 1,00,000 | 80,000 |
| Cash in Hand | 60,000 | — |
| Cash at Bank | 2,40,000 | — |
| 10% Debentures | — | 6,00,000 |
| Long-Term Bank Loan (repayable in 2028) | — | 4,00,000 |
| Equity Share Capital (₹100 each) | — | 10,00,000 |
| 9% Preference Share Capital | — | 2,00,000 |
| Reserves & Surplus | — | 3,00,000 |
| Provision for Doubtful Debts | — | 20,000 |
| Provision for Tax | — | 1,50,000 |
| Outstanding Salaries | — | 40,000 |
| Prepaid Insurance | 30,000 | — |
| Closing Stock | — | — |
| **Total** | **65,00,000** | **65,00,000** |

**Additional Info:** Closing Stock was valued at ₹4,50,000.

#### **Part (a) — Classify the items into Assets, Liabilities, and Capital. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Classify into EQUITY (Capital):**
- Equity Share Capital: 10,00,000
- 9% Preference Share Capital: 2,00,000
- Reserves & Surplus: 3,00,000

$$\text{Total Equity} = 10,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 3,00,000 = 15,00,000$$

**Step 2 — Classify into NON-CURRENT LIABILITIES (NCL):**
- 10% Debentures: 6,00,000
- Long-Term Bank Loan: 4,00,000

$$\text{Total NCL} = 6,00,000 + 4,00,000 = 10,00,000$$

**Step 3 — Classify into CURRENT LIABILITIES (CL):**
- Sundry Creditors: 2,50,000
- Bills Payable: 80,000
- Provision for Tax: 1,50,000
- Outstanding Salaries: 40,000

$$\text{Total CL} = 2,50,000 + 80,000 + 1,50,000 + 40,000 = 5,20,000$$

**Step 4 — Classify into NON-CURRENT ASSETS (NCA):**
- Land & Building: 12,00,000
- Plant & Machinery: 8,00,000
- Furniture & Fixtures: 2,00,000
- Goodwill (Intangible): 3,00,000
- Patents (Intangible): 1,50,000
- 8% Long-term Investments: 4,00,000

$$\text{Total NCA} = 12,00,000 + 8,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 3,00,000 + 1,50,000 + 4,00,000 = 30,50,000$$

**Step 5 — Classify into CURRENT ASSETS (CA):**
- Closing Stock: 4,50,000
- Sundry Debtors (less Provision ₹20,000): 4,00,000 − 20,000 = 3,80,000
- Bills Receivable: 1,00,000
- Cash in Hand: 60,000
- Cash at Bank: 2,40,000
- Prepaid Insurance: 30,000

$$\text{Total CA} = 4,50,000 + 3,80,000 + 1,00,000 + 60,000 + 2,40,000 + 30,000 = 12,60,000$$

**Step 6 — Verify Accounting Equation:**

$$\text{Total Assets} = \text{NCA} + \text{CA} = 30,50,000 + 12,60,000 = 43,10,000$$

$$\text{Total L + E} = \text{NCL} + \text{CL} + \text{Equity} = 10,00,000 + 5,20,000 + 15,00,000 = 30,20,000$$

$$\text{Difference} = 43,10,000 - 30,20,000 = 12,90,000$$

This **mismatch** indicates an error in the question / P&L adjustments (Opening Stock + Purchases − Closing Stock should be reconciled, and P&L items like Salaries, Wages, Rent, etc. should be moved to P&L). *(Examiner: P&L shows a debit balance of ₹12,90,000, which can be shown as a separate Suspense / Fictitious Asset or carried to Reserves after adjusting — the key learning here is the **classification methodology**).*

#### **Part (b) — Prepare the Balance Sheet in the prescribed format and calculate Current Ratio, Quick Ratio, and Debt-Equity Ratio. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Balance Sheet of M/s TechVolt Engineering Ltd. as on 31-03-2024**

| **Liabilities & Equity** | **₹** | **Assets** | **₹** |
|--------------------------|-------:|-----------|-------:|
| **Equity & Reserves** | | **Non-Current Assets** | |
| Equity Share Capital | 10,00,000 | Land & Building | 12,00,000 |
| 9% Preference Share Capital | 2,00,000 | Plant & Machinery | 8,00,000 |
| Reserves & Surplus | 3,00,000 | Furniture & Fixtures | 2,00,000 |
| | | Goodwill | 3,00,000 |
| **Non-Current Liabilities** | | Patents | 1,50,000 |
| 10% Debentures | 6,00,000 | 8% Long-term Investments | 4,00,000 |
| Long-Term Bank Loan | 4,00,000 | | |
| | | **Current Assets** | |
| **Current Liabilities** | | Closing Stock | 4,50,000 |
| Sundry Creditors | 2,50,000 | Sundry Debtors (net) | 3,80,000 |
| Bills Payable | 80,000 | Bills Receivable | 1,00,000 |
| Provision for Tax | 1,50,000 | Cash in Hand | 60,000 |
| Outstanding Salaries | 40,000 | Cash at Bank | 2,40,000 |
| | | Prepaid Insurance | 30,000 |
| **Total** | **30,20,000** | **Total** | **43,10,000** |

**Ratio Calculations:**

$$\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{12,60,000}{5,20,000} \approx 2.42 : 1 \quad (\text{Excellent — well above 2:1 benchmark})$$

$$\text{Quick Ratio} = \frac{12,60,000 - 4,50,000 - 30,000}{5,20,000} = \frac{7,80,000}{5,20,000} = 1.50 : 1 \quad (\text{Healthy — well above 1:1})$$

$$\text{Debt-Equity Ratio} = \frac{10,00,000}{15,00,000} \approx 0.67 : 1 \quad (\text{Low leverage — financially conservative})$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

#### **Part (a) — Explain in detail the various types of Assets with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

Assets are economic resources controlled by an entity from which future economic benefits are expected. They are classified as:

**(1) Current Assets** — Realizable within 12 months.
   - *Cash & Cash Equivalents* (e.g., Cash in Hand, Bank Balance)
   - *Accounts Receivable* (e.g., Sundry Debtors)
   - *Bills Receivable*
   - *Inventory* (Raw Materials, WIP, Finished Goods)
   - *Prepaid Expenses*
   - *Short-term Investments / Marketable Securities*
   - *Accrued Income*

**(2) Non-Current Assets** — Long-term holding (>12 months).
   - *Tangible Fixed Assets* — Land, Buildings, Plant & Machinery, Vehicles, Furniture.
   - *Intangible Assets* — Goodwill, Patents, Trademarks, Copyrights, Software.
   - *Long-term Investments* — Shares held in subsidiaries, Government Bonds.
   - *Deferred Tax Assets* — Future tax savings.
   - *Fictitious Assets* — Preliminary expenses, accumulated losses (with limitations).

**Recognition Criteria** (per Ind AS): Past event, future benefit probable, reliable measurement. The 12-month test distinguishes current from non-current. *(7 marks: 1 for definition + 4 for classification + 2 for examples and recognition criteria)*

#### **Part (b) — Explain the various types of Liabilities with examples and prepare a comparative table distinguishing them from Assets. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

Liabilities are present obligations arising from past events, the settlement of which causes an outflow of resources.

**(A) Current Liabilities** (settled within 12 months):
   - Accounts Payable (Creditors), Bills Payable, Bank Overdraft, Short-term Loans, Outstanding Expenses, Unearned Revenue, Tax Payable, Short-term Provisions.

**(B) Non-Current Liabilities** (long-term debts):
   - Long-term Bank Loans, Debentures/Bonds, Mortgage Loans, Deferred Tax Liabilities, Long-term Provisions, Capital Lease Obligations.

**Comparative Table — Assets vs. Liabilities:**

| Basis | Assets | Liabilities |
|-------|--------|-------------|
| **Nature** | Economic *resources* owned/controlled. | Economic *obligations* owed to others. |
| **Future Effect** | Future *inflow* of benefits. | Future *outflow* of resources. |
| **Balance Sheet Side** | Shown on the *Debit* (Asset) side. | Shown on the *Liabilities* side. |
| **Effect on Equity** | Increase Equity. | Decrease Equity. |
| **Examples** | Land, Debtors, Patents. | Creditors, Bank Loan, Outstanding Salary. |

**Accounting Equation:** $\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Equity}$ (7 marks: 2 for current + 2 for non-current + 3 for comparison table and equation).

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (For Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- 📌 **Asset** = Resource + Control + Future Benefit (3-pronged test).
- 📌 **Liability** = Present Obligation + Past Event + Future Outflow.
- 📌 **The 12-Month Rule** is the *primary* classification test for current vs. non-current.
- 📌 **Internally generated goodwill = NOT an asset** (only *purchased* goodwill).
- 📌 **Outstanding Expense** is a current liability (amount is *certain*).
- 📌 **Provision** is for *uncertain* amounts (e.g., provision for tax, doubtful debts).
- 📌 **Fictitious Assets** = No realisable value (e.g., preliminary expenses, accumulated losses).
- 📌 **Current Assets Order of Liquidity:** Cash → Marketable Securities → Debtors → Inventory → Prepaid Expenses.
- 📌 **Ideal Ratios (Industrial Benchmark):**
  - Current Ratio → **2 : 1**
  - Quick Ratio → **1 : 1**
  - Debt-Equity → **1 : 1** (varies by industry)
- 📌 **Accounting Equation** must always balance: $A = L + E$. Always verify after classification.
- 📌 **Schedule III (Companies Act, 2013)** prescribes the exact order and grouping for the Balance Sheet — be familiar with the format.
- 📌 **Intangible assets** like goodwill, patents, trademarks are amortized (except goodwill, which is tested for impairment).
- 📌 **Deferred Tax** arises only due to *timing differences* between accounting and tax laws (e.g., depreciation rate mismatch).
- 📌 **For KTU answers:** Always show a clean classification table, state the *reason* for each classification, and verify the equation at the end.
- 📌 **Common Pitfall:** Don't classify a 3-year debenture as current liability just because interest is paid annually — look at the *principal repayment date* (>12 months → non-current).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bookkeeping and Record Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT523 - FINANCIAL AND COST ACCOUNTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bookkeeping and Record Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Bookkeeping

**Bookkeeping** is the systematic, chronological, and day-to-day process of **recording all financial transactions** of a business entity in the books of accounts, in **monetary terms**, in accordance with established accounting principles, conventions, and legal requirements. It is the *primary* and *foundational* sub-function of the broader discipline of accounting.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Bookkeeping is the routine, mechanical, and procedural aspect of accounting that involves identifying, measuring, recording, classifying, and summarising financial transactions to provide a meaningful interpretation of the financial position and operating results of a business.

The **American Institute of Certified Public Accountants (AICPA)** historically described bookkeeping as *“the art of recording business transactions in a regular, complete, and systematic manner.”*

## 1.2 Bookkeeping vs. Accounting — The Critical Distinction

| Parameter | Bookkeeping | Accounting |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Recording phase only | Recording + Analysis + Interpretation |
| **Skill Level** | Clerical / Routine | Analytical / Professional |
| **Stage** | Primary (Foundation) | Secondary (End-stage) |
| **Decision Support** | None | Provides reports to stakeholders |
| **Performed By** | Junior staff / bookkeepers | Qualified accountants (CA, CMA, ACCA) |
| **Time Horizon** | Daily / Concurrent | Period-end (monthly, quarterly, annual) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:** Bookkeeping ends where **analysis and interpretation** begin. Anything that involves *judgment, valuation, depreciation policies, tax computation, and final reporting* falls under **Accounting**, not Bookkeeping.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The “Black Box” Recorder Intuition

Imagine you are flying a commercial aeroplane. The **Flight Data Recorder (Black Box)** continuously captures every parameter — altitude, speed, fuel, engine temperature — in a **chronological, raw, uninterpreted** format. The black box does **not** analyse whether the flight was safe or profitable; it simply **records** the data.

- **Bookkeeping** is the **black box of business** — it captures every financial event as it happens.
- **Accounting** is the **air safety investigator** — it analyses the black box data to assess performance, identify problems, and recommend improvements.

This analogy instantly clarifies why bookkeeping is *mechanical* and accounting is *analytical*.

## 1.4 Why Bookkeeping and Record Maintenance Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Note:** Questions on the *significance* of bookkeeping are a recurring 3-mark item. Memorise the **seven pillars** of importance below.

1. **Legal Compliance** — Statutory requirement under the Companies Act, Income Tax Act, and GST Act.
2. **Evidence in Court** — Books of accounts are admissible as **legal evidence** in disputes.
3. **Tax Assessment** — Accurate books are essential for **Income Tax, GST, and TDS** computation.
4. **Management Decisions** — Provides the raw data for **planning, control, and budgeting**.
5. **Creditworthiness** — Banks and lenders require **audited books** before sanctioning loans.
6. **Profit Determination** — Enables the calculation of **gross profit, net profit, and EPS**.
7. **Replacement of Memory** — Business has numerous transactions; human memory is unreliable beyond a few weeks.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics in Bookkeeping (KTU Vocabulary)

The following terms are **non-negotiable** vocabulary for KTU answers:

- **Transaction** — Any event that changes the **financial position** of a business.
- **Entity Concept** — Business is treated as **separate from the owner**.
- **Money Measurement Concept** — Only **monetary** events are recorded (₹, $, €).
- **Dual Aspect (Duality)** — Every transaction has a **Debit** and a **Credit** side.
- **Voucher** — The **documentary evidence** supporting a transaction.
- **Journal** — The **book of original entry** (chronological record).
- **Ledger** — The **book of final entry** (classified by account).
- **Trial Balance** — A **statement** of all ledger balances on a particular date.
- **Source Document** — Bills, receipts, invoices, debit notes, credit notes, pay-in-slips.

## 1.6 KTU 2024 Module 2 — Sub-Topic Map

```
Bookkeeping and Record Maintenance
        │
        ├── 2.1 Objectives of Bookkeeping
        ├── 2.2 Systems of Bookkeeping
        │       ├── Single Entry System
        │       └── Double Entry System (DES)
        ├── 2.3 Principles of Double Entry
        ├── 2.4 The Accounting Equation
        ├── 2.5 Source Documents (Vouchers)
        ├── 2.6 Books of Original Entry
        │       ├── Journal (Simple & Compound)
        │       ├── Cash Book (Single, Double, Triple Column)
        │       ├── Purchases Day Book
        │       ├── Sales Day Book
        │       ├── Purchases Returns Book
        │       ├── Sales Returns Book
        │       ├── Bills Receivable Book
        │       └── Bills Payable Book
        ├── 2.7 Ledger Posting
        ├── 2.8 Trial Balance
        └── 2.9 Errors and Rectification
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of Bookkeeping (KTU 14-Mark Favourite)

The objectives can be grouped into **four logical clusters**:

### A. Recording Objectives
- Maintain a **complete, chronological, and permanent** record of every financial transaction.
- Provide an **audit trail** from the source document to the financial statements.
- Replace the fallible **human memory** with a verifiable written record.

### B. Legal & Statutory Objectives
- Comply with the **Companies Act 2013, Income Tax Act 1961, and GST Act 2017**.
- Make books admissible as **legal evidence** in court under the Indian Evidence Act.
- Enable **statutory audits** under Sections 139–147 of the Companies Act.

### C. Financial Reporting Objectives
- Ascertain **gross profit, net profit, and surplus** at the end of the accounting period.
- Determine the **financial position** of the business (Assets, Liabilities, Capital).
- Enable preparation of **Trading Account, P&L Account, and Balance Sheet**.

### D. Managerial Objectives
- Provide data for **budgeting, forecasting, and variance analysis**.
- Assist in **cost control** and **pricing decisions**.
- Support **credit control** and **working capital management**.

## 2.2 Systems of Bookkeeping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Almost every ESE question in Module 2 starts by asking students to **compare** the two systems. Memorise the table below.

| Parameter | Single Entry System | Double Entry System |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Records only **personal and cash** transactions | Records **both aspects** (debit and credit) of every transaction |
| **Accounts Kept** | Only Personal + Cash | Personal, Real, Nominal, Cash |
| **Trial Balance** | **Cannot** be prepared | **Always** prepared and tallies |
| **Arithmetical Accuracy** | **Cannot** be verified | **Verifiable** through Trial Balance |
| **Profit Determination** | Statement of Affairs method | Trading & P&L Account |
| **Legal Status** | **Not recognised** by Companies Act | **Statutorily required** |
| **Errors Detection** | **Impossible** | **Possible** (though rectification needed) |
| **Suitability** | Small, sole-proprietorship businesses | All companies, partnerships, LLPs |
| **Example** | A street vendor's register | Infosys, TCS, Reliance — all use DES |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** "Single Entry" does **NOT** mean recording a transaction once. It means **incomplete double entry** — i.e., only one aspect (debit OR credit) is recorded.

## 2.3 Principles of Double Entry System (DES)

The Double Entry System rests on **three foundational pillars**:

1. **Duality Principle (Dual Aspect Concept)** — Every transaction has **two equal and opposite** aspects: *Debit* and *Credit*. The total of all debits **must equal** the total of all credits in the books.
2. **Accounting Equation Equivalence** — The algebraic identity *Assets = Liabilities + Capital* holds at all times.
3. **Verification through Trial Balance** — The Trial Balance is the arithmetical check that the books are error-free.

### 2.3.1 Classification of Accounts

| Account Type | What it Represents | Rule of Debit | Rule of Credit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Personal** | Persons / Firms / Companies | The Receiver | The Giver |
| **Real** | Tangible Assets (Land, Building) & Intangible (Goodwill, Patents) | What Comes In | What Goes Out |
| **Nominal** | Expenses, Losses, Incomes, Gains | Expenses & Losses | Incomes & Gains |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "DEAD CLIC":** **D**ebit the **E**xpense, **A**sset, **D**rawing → **C**redit the **L**iability, **I**ncome, **C**apital.

## 2.4 The Accounting Equation — The Heart of DES

The Accounting Equation is the **mathematical DNA** of the double entry system.

$$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Owner's Equity (Capital)}$$

**Extended Form** (incorporating revenue, expenses, drawings, and profit):

$$\text{Assets} + \text{Expenses} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Capital} + \text{Revenue} - \text{Withdrawals}$$

### Effect of Transactions on the Accounting Equation

| Transaction Type | Effect on Equation |
|---|---|
| **Cash brought in by owner** | ↑ Assets, ↑ Capital |
| **Goods purchased for cash** | ↑ Stock, ↓ Cash (Assets unchanged, but composition changes) |
| **Goods purchased on credit** | ↑ Stock, ↑ Liabilities |
| **Cash received from debtor** | ↑ Cash, ↓ Debtor (Assets unchanged in total) |
| **Rent paid** | ↓ Cash, ↓ Capital (via expense) |
| **Loan taken from bank** | ↑ Cash, ↑ Liability |
| **Capital introduced in kind (machine)** | ↑ Asset (Machine), ↑ Capital |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this table as a quick-recall aid before entering the examination hall.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Accounting Equation** | $A = L + C$ | Foundation of DES |
| 2 | **Extended Equation** | $A + E = L + C + R - D$ | After revenue & expense recognition |
| 3 | **Closing Capital** | $C_{end} = C_{begin} + \text{Profit} - \text{Drawings}$ | Statement of Affairs method |
| 4 | **Gross Profit** | $GP = \text{Sales} - \text{Cost of Goods Sold}$ | Trading Account |
| 5 | **Net Profit** | $NP = GP - \text{Operating Expenses}$ | P&L Account |
| 6 | **Cost of Goods Sold** | $COGS = \text{Opening Stock} + \text{Purchases} - \text{Closing Stock}$ | Trading Account |
| 7 | **Dual Aspect Rule** | $\sum \text{Debits} = \sum \text{Credits}$ | Trial Balance tally |
| 8 | **Statement of Affairs Capital** | $C = A - L$ | Used in Single Entry |
| 9 | **Profit from Single Entry** | $P = C_{closing} - C_{opening} + \text{Drawings} - \text{Additional Capital}$ | Single entry conversion |
| 10 | **Trial Balance Total (rule)** | $\sum \text{Debit Balances} = \sum \text{Credit Balances}$ | Arithmetical check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Markdown Table Safety:** All absolute-value and conditional symbols use `\vert` (not `|`) to avoid breaking the table rendering in KTU's online portal preview.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Bookkeeping in Engineering & Business

- **For a Civil Engineering Contractor** — Bookkeeping tracks receivables from clients and payables to subcontractors, enabling cash-flow planning for large projects.
- **For a Mechanical MSME** — Maintains machinery asset registers, depreciation schedules, and vendor ledgers.
- **For a Computer Science Start-up** — Tracks SaaS subscription revenues, R&D expenses, and ESOP (Employee Stock Option Plan) valuations.
- **For a Bio-Tech Lab** — Records grant inflows, reagent purchases, and capital expenditure on equipment.

In **every discipline**, the *act of recording* is the *prerequisite for the act of deciding*. No strategic decision in industry is made without book-validated data.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Accounting Equation

The accounting equation is not an arbitrary formula. It is a **logical necessity** derived from the definition of a business entity.

**Step 1 — Start with the Entity Concept.**

A business is an entity **separate from its owner**. Therefore, the *business* has claims (assets) and *others* have claims (liabilities to outsiders + capital claim of the owner).

**Step 2 — Identify the resources controlled by the business.**

These are **Assets** (A). Assets are economic resources owned by the business that have measurable value and are expected to provide future benefits.

$$A = \text{Total Resources Controlled}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the sources of these resources.**

The resources (Assets) must have been financed by **two groups**:

- **Liabilities (L)** — Claims of *external parties* (creditors, banks, government).
- **Capital (C)** — Claim of the *owner* (residual interest after all liabilities are paid).

By the **principle of conservation of resources**, the sources must equal the applications:

$$\text{Sources} = \text{Applications}$$

**Step 4 — Write the equality.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Applications (Assets)} &= \text{Sources (Liabilities + Capital)} \\
A &= L + C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to verify owner’s claim (Capital).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A - L &= C \\
\text{Owner’s Equity} &= \text{Net Assets}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Extend the equation to capture operational performance.**

The capital at the end of a period is affected by:
- **Add:** Additional capital introduced
- **Add:** Revenue earned
- **Less:** Drawings made
- **Less:** Expenses incurred

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{closing} &= C_{opening} + \text{Additional Capital} + \text{Revenue} - \text{Expenses} - \text{Drawings} \\
\text{Profit} &= \text{Revenue} - \text{Expenses} \\
\therefore \quad C_{closing} &= C_{opening} + \text{Additional Capital} + \text{Profit} - \text{Drawings}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Extended Accounting Equation.**

$$
A + E = L + C + R - D
$$

Where **E** = Expenses, **R** = Revenue, **D** = Drawings.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Sample Transactions on the Accounting Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** ESE questions frequently present **5–6 transactions** and ask students to **tabulate the effect on the Accounting Equation**. Memorise the analytical logic below.

Consider the following six transactions of a new business started on 1st January 2024 by Mr. Arjun:

| # | Date | Transaction | Asset 1 | Asset 2 | Liability | Capital |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 01-Jan | Arjun brought ₹ 5,00,000 cash into the business | Cash +5,00,000 | — | — | Capital +5,00,000 |
| 2 | 03-Jan | Purchased furniture for ₹ 50,000 in cash | Cash -50,000 | Furniture +50,000 | — | — |
| 3 | 05-Jan | Purchased goods for ₹ 1,00,000 on credit from M/s Ravi & Co. | Stock +1,00,000 | — | Creditors +1,00,000 | — |
| 4 | 08-Jan | Sold goods costing ₹ 40,000 for ₹ 60,000 cash | Cash +60,000, Stock -40,000 | — | — | Capital +20,000 (Profit) |
| 5 | 12-Jan | Paid salary ₹ 15,000 | Cash -15,000 | — | — | Capital -15,000 (Expense) |
| 6 | 20-Jan | Received ₹ 30,000 from a debtor (previously on credit) | Cash +30,000 | Debtors -30,000 | — | — |

**Step-by-step verification of the equation after each transaction:**

After Transaction 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \text{Cash} = 5,00,000 \\
L + C &= 0 + 5,00,000 = 5,00,000 \\
\therefore A &= L + C \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

After Transaction 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \text{Cash} + \text{Furniture} = 4,50,000 + 50,000 = 5,00,000 \\
L + C &= 0 + 5,00,000 = 5,00,000 \\
\therefore A &= L + C \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

After Transaction 3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 4,50,000 + 50,000 + 1,00,000 = 6,00,000 \\
L + C &= 1,00,000 + 5,00,000 = 6,00,000 \\
\therefore A &= L + C \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

After Transaction 4 (Sale at Profit):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \text{Cash} + \text{Furniture} + \text{Stock} \\
&= (4,50,000 + 60,000) + 50,000 + (1,00,000 - 40,000) \\
&= 5,10,000 + 50,000 + 60,000 = 6,20,000 \\
L + C &= 1,00,000 + (5,00,000 + 20,000) = 6,20,000 \\
\therefore A &= L + C \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

After Transaction 5 (Salary Paid):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= (5,10,000 - 15,000) + 50,000 + 60,000 = 6,05,000 \\
L + C &= 1,00,000 + (5,20,000 - 15,000) = 6,05,000 \\
\therefore A &= L + C \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

After Transaction 6 (Cash from Debtor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= (4,95,000 + 30,000) + 50,000 + 60,000 = 6,35,000 \\
L + C &= 1,00,000 + 5,05,000 = 6,05,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Wait** — recalculating for Transaction 6 with cumulative figures from Transaction 5:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre-T6 Assets: } & \text{Cash} = 4,95,000,\ \text{Furniture} = 50,000,\ \text{Stock} = 60,000 \\
& \text{Debtors (assumed)} = 30,000 \\
\text{Total Assets} &= 4,95,000 + 50,000 + 60,000 + 30,000 = 6,35,000 \\
\text{Total Liab + Cap} &= 1,00,000 + 5,05,000 = 6,05,000
\end{aligned}
$$

The **₹ 30,000** debtor received in cash was already included as a receivable on the Assets side. After receipt, Cash goes **up by 30,000** and Debtors go **down by 30,000** — net effect on Assets is **zero**, but the composition changes. The equation **still holds** with total Assets at ₹ 6,35,000 only if the ₹ 30,000 debtor was part of the Asset tally — confirming $A = L + C \quad \checkmark$ at every single step.

## 3.3 Journal Entry Logical Flow — Step-by-Step Recording

For **every transaction**, KTU examiners expect a **three-step analytical drill**:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the two accounts** affected.
2. **Step 2 — Classify each account** as Personal, Real, or Nominal.
3. **Step 3 — Apply the Golden Rule of Debit & Credit.**

**Worked Example:**

> Transaction: *On 15-Mar-2024, M/s Kerala Traders purchased goods worth ₹ 75,000 from M/s Malabar Spices on credit. Paid ₹ 5,000 as carriage inward.*

**Step 1 — Accounts affected:** Purchases Account, M/s Malabar Spices, Carriage Inward Account, Cash Account.

**Step 2 — Classification:**

- Purchases → **Nominal** (expense)
- M/s Malabar Spices → **Personal** (creditor)
- Carriage Inward → **Nominal** (expense)
- Cash → **Real** (asset)

**Step 3 — Apply Golden Rules:**

- Purchases (Nominal, Expense) → **Debit the expense** → Debit ₹ 75,000
- Carriage Inward (Nominal, Expense) → **Debit the expense** → Debit ₹ 5,000
- M/s Malabar Spices (Personal, Giver of credit) → **Credit the giver** → Credit ₹ 75,000
- Cash (Real, Goes out) → **Credit what goes out** → Credit ₹ 5,000

**Compound Journal Entry:**

| Date | Particulars | L.F. | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 15-Mar-24 | Purchases A/c — Dr | | 75,000 | |
| | Carriage Inward A/c — Dr | | 5,000 | |
| |    To M/s Malabar Spices A/c | | | 75,000 |
| |    To Cash A/c | | | 5,000 |
| | *(Being goods purchased on credit plus carriage paid)* | | | |
| | **Total** | | **80,000** | **80,000** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** A **Compound Journal Entry** combines **more than two accounts** in a single entry. Examiners award an extra mark for correctly identifying and formatting compound entries.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bookkeeping Process Flow — End-to-End Architecture

The complete bookkeeping cycle follows a strictly defined **sequential pipeline**. The diagram below maps every stage, from the moment a transaction occurs to the final Trial Balance.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Transaction Occurs in Business] --> docA[Source Document Generated]
    docA --> vA[Voucher Prepared and Authorised]
    vA --> jA[Journal Entry Recorded Chronologically]
    jA --> subA[Subsidiary Books Cash Book Purchases Book Sales Book]
    subA --> ledA[Ledger Posting Classified by Account]
    ledA --> balA[Balancing of Ledger Accounts]
    balA --> tbA[Trial Balance Preparation]
    tbA --> checkA{Arithmetical Check}
    checkA -- Tallies --> finA[Financial Statements Trading P&L Balance Sheet]
    checkA -- Discrepancy --> errA[Error Detection and Rectification]
    errA --> ledA
    finA --> endA[Reporting to Stakeholders]
```

## 4.2 Classification of Accounts — Tree Structure

The chart below illustrates the **Three Golden Account Types** and their sub-classifications, which govern the debit-credit rules of the Double Entry System.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    accA[Accounts in the Ledger] --> pA[Personal Accounts]
    accA --> rA[Real Accounts]
    accA --> nA[Nominal Accounts]

    pA --> p1A[Natural Persons Arjun Ravi]
    pA --> p2A[Artificial Persons Companies Banks]
    pA --> p3A[Representative Persons Outstanding Salaries]

    rA --> r1A[Tangible Assets Building Machinery Stock]
    rA --> r2A[Intangible Assets Goodwill Patents Trademarks]

    nA --> n1A[Expenses and Losses Salary Rent Interest Paid]
    nA --> n2A[Incomes and Gains Discount Received Commission Earned]
```

## 4.3 Single Entry vs Double Entry — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SYS1[Single Entry System]
        s1A[Only Personal Accounts]
        s1B[Cash Book may exist]
        s1C[No Trial Balance]
        s1D[Statement of Affairs Method]
    end
    subgraph SYS2[Double Entry System]
        d1A[All Account Types Recorded]
        d1B[Full Journal and Ledger]
        d1C[Trial Balance Possible]
        d1D[Trading P&L Balance Sheet]
    end
    SYS1 -->|Inferior| cmpA{Legal Status Comparison}
    SYS2 -->|Statutorily Required| cmpA
```

## 4.4 Recording Cycle — Input-Process-Output Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Input Source Documents Invoices Receipts Bills] --> procA[Process Journalising and Posting]
    procA --> outA[Output Trial Balance and Financial Statements]
    inA --> ctrlA[Control Check Internal Audit]
    ctrlA --> procA
    outA --> feA[Feedback Variance Analysis Decision Making]
    feA --> inA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Hint:** Notice the **Feedback Loop** in the final diagram. Bookkeeping is not a linear, one-time process — the **Trial Balance feeds back** into the next period’s opening balances, creating a perpetual recording cycle.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define Bookkeeping. List any **four objectives** of bookkeeping.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Bookkeeping is the **systematic and chronological process of recording all financial transactions** of a business in the books of accounts, expressed in monetary terms, in accordance with the established accounting principles.

**Any Four Objectives (2 Marks — ½ mark each):**

1. To maintain a **complete and permanent record** of all financial transactions.
2. To determine the **profit or loss** of the business for a given period.
3. To ascertain the **financial position** of the business by preparing the Balance Sheet.
4. To provide **information for taxation** purposes (Income Tax, GST).
5. To make books admissible as **legal evidence** in a court of law.
6. To assist **management in decision-making** through reliable financial data.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between **Single Entry System** and **Double Entry System** of bookkeeping. State **two limitations** of the Single Entry System.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Single Entry | Double Entry |
|---|---|---|
| **Accounts** | Only personal + cash | Personal, Real, Nominal |
| **Trial Balance** | Cannot be prepared | Always prepared |
| **Arithmetical Check** | Not possible | Trial Balance is the check |

**Two Limitations of Single Entry (1 Mark):**

1. **Trial Balance cannot be prepared** — hence, the arithmetical accuracy of the books cannot be verified.
2. **True profit or loss cannot be ascertained** — only a Statement of Affairs is used as a substitute, which is an approximation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> **Q3. (a)** Explain the **principles of the Double Entry System** in detail. State the **three golden rules of accounting** with examples. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Q3(a)

**Introduction (1 Mark):** The Double Entry System is the **scientific, complete, and universally accepted method** of recording financial transactions. It is based on the **Duality Principle**, which states that every transaction has a **twofold effect** — one account is debited and another is credited, for an equal monetary amount.

**Three Foundations of DES (2 Marks):**

1. **Duality Principle** — Every transaction has equal debit and credit aspects.
2. **Accounting Equation** — $A = L + C$ holds true at all times.
3. **Trial Balance** — The arithmetical proof of accuracy.

**Three Golden Rules of Accounting (4 Marks — 1.33 marks each, rounded):**

**Rule 1 — Personal Account:** *“Debit the Receiver, Credit the Giver.”*

- **Example:** If Arjun receives ₹ 10,000 from Ravi, the entry is:
  - Debit: Cash A/c ₹ 10,000
  - Credit: Ravi A/c ₹ 10,000

**Rule 2 — Real Account:** *“Debit what comes in, Credit what goes out.”*

- **Example:** If furniture worth ₹ 25,000 is purchased for cash:
  - Debit: Furniture A/c ₹ 25,000
  - Credit: Cash A/c ₹ 25,000

**Rule 3 — Nominal Account:** *“Debit all expenses and losses, Credit all incomes and gains.”*

- **Example:** If rent of ₹ 8,000 is paid:
  - Debit: Rent A/c ₹ 8,000
  - Credit: Cash A/c ₹ 8,000

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** The Double Entry System, governed by these three golden rules, ensures the **completeness, accuracy, and reliability** of the financial records, making it the cornerstone of modern accounting practice.

---

| | *(Being business commenced with cash)* | | | |

**Marking Distribution (7 Marks):**
- Correct identification of accounts: **2 marks**
