<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Define Cost Accounting. State any four of its features.**

**Model Answer (Valuation-Ready):**

**Definition**: Cost Accounting is the systematic process of recording, classifying, analyzing, summarizing, and reporting the costs incurred in producing a product or rendering a service, to enable management to control costs and make informed decisions.

**Four Features**:
1. It is **analytical** — it investigates the *why* of cost incurrence, not just the *what*.
2. It serves **internal users** (management) and not external parties.
3. It is **future-oriented** — besides historical data, it uses standards, budgets, and estimates.
4. It is **a continuous process**, performed throughout the accounting period rather than once at year-end.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**Distinguish between Costing and Cost Accountancy.**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form — Board-Preferred Layout)**:

| Basis of Distinction | Costing | Cost Accountancy |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | The technique of ascertaining cost | The entire body of knowledge on cost |
| **Scope** | Narrow — only cost computation | Broad — includes principles, methods, application |
| **Nature** | Practical/operational | Theoretical + practical |
| **Usage** | A sub-part of cost accounting | The parent discipline |
| **Example** | Calculating cost per kg of cement | Studying why and how cement cost is determined |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Adapted]*

> **(a)** Define Cost Accounting. Explain in detail its **features** and **objectives**. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the **scope of Cost Accounting** in modern business. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Q.A(a)

**Definition** (2 marks):
Cost Accounting is the systematic application of accounting principles to the recording, classification, analysis, summarization, and reporting of costs, both historical and predetermined, for the purpose of cost control, cost reduction, pricing, and managerial decision-making.

**Features** (3 marks):
1. **Analytical in nature** — goes beyond mere recording to investigate causes.
2. **Both historical and future-oriented** — uses past data + standards/estimates.
3. **Internal-purpose oriented** — serves management, not statutory authorities.
4. **Integrated with other disciplines** — links with production, finance, HR.
5. **Continuous process** — operates throughout the year.
6. **Aids decision-making** — every report culminates in a managerial action.

**Objectives** (2 marks):
- Ascertainment of cost
- Cost control and cost reduction
- Pricing decisions
- Profitability measurement
- Budgetary control support
- Performance evaluation

#### Solution to Q.A(b)

**Scope of Cost Accounting** (7 marks):
1. **Material Cost** — procurement, issue, valuation, control of direct and indirect material.
2. **Labour Cost** — wage fixation, timekeeping, idle time, overtime, incentive schemes.
3. **Overhead Cost** — manufacturing, administrative, selling, and distribution overheads — both allocation and apportionment.
4. **Cost of Production** — determination of per-unit cost of finished goods.
5. **Cost of Sales** — cost incurred in marketing and distribution.
6. **Cost of Service** — applied to transport, hospitality, banking, and IT sectors.
7. **Decision-Making Costs** — special cost studies such as marginal costing, differential costing, opportunity cost, and replacement cost.
8. **Statutory Compliance** — industries like cement, sugar, fertilizers, and electricity are mandated to maintain cost records under the Companies (Cost Records and Audit) Rules.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Adapted]*

> **(a)** Explain the **relationship** among Cost, Costing, Cost Accounting, and Cost Accountancy with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Distinguish between **Cost Accounting and Financial Accounting** on any seven relevant parameters. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Q.B(a)

**Layered Explanation** (5 marks):
- **Cost** — A monetary measure of resources sacrificed to achieve a specific objective. The base object.
- **Costing** — The *technique* of computing cost. Asks *"How much does it cost?"*
- **Cost Accounting** — The *process* of recording, classifying, and reporting costs. Asks *"What does the cost data mean?"*
- **Cost Accountancy** — The *discipline* that includes principles, methods, and practices governing cost determination. Asks *"Why and in what framework should costing be done?"*

**Diagram** (2 marks): Refer to **Diagram A** in Section 4.1 above. Reproduce the hierarchical Mermaid chart on paper.

#### Solution to Q.B(b)

| Parameter | Cost Accounting | Financial Accounting |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Cost ascertainment & control | Profit computation & financial position |
| **Users** | Internal management | External — shareholders, banks, tax authorities |
| **Statutory Obligation** | Not mandatory in most industries | Mandatory under Companies Act |
| **Time Orientation** | Past + Future | Past only |
| **Data Granularity** | Product, Job, Process, Batch level | Entity as a whole |
| **Valuation of Stock** | At cost (FIFO/LIFO/Weighted Avg.) | At cost or NRV, as per Ind AS |
| **Reporting Period** | Weekly, monthly, quarterly | Annually / half-yearly |
| **Analytical Depth** | High — variance analysis, marginal costing | Low — aggregate financial statements |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Triggers on this Topic**:
> 1. **Defining "Cost Accounting" and "Cost Accountancy" interchangeably** — the examiner docks 1–2 marks. Always clarify the layered relationship.
> 2. **Writing objectives without supporting examples** — list 6+ objectives, but *pick 2 and illustrate* (e.g., "Cost control is achieved through budgetary control and standard costing").
> 3. **Omitting a diagram in the 7-mark "Explain the relationship" sub-question** — diagrams carry 2 marks and are *non-negotiable* in KTU 2024 Scheme.
> 4. **Confusing Cost Accounting with Management Accounting** — Management Accounting is *broader* and *more strategic*; Cost Accounting is a *subset* feeding it.
> 5. **Not mentioning statutory cost records** for industries like cement, sugar, electricity — this is a high-value add-on that boosts marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition ready**: Cost Accounting is the systematic recording, classifying, analyzing, summarizing, allocating, and reporting of costs for managerial use.
- **Layered relationship** must be memorized in order: *Cost → Costing → Cost Accounting → Cost Accountancy* (smallest to largest).
- **Six to Eight Objectives** to be learned by heart, especially *ascertainment, control, reduction, pricing, profitability, budgeting, performance evaluation*.
- **Seven broad areas of scope**: Material, Labour, Overhead, Production, Sales, Service, and Decision-making cost.
- **Seven features** of cost accounting — especially its *analytical, future-oriented, internal, continuous* nature.
- **Three-way distinction** is mandatory: Cost Accounting vs Financial Accounting vs Management Accounting.
- **Costing ≠ Cost Accounting** — Costing is a *technique*; Cost Accounting is the *applied process*; Cost Accountancy is the *theory/framework*.
- **Formula to remember**:
$$
\text{Total Cost} = \text{Prime Cost} + \text{Overheads} = \text{Direct Material} + \text{Direct Labour} + \text{Direct Expenses} + \text{Overheads}
$$
- **Cost per Unit Formula**:
$$
\text{Cost per Unit} = \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Number of Units Produced}}
$$
- **Key statutory industries** (under cost records rules): cement, sugar, fertilizers, electricity, petroleum.
- **Installation Steps** (7-step chain): Investigation → Method selection → Cost centers/units → Coding → Forms → Overhead absorption rate → Integration with financial accounts.
- **First-line answer opener** for any 14-mark question: always begin with a *definition* and a *diagram*.
- **Practical link**: connect every concept to a real industrial example (e.g., Kerala Coir Industry, KMML, BPCL Kochi) for examiner-richness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT523 - FINANCIAL AND COST ACCOUNTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Elements of Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Cost Accounting, the **Elements of Cost** refer to the three fundamental categories of expenditure incurred in the production of a good or service. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECBT523) and standard Cost Accounting principles (CIMA terminology), every cost incurred in a manufacturing or service organization can be classified into one of the following three primary elements:

1. **Material** — The physical inputs consumed in production (raw materials, consumables, components).
2. **Labour** — The human effort (manual and mental) expended for converting raw materials into finished goods.
3. **Expenses** — All other costs incurred which are neither material nor labour (e.g., rent, depreciation, power, insurance).

These three elements are further sub-classified as **Direct** (traceable to a specific cost object/product) or **Indirect** (not traceable; recovered as **Overheads**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Expected in 3-Mark Answers):**
> "The three elements of cost are **Material, Labour, and Expenses**. These are the foundation blocks of the cost sheet and are used to build up the total cost of a product or service by adding appropriate overheads at each stage."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cake Bakery" Intuition

Imagine a small bakery producing a **1 kg Chocolate Cake**. The total cost to produce this cake consists of:

| Bakery Element | Element of Cost | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Flour, cocoa, sugar, butter, eggs | **Direct Material** | ₹200 |
| Chef's wages (specifically for this cake) | **Direct Labour** | ₹100 |
| Cake box, ribbon, customized label | **Direct Expenses** | ₹50 |
| Bakery rent, oven electricity, supervisor's salary | **Indirect (Overheads)** | ₹150 |

**Total Cost of Cake = ₹500**

Just as every bakery item can be broken down into ingredients, effort, and overheads, every manufactured product's cost is built by aggregating these three elements. This is the essence of **Cost Ascertainment** in Cost Accounting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Elements" and not "Components"?**
> The term *elements* is used because these are the *primary, irreducible* categories of cost. All other derived cost concepts (Prime Cost, Factory Cost, Cost of Production, Cost of Sales) are simply *summations* or *aggregations* of these three elements combined with overheads.

---

## 1.3 Classification Matrix of Cost Elements

```
                    ┌─────────────────────────────┐
                    │      ELEMENTS OF COST       │
                    └──────────────┬──────────────┘
                                   │
        ┌──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┐
        │                          │                          │
   ┌────▼─────┐              ┌─────▼─────┐              ┌─────▼─────┐
   │ MATERIAL │              │  LABOUR   │              │ EXPENSES  │
   └────┬─────┘              └─────┬─────┘              └─────┬─────┘
        │                          │                          │
   ┌────┴─────────┐           ┌────┴─────────┐           ┌────┴─────────┐
   │              │           │              │           │              │
DIRECT        INDIRECT     DIRECT         INDIRECT    DIRECT        INDIRECT
MATERIAL      MATERIAL     LABOUR         LABOUR      EXPENSES      EXPENSES
(Traceable)  (Stores,      (Wages to      (Supervisor, (Special       (Rent, Light,
              consumables)  workers)       foreman)    job hire)     insurance)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024 Board Exams, you MUST know the sub-classification: **Direct vs Indirect** for each of the three elements. A 7-mark question frequently asks students to classify a given list of expenses into the appropriate cost element sub-category.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Element 1: MATERIAL (Detailed Breakdown)

**Material** is the physical substance from which the product is made. It is sub-divided into:

### 2.1.1 Direct Material
- Raw materials that become an **integral part** of the finished product.
- **Easily identifiable** and **measurable** per unit of output.
- Examples: Timber in furniture, Steel in machinery, Cloth in garments, Flour in bread.

### 2.1.2 Indirect Material
- Materials that **cannot be conveniently traced** to a specific product.
- Used to support production but do not form the main body of the product.
- Examples: Lubricants, Cotton waste, Nails, Sandpaper, Cleaning materials, Stationery in factory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Cost Control Techniques** (Expected in KTU viva): *Economic Order Quantity (EOQ), ABC Analysis, VED Analysis, Perpetual Inventory System, Material Issue Pricing (FIFO, LIFO, Weighted Average, Standard Cost).*

---

## 2.2 Element 2: LABOUR (Detailed Breakdown)

**Labour** is the human resource (manual or mental) used for converting raw materials into finished goods. It represents the remuneration paid to employees.

### 2.2.1 Direct Labour
- Wages paid to workers who are **directly engaged** in production.
- Their time and effort can be **specifically traced** to the product.
- Examples: Wages of machine operators, assembly line workers, tailors in a garment unit.

### 2.2.2 Indirect Labour
- Wages paid to workers who **support production** but are not directly involved in conversion.
- Examples: Salaries of supervisors, foremen, store-keepers, time-keepers, inspectors, maintenance staff.

### 2.2.3 Components of Labour Cost (per worker)

$$\text{Total Labour Cost per Worker} = \text{Basic Pay} + \text{DA} + \text{HRA} + \text{Bonus} + \text{PF (Employer Share)} + \text{ESI} + \text{Overtime} + \text{Gratuity Provision}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Idle Time vs Overtime Premium** (Frequently tested): Idle time wages are usually treated as **indirect labour (factory overhead)**. Overtime premium, when incurred due to the general policy of the firm, is also indirect; when due to a specific customer's rush order, it is **direct labour**.

---

## 2.3 Element 3: EXPENSES (Detailed Breakdown)

**Expenses** are all costs incurred other than material and labour. They are 'consumed' (used up) during the accounting period.

### 2.3.1 Direct Expenses
- Expenditure specifically incurred **for a particular product/job** (other than material and labour).
- Examples: Hire charges of special equipment for a specific job, Royalty paid per unit produced, Patent fees, Special patterns, Designs bought for a specific order.

### 2.3.2 Indirect Expenses (Overheads)
- All expenses other than direct material, direct labour, and direct expenses.
- Cannot be directly traced to a product.
- Examples: Rent, Rates & Taxes, Depreciation, Insurance, Power, Lighting, Repairs, Advertising, Office Salaries.

---

## 2.4 Overheads — A Special Category (KTU Favourite Topic)

**Overheads = Total Cost − Direct Costs (Prime Cost)**

Overheads are classified into three functional groups:

| Category | Also Called | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Factory / Manufacturing Overheads** | Works Overheads, Production Overheads | Factory rent, factory lighting, depreciation of plant, factory supervisor salary, consumable stores |
| **Office Overheads** | Administrative Overheads | Office rent, office stationery, depreciation of office equipment, salaries of office staff |
| **Selling & Distribution Overheads** | S&D Overheads | Advertising, packing, carriage outwards, sales commission, showroom expenses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Trick:** Students often confuse *Administration Overheads* with *Selling Overheads*. Remember: **Selling Overheads begin where the product is ready to be sold**. Office overheads relate to policy-making and overall administration.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Cost Build-Up Sequence)

The following table gives the **stepwise build-up** from elements of cost to the final Cost of Sales. This is the **most-expected** 14-mark question in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| Step | Component | Formula (in LaTeX) | What it Adds |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Direct Material** $DM$ | Purchases + Carriage − Returns | Material traceable to product |
| 2 | **Direct Labour** $DL$ | Wages to production workers | Human effort traceable |
| 3 | **Direct Expenses** $DE$ | Specific job expenses | Traceable non-material/labour |
| 4 | **PRIME COST** $PC$ | $PC = DM + DL + DE$ | All direct costs combined |
| 5 | **Add: Factory Overheads** $FOH$ | Indirect material + Indirect labour + Indirect factory expenses | Recovered on absorption basis |
| 6 | **FACTORY COST / WORKS COST** $WKC$ | $WKC = PC + FOH$ | Cost up to factory gate |
| 7 | **Add: Office Overheads** $OH_{admin}$ | Administrative expenses | Policy/management cost |
| 8 | **COST OF PRODUCTION** $COP$ | $COP = WKC + OH_{admin}$ | Total cost of producing the goods |
| 9 | **Add: Selling & Distribution OH** $S\&D$ | Selling + distribution expenses | Cost of bringing to market |
| 10 | **COST OF SALES** $COS$ | $COS = COP + S\&D$ | Total cost delivered to customer |

**Alternative total cost formula:**

$$COS = DM + DL + DE + FOH + Admin\ OH + S\&D\ OH$$

**Inventory Value Rule (KTU Board Favourite):**

$$Stock\ Value\ (Closing) = COP \pm (Adjustments)$$
$$Finished\ Goods\ Value = Cost\ of\ Production$$
$$WIP\ Value = Factory\ Cost$$
$$Raw\ Material\ Value = Material\ Cost$$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Industry

| Sector | Application of Elements of Cost |
|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Quotation pricing, tender preparation, cost control in CNC shops |
| **Construction** | BOQ (Bill of Quantities) preparation, contract cost monitoring |
| **IT / Software** | Project cost estimation (developers' wages = direct labour) |
| **Automobile** | Per-vehicle cost ascertainment, vendor negotiation on material |
| **Cement / Steel** | Process-costing application where bulk raw material dominates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Engineers should know this:** As future B.Tech graduates, you may head a production unit, a project, or your own start-up. The ability to break down the cost of a product into material, labour, and expenses — and to identify direct vs indirect — is the foundation of every managerial decision: **pricing, budgeting, make-or-buy, and break-even analysis.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of Prime Cost

**Statement:** Prime Cost is the aggregate of all direct costs incurred in producing a product.

**Derivation:**

Let:
- $DM_i$ = Direct Material consumed for item $i$
- $DL_i$ = Direct Labour wages paid for item $i$
- $DE_i$ = Direct Expenses specifically for item $i$

Then, by the **principle of direct cost aggregation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Prime\ Cost\ (PC) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} DM_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} DL_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} DE_i \\
&= (DM_1 + DM_2 + \ldots + DM_n) \\
&\quad + (DL_1 + DL_2 + \ldots + DL_n) \\
&\quad + (DE_1 + DE_2 + \ldots + DE_n)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a single product unit (most common in KTU problems):

$$
\boxed{Prime\ Cost = Direct\ Material + Direct\ Labour + Direct\ Expenses}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Factory Cost (Works Cost)

**Statement:** Factory Cost is the cost of production up to the factory gate, inclusive of all manufacturing overheads.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Factory\ Cost\ (WKC) &= Prime\ Cost + Factory\ Overheads \\
&= (DM + DL + DE) + FOH
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Factory\ Overheads\ (FOH) &= Indirect\ Material + Indirect\ Labour \\
&\quad + Factory\ Indirect\ Expenses
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked KTU-Style Example (14-mark pattern):**

**Q:** From the following data of M/s ABC Manufacturing Ltd., prepare a Cost Sheet for the year ended 31-03-2024 and calculate the profit:

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Opening Raw Material | 50,000 |
| Purchases of Raw Material | 3,00,000 |
| Closing Raw Material | 40,000 |
| Wages (Production) | 2,00,000 |
| Salaries (Factory Supervisor) | 30,000 |
| Rent of Factory | 60,000 |
| Depreciation — Plant & Machinery | 40,000 |
| Office Salaries | 50,000 |
| Selling Expenses | 25,000 |
| Distribution Expenses | 15,000 |
| Sales (1,000 units) | 8,00,000 |
| Closing Work-in-Progress | 20,000 |
| Opening Work-in-Progress | 10,000 |
| Closing Finished Goods (200 units) | ? |

**Step 1: Direct Material Consumed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DM &= Opening\ RM + Purchases - Closing\ RM \\
&= 50{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 - 40{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Direct Labour (Wages to production workers)**

$$DL = 2{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3: Direct Expenses** = NIL (given)

**Step 4: Prime Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC &= DM + DL + DE \\
&= 3{,}10{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 0 \\
&= 5{,}10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Factory Overheads**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FOH &= Supervisor\ Salaries + Factory\ Rent + Dep.\ (Plant) \\
&= 30{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 40{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}30{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Gross Works Cost (before WIP adjustment)**

$$GWC = PC + FOH = 5{,}10{,}000 + 1{,}30{,}000 = 6{,}40{,}000$$

**Step 7: Works Cost (Factory Cost) after WIP adjustment**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WKC &= GWC + Opening\ WIP - Closing\ WIP \\
&= 6{,}40{,}000 + 10{,}000 - 20{,}000 \\
&= 6{,}30{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Cost of Production (after adding Admin OH)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP &= WKC + Office\ Salaries \\
&= 6{,}30{,}000 + 50{,}000 \\
&= 6{,}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9: Cost of Sales (after adding S&D)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COS &= COP + Selling\ Exp + Distribution\ Exp \\
&= 6{,}80{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 15{,}000 \\
&= 7{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10: Profit Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Profit &= Sales - COS \\
&= 8{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}20{,}000 \\
&= 80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by Unit Cost (KTU Examiner Tip):** The examiner may ask for *per unit cost*. Compute $\text{Cost per unit} = COS \div \text{Units Sold} = 7{,}20{,}000 \div 1{,}000 = ₹720$. Then $\text{Selling Price} = 8{,}00{,}000 \div 1{,}000 = ₹800$. Profit per unit = ₹80 ✓ (matches step 10).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Cost of Sales (Alternate Path)

For students who confuse whether to add Opening/Closing FG stock:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost\ of\ Sales &= Cost\ of\ Production \\
&\quad + Opening\ Finished\ Goods - Closing\ Finished\ Goods \\
&\quad + Selling\ \&\ Distribution\ Overheads
\end{aligned}
$$

In the above problem, since Closing FG is not given, we assume the cost of production equals the cost of goods available, and we simply add S&D to get Cost of Sales.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Pattern (Mandatory Steps in Cost Sheet):**
> Step 1: Direct Material Consumed
> Step 2: Direct Labour
> Step 3: Direct Expenses
> Step 4: Prime Cost
> Step 5: Factory Overheads
> Step 6: Works Cost (with WIP adjustment)
> Step 7: Office Overheads
> Step 8: Cost of Production (with Finished Stock adjustment)
> Step 9: Selling & Distribution Overheads
> Step 10: Cost of Sales
> Step 11: Profit (Sales − Cost of Sales)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cost Build-Up Flow Diagram (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the flow of cost elements from raw material procurement to the final **Cost of Sales**, with overheads injected at each functional stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Stock] --> B[Direct Material]
    C[Production Workers] --> D[Direct Labour]
    E[Special Job Hire Royalty] --> F[Direct Expenses]
    B --> G[PRIME COST]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[ADD Factory Overheads]
    H --> I[Factory Indirect Material]
    H --> J[Factory Indirect Labour]
    H --> K[Factory Indirect Expenses]
    I --> L[WORKS COST]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[ADD Office Overheads]
    M --> N[COST OF PRODUCTION]
    N --> O[ADD Selling Overheads]
    N --> P[ADD Distribution Overheads]
    O --> Q[COST OF SALES]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[MINUS Sales Value = PROFIT OR LOSS]
```

---

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Direct vs Indirect

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Elements of Cost] --> S[Material]
    R --> T[Labour]
    R --> U[Expenses]
    S --> V[Direct Material Raw components]
    S --> W[Indirect Material Consumables Stores]
    T --> X[Direct Labour Operators Wages]
    T --> Y[Indirect Labour Supervisor Foreman Salaries]
    U --> Z[Direct Expenses Royalty Hire Charges Special Patterns]
    U --> AA[Indirect Expenses Overheads]
    AA --> AB[Factory Manufacturing Overheads]
    AA --> AC[Office Administrative Overheads]
    AA --> AD[Selling and Distribution Overheads]
```

---

## 4.3 Cost Sheet Functional Architecture (Textual Schematic)

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  COST SHEET OF M/s ABC MANUFACTURING LTD. (2023-24)    │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Particulars           │   Debit (₹)  │   Credit (₹)  │
├────────────────────────┼──────────────┼───────────────┤
│  To Raw Material (Op)  │     50,000   │               │
│  To Purchases          │    300,000   │               │
│      Less: Returns     │       —      │               │
│      Less: Cl. Stock   │     40,000   │               │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────     │
│  To Direct Material    │    310,000   │               │
│  To Direct Labour      │    200,000   │               │
│  To Direct Expenses    │          0   │               │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────     │
│  **PRIME COST**        │ **510,000**  │               │
│  + Factory Overheads   │    130,000   │               │
│  + Op. WIP             │     10,000   │               │
│  − Cl. WIP             │     20,000   │               │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────     │
│  **WORKS COST**        │ **630,000**  │               │
│  + Office Overheads    │     50,000   │               │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────     │
│  **COST OF PRODUCTION**| **680,000**  │               │
│  + Selling OH          │     25,000   │               │
│  + Distribution OH     │     15,000   │               │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────     │
│  **COST OF SALES**     │ **720,000**  │               │
│  By Sales              │              │     800,000   │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────     │
│  **PROFIT**            │     80,000   │               │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Significance for KTU Exam:** Always present the **Cost Sheet in vertical format** (as above) with clear sub-totals. Examiners award 2 marks in a 14-mark question purely for **layout and presentation**. The double-rule between sub-totals and clear segregation of Overheads into three categories is a winning strategy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define the term "Elements of Cost". List the three elements and give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The three **Elements of Cost** are the fundamental categories of expenditure incurred in producing a product, as per the KTU 2024 Cost Accounting syllabus:
>
> 1. **Material** — Physical inputs used in production. *Example: Timber used in furniture manufacturing.*
> 2. **Labour** — Human effort (manual or mental) used in conversion. *Example: Wages paid to a machine operator in a CNC workshop.*
> 3. **Expenses** — All other costs incurred besides material and labour. *Example: Factory rent and depreciation of plant & machinery.*
>
> Each of these is further classified as **Direct** (traceable to a specific product) or **Indirect** (overheads).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Direct Material and Indirect Material with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> | Basis | Direct Material | Indirect Material |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Traceability** | Easily traceable to a specific product | Cannot be conveniently traced |
> | **Use** | Becomes part of the finished product | Supports production; not part of main body |
> | **Example 1** | Steel used in a car body | Lubricant oil for machines |
> | **Example 2** | Cotton in shirt manufacturing | Cotton waste / sandpaper |
>
> (1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for examples = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the three elements of cost and their sub-classification into Direct and Indirect categories. (7 Marks)

**(b)** From the following particulars, prepare a **Cost Sheet** and calculate the **Profit per unit** for 500 units produced and sold at ₹300 each. (7 Marks)

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Opening Stock of Raw Material | 25,000 |
| Purchases of Raw Material | 1,75,000 |
| Closing Stock of Raw Material | 15,000 |
| Wages (Direct) | 80,000 |
| Factory Expenses | 35,000 |
| Office Expenses | 20,000 |
| Selling Expenses | 12,000 |
| Distribution Expenses | 8,000 |

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Direct Material Consumed**

$$DM = 25{,}000 + 1{,}75{,}000 - 15{,}000 = 1{,}85{,}000$$

**Step 2: Prime Cost** (no direct expenses given)

$$PC = 1{,}85{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 0 = 2{,}65{,}000$$

**Step 3: Works Cost (Factory Cost)**

$$WKC = 2{,}65{,}000 + 35{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4: Cost of Production**

$$COP = 3{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000 = 3{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 5: Cost of Sales**

$$COS = 3{,}20{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 8{,}000 = 3{,}40{,}000$$

**Step 6: Sales and Profit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Total\ Sales &= 500 \times 300 = 1{,}50{,}000 \\
Loss &= 1{,}50{,}000 - 3{,}40{,}000 \\
&= (1{,}90{,}000)\ Loss
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Many students forget to **subtract Closing Stock of Raw Material**. This results in inflated Direct Material and an overestimated Cost of Production. (Lose 1 mark)
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **Selling Expenses with Office Expenses**. Remember: Office overheads are added to get **Cost of Production**, while Selling and Distribution overheads are added afterwards to get **Cost of Sales**. (Lose 2 marks)
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to multiply 500 units with ₹300 to get Total Sales before comparing with Cost of Sales. Some students compute only per-unit cost and stop. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Not providing the **Cost Sheet in proper tabular format**. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for poor presentation. Always use double rules for sub-totals.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the difference between **Factory Cost, Cost of Production, and Cost of Sales**. How is each one derived? (7 Marks)

**(b)** The following data relates to M/s XYZ Industries for January 2024. Calculate **(i) Prime Cost, (ii) Works Cost, (iii) Cost of Production, and (iv) Profit** if goods are sold at a profit of 25% on Cost. (7 Marks)

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Raw Material Consumed | 1,20,000 |
| Direct Wages | 80,000 |
| Factory Overheads | 60,000 |
| Office Overheads | 25,000 |
| Selling & Distribution Overheads | 15,000 |
| Sales Units | 1,000 |

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Prime Cost**

$$PC = 1{,}20{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 0 = 2{,}00{,}000$$

**(ii) Works Cost (Factory Cost)**

$$WKC = 2{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 2{,}60{,}000$$

**(iii) Cost of Production**

$$COP = 2{,}60{,}000 + 25{,}000 = 2{,}85{,}000$$

**(iv) Cost of Sales and Sales Value**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost\ of\ Sales &= 2{,}85{,}000 + 15{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \\
Per\ Unit\ COS &= 3{,}00{,}000 \div 1{,}000 = ₹300 \\
Profit\ (25\%\ on\ Cost) &= 25\%\ of\ 300 = ₹75 \\
Selling\ Price\ per\ Unit &= 300 + 75 = ₹375 \\
Total\ Sales &= 1{,}000 \times 375 = 3{,}75{,}000 \\
Total\ Profit &= 3{,}75{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = ₹75{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Treating "25% profit on cost" as "25% profit on sales". Read the question carefully. Profit on Cost = 25% of Cost of Sales. Profit on Sales = 25% of Selling Price. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Writing "Sales = ₹3,00,000" because students confuse **Cost of Sales** with **Sales Value**. Always clarify in your answer. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Skipping the per-unit calculation. KTU 2024 pattern demands **per-unit cost AND total cost** both to be shown. (Lose 1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] The **three elements of cost** are: **Material, Labour, Expenses**.
- [x] Each element is classified as **Direct** (traceable) or **Indirect** (overheads).
- [x] **Direct Material** + **Direct Labour** + **Direct Expenses** = **Prime Cost**.
- [x] **Prime Cost** + **Factory Overheads** = **Works Cost (Factory Cost)**.
- [x] **Works Cost** + **Office Overheads** = **Cost of Production**.
- [x] **Cost of Production** + **Selling & Distribution Overheads** = **Cost of Sales**.
- [x] **Sales − Cost of Sales = Profit (or Loss)**.
- [x] **Raw Material Stock** is valued at material cost; **WIP** at factory cost; **Finished Goods** at Cost of Production.
- [x] **Overtime premium** and **Idle time wages** are normally treated as **Indirect Labour (Factory Overhead)** unless caused by a specific customer.
- [x] **Hire charges of special equipment for a specific job** = **Direct Expense**.
- [x] **Carriage outwards** = Selling Overhead. **Carriage inwards** = Material cost.
- [x] Always present the **Cost Sheet in vertical tabular format** with double rules for sub-totals.
- [x] **KTU 2024 Board Favourite Sub-Question:** "Distinguish between Cost of Production and Cost of Sales" — Answer: Cost of Production ends at factory; Cost of Sales includes all costs up to the customer's doorstep.
- [x] **Quick Memory Trick:** **"P-W-O-C-S"** = **P**rime, **W**orks, **C**ost of Production, **C**ost of **S**ales — read the cost sheet as P → W → C → S.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip:**
> In a 14-mark cost sheet question, the examiner awards marks in the following ratio:
> - **5 marks** for correct identification of Direct Material, Direct Labour, Direct Expenses
> - **3 marks** for Prime Cost + Factory Cost calculation
> - **3 marks** for Cost of Production + Cost of Sales
> - **2 marks** for Profit/Loss
> - **1 mark** for proper **presentation and layout**
> Skipping a single line (e.g., not showing WIP adjustment or finished goods adjustment) can cost you 2-3 marks even if the arithmetic is correct. Always prepare a **fully-labeled, complete cost sheet**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT523 - FINANCIAL AND COST ACCOUNTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cost Behaviour # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cost Behaviour** is the study of how a cost (an expense incurred by an organization) reacts or changes in total with respect to fluctuations in the underlying **level of business activity** (commonly measured in units produced, units sold, labour hours worked, or machine hours run). In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, **Cost Behaviour** is a foundational concept in **Cost Accounting & Management** that classifies costs based on their **functional response** to changes in the **Activity Volume (x)**. The activity driver is normally taken as **production output, sales volume, labour hours, or machine hours**. Mathematically, the total cost function is represented as: $$C(x) = F + V(x)$$ Where: - $C(x)$ = Total Cost - $F$ = Fixed Component (constant) - $V(x)$ = Variable Component (function of activity $x$) > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Module 4 learning outcome tests the student's ability to *classify costs by behaviour*, *separate mixed costs into fixed and variable elements*, and *apply analytical methods (High-Low and Least Squares) to determine cost equations* for decision-making and budgeting. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The House Rent + Electricity Bill" Model > [!TIP] > Imagine a student living in a rented flat. **House Rent** is **₹8,000/month** — whether they use 1 day of the month or all 30 days, the rent doesn't change. This is a **Fixed Cost**. On the other hand, **Electricity Bill** depends on units consumed — the more they use AC, fans, and lights, the higher the bill. This is a **Variable Cost**. The actual electricity bill, however, has a small fixed meter charge + per-unit charge, making it a **Semi-Variable Cost**. **Mapping the analogy to cost accounting:** | Real-life Element | Cost Accounting Equivalent | Behaviour Type | |---|---|---| | House Rent | Factory Rent, Manager Salary | Fixed Cost | | Electricity Units Consumed | Direct Materials, Direct Labour | Variable Cost | | Electricity Bill (Fixed Charge + Units) | Telephone Bill, Repairs | Semi-Variable (Mixed) Cost | | Hiring a new security guard for every 100 extra visitors | Supervisor for every shift | Step-Fixed Cost | This simple mental model is exactly how KTU examiners expect students to demonstrate intuitive clarity in their answers. ## 1.3 Why Cost Behaviour Matters Understanding cost behaviour empowers a manager to: - **Predict** future costs at different activity levels (Forecasting). - **Budget** accurately for varying production volumes. - **Perform Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis** — a cornerstone of managerial economics. - **Make Pricing Decisions** with knowledge of marginal cost contributions. - **Identify Break-Even Points** for new products or divisions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiners' Hot-Question Theme:** Almost every Module 4 question directly tests a student's ability to **separate semi-variable costs** into their **Fixed** and **Variable** components and then **formulate a cost equation** of the form $y = a + bx$. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Costs by Behaviour ### 2.1.1 Fixed Costs (FC) **Definition:** Costs that remain **constant in total** over a **relevant range** of activity, regardless of changes in output. **Logic Steps:** - Fixed costs do **not** vary with production volume in total amount. - However, the **per-unit fixed cost** *decreases* as volume increases (since the same fixed amount is spread over more units). - They exist only within a **Relevant Range**. If activity drops below the range, costs may be cut (e.g., closing a factory). - They are **period costs** — incurred by the passage of time, not by production. **Examples:** Factory rent, depreciation (straight-line), salary of permanent staff, insurance premium, property tax. ### 2.1.2 Variable Costs (VC) **Definition:** Costs that vary **directly and proportionately** with the level of activity. **Logic Steps:** - Total variable cost = 0 when output = 0. - If output doubles, total variable cost doubles. - **Per-unit variable cost remains constant** — this is a key feature distinguishing them from fixed costs. - They are **product costs** in nature (consumed by production). **Examples:** Direct materials, direct labour (piece-rate), commission on sales, packing cost, freight outwards. ### 2.1.3 Semi-Variable (Mixed) Costs **Definition:** Costs that contain **both a fixed and a variable element** — change with activity but not proportionately. **Logic Steps:** - Even at zero output, a minimum charge exists (the **fixed portion**). - Beyond the minimum, the cost rises with output (the **variable portion**). - The graphical representation is a **straight line with a positive y-intercept** (non-zero starting point). - They must be **separated** into fixed and variable elements for planning purposes. **Examples:** Electricity bill, telephone bill, depreciation (written-down value method), factory manager's salary with productivity bonus, repair and maintenance. ### 2.1.4 Step-Fixed (Step) Costs **Definition:** Costs that remain **fixed over a narrow range** of activity but **jump to a new level** once a threshold is crossed. **Logic Steps:** - Step costs are **discontinuous** — they form a staircase pattern. - They arise from adding a whole new resource (a supervisor, a shift, a machine) when activity crosses a certain limit. - Sometimes called **"step-variable"** costs when the step width is narrow. **Examples:** Hire of a supervisor for every 50 workers; salary of additional accountant per ₹10 lakh of revenue. ### 2.1.5 Curvilinear (Non-Linear) Costs > [!NOTE] > Although KTU Module 4 primarily focuses on **linear** cost behaviour (assumed for analytical convenience), real-world costs often display a **curvilinear** pattern — convex (economies of scale), concave (diseconomies of scale), or S-shaped (learning curve effects). These are typically approximated by piecewise linear segments in practice. ## 2.2 Methods of Separating Semi-Variable (Mixed) Costs The three canonical methods taught in PECBT523 are: ### A. High-Low Method (Most Common in KTU Papers) - Select the **highest** and **lowest** activity periods. - Compute the **Variable Cost per unit (b)** as the slope of the line connecting these two points. - Compute the **Fixed Cost (a)** by subtracting variable cost from total cost at either point. ### B. Scattergraph Method - Plot all data points on a graph and visually fit a straight line. - Subjective, but useful for spotting outliers. ### C. Least Squares (Regression) Method - Statistically optimal method that minimises the sum of squared deviations. - Gives the **best-fitting straight line** through all observations. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they cover ~90% of all KTU 2024 Module 4 numerical problems.** | # | Concept | Formula | Notation | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total Cost Equation | $C(x) = a + b \cdot x$ | $C$ = Total cost, $a$ = Fixed, $b$ = Var/unit, $x$ = activity | ₹ | | 2 | Variable Cost per unit | $b = \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$ | $\Delta C$ = change in cost, $\Delta x$ = change in activity | ₹/unit | | 3 | High-Low Variable Cost | $b = \dfrac{C_{H} - C_{L}}{x_{H} - x_{L}}$ | Subscripts $H$ = high, $L$ = low | ₹/unit | | 4 | High-Low Fixed Cost | $a = C_{H} - b \cdot x_{H}$ | Same as above | ₹ | | 5 | Least Squares Slope | $b = \dfrac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum x^{2} - (\sum x)^{2}}$ | $n$ = no. of periods | ₹/unit | | 6 | Least Squares Intercept | $a = \dfrac{\sum y - b \sum x}{n} = \bar{y} - b \bar{x}$ | $\bar{y}, \bar{x}$ = means | ₹ | | 7 | Correlation Coefficient | $r = \dfrac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{\sqrt{[n\sum x^{2}-(\sum x)^{2}][n\sum y^{2}-(\sum y)^{2}]}}$ | $-1 \le r \le 1$ | Dimensionless | | 8 | Break-Even Volume | $x_{BE} = \dfrac{a}{p - b}$ | $p$ = selling price/unit | units | | 9 | Profit Function | $P(x) = (p - b)x - a$ | $P$ = profit | ₹ | | 10 | Contribution Margin | $CM = p - b$ | $p$ = price, $b$ = var cost | ₹/unit | | 11 | Margin of Safety (MoS) | $MoS = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BE Sales}$ | Both in ₹ or units | ₹ / units | | 12 | P/V Ratio | $\text{P/V Ratio} = \dfrac{CM}{p} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}}$ | As a fraction or % | % | | 13 | Fixed Cost per unit | $\text{Fixed cost/unit} = \dfrac{a}{x}$ | Spreads over activity | ₹/unit | | 14 | Variable Cost per unit | $\text{VC/unit} = b$ | Constant by definition | ₹/unit | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In real engineering and industrial contexts, understanding cost behaviour is essential for: - **Product Pricing & Tendering:** A civil engineering firm bidding on a road project must know how fuel and concrete costs (variable) will scale with project length, while their head-office rent (fixed) stays constant. - **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** Knowing the variable cost of in-house production versus external supplier costs. - **Capacity Planning:** Identifying the relevant range within which current fixed cost structure remains valid before expansion triggers new fixed costs (step costs). - **Performance Evaluation:** Variance analysis between budgeted (flexed) costs and actual costs relies entirely on a correct cost equation. > [!TIP] > **Engineering tie-in (PECBT context):** When a B.Tech student later becomes a project manager, the **"Relevant Range"** concept is critical — beyond a certain order volume, an entire new production line (step-fixed cost) must be added, fundamentally shifting the cost function. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivations of the Core Cost Equations ### 3.1.1 Deriving the Variable Cost Rate (b) — High-Low Method We start with the linear cost assumption: $$C(x) = a + b \cdot x$$ Apply this to two distinct data points: the **High activity point** $(x_{H}, C_{H})$ and the **Low activity point** $(x_{L}, C_{L})$. At the high point: $$C_{H} = a + b \cdot x_{H} \quad \text{...(1)}$$ At the low point: $$C_{L} = a + b \cdot x_{L} \quad \text{...(2)}$$ Subtracting (2) from (1) eliminates the fixed cost $a$ (since $a$ is constant): $$C_{H} - C_{L} = b \cdot x_{H} - b \cdot x_{L}$$ Factor out $b$: $$C_{H} - C_{L} = b (x_{H} - x_{L})$$ Solve for $b$: $$b = \dfrac{C_{H} - C_{L}}{x_{H} - x_{L}}$$ **Interpretation:** The variable cost per unit is the **slope** of the line passing through the high and low points. It represents how much the cost increases per additional unit of activity. Now solve for $a$ by substituting $b$ back into equation (1): $$a = C_{H} - b \cdot x_{H}$$ ### 3.1.2 Deriving the Least Squares Regression Coefficients The least squares method minimises the sum of squared vertical deviations: $$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_{i} - a - b \cdot x_{i})^{2}$$ To minimise, take partial derivatives with respect to $a$ and $b$ and set them to zero. **Step 1:** Partial derivative w.r.t. $a$: $$\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial a} = -2 \sum (y_{i} - a - b x_{i}) = 0$$ This simplifies to the **normal equation**: $$\sum y_{i} = n \cdot a + b \sum x_{i} \quad \text{...(A)}$$ **Step 2:** Partial derivative w.r.t. $b$: $$\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial b} = -2 \sum x_{i} (y_{i} - a - b x_{i}) = 0$$ This yields the second **normal equation**: $$\sum x_{i} y_{i} = a \sum x_{i} + b \sum x_{i}^{2} \quad \text{...(B)}$$ **Step 3:** Solve equations (A) and (B) simultaneously. From (A), solve for $a$: $$a = \dfrac{\sum y - b \sum x}{n}$$ Substitute this into (B): $$\sum xy = \left( \dfrac{\sum y - b \sum x}{n} \right) \sum x + b \sum x^{2}$$ Multiply through by $n$: $$n \sum xy = \sum x \sum y - b (\sum x)^{2} + b \cdot n \sum x^{2}$$ Collect the $b$ terms: $$n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y = b \left[ n \sum x^{2} - (\sum x)^{2} \right]$$ Therefore: $$b = \dfrac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum x^{2} - (\sum x)^{2}}$$ And the intercept: $$a = \dfrac{\sum y - b \sum x}{n}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why "Least Squares"?** By squaring the deviations, the method penalises large errors heavily and ensures the fitted line is the *unique* straight line that has the minimum total squared error from the actual data points. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked-Out Examples ### Example 1: High-Low Method > A factory has the following semi-variable overhead data: > - **March:** Activity = 4,000 units, Total Overhead = ₹40,000 > - **April:** Activity = 6,000 units, Total Overhead = ₹52,000 > - **May:** Activity = 5,000 units, Total Overhead = ₹46,000 > - **June:** Activity = 3,000 units, Total Overhead = ₹34,000 > > **Required:** Determine the cost equation using the High-Low Method. Estimate the cost for a planned activity of **8,000 units**. **Step 1 — Identify High and Low activity:** - Highest activity: **April** → $x_{H} = 6{,}000$ units, $C_{H} = ₹52{,}000$ - Lowest activity: **June** → $x_{L} = 3{,}000$ units, $C_{L} = ₹34{,}000$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Always pick the **HIGHEST and LOWEST ACTIVITY LEVELS**, not the highest/lowest *cost*! Many students erroneously use cost values, leading to wrong answers. **Step 2 — Compute Variable Cost per unit ($b$):** $$b = \dfrac{C_{H} - C_{L}}{x_{H} - x_{L}} = \dfrac{52{,}000 - 34{,}000}{6{,}000 - 3{,}000} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{3{,}000} = ₹6 \text{ per unit}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Fixed Cost ($a$):** Using the high point: $$a = C_{H} - b \cdot x_{H} = 52{,}000 - (6 \times 6{,}000) = 52{,}000 - 36{,}000 = ₹16{,}000$$ Verify using the low point: $$a = C_{L} - b \cdot x_{L} = 34{,}000 - (6 \times 3{,}000) = 34{,}000 - 18{,}000 = ₹16{,}000 \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Formulate the Cost Equation:** $$C(x) = 16{,}000 + 6x$$ **Step 5 — Estimate cost for 8,000 units:** $$C(8{,}000) = 16{,}000 + 6 \times 8{,}000 = 16{,}000 + 48{,}000 = ₹64{,}000$$ ### Example 2: Least Squares Method (Comprehensive) > The semi-variable overhead data for the last 6 months of a firm are: > > | Month | Machine Hours ($x$) | Total Overhead (₹) ($y$) | > |---|---|---| > | Jan | 1,000 | 25,000 | > | Feb | 1,200 | 28,000 | > | Mar | 1,500 | 33,000 | > | Apr | 1,400 | 31,000 | > | May | 1,800 | 37,000 | > | Jun | 1,100 | 26,000 | > > **Required:** Use the Least Squares Method to find the cost equation. **Step 1 — Build the calculation table:** | Month | $x$ | $y$ | $xy$ | $x^{2}$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | Jan | 1,000 | 25,000 | 25,000,000 | 1,000,000 | | Feb | 1,200 | 28,000 | 33,600,000 | 1,440,000 | | Mar | 1,500 | 33,000 | 49,500,000 | 2,250,000 | | Apr | 1,400 | 31,000 | 43,400,000 | 1,960,000 | | May | 1,800 | 37,000 | 66,600,000 | 3,240,000 | | Jun | 1,100 | 26,000 | 28,600,000 | 1,210,000 | | **Sum** | **8,000** | **1,80,000** | **2,46,70,000** | **11,100,000** | Thus: $n = 6$, $\sum x = 8{,}000$, $\sum y = 1{,}80{,}000$, $\sum xy = 2{,}46{,}70{,}000$, $\sum x^{2} = 11{,}100{,}000$. **Step 2 — Calculate Slope $b$:** $$b = \dfrac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum x^{2} - (\sum x)^{2}}$$ $$b = \dfrac{6(2{,}46{,}70{,}000) - (8{,}000)(1{,}80{,}000)}{6(11{,}100{,}000) - (8{,}000)^{2}}$$ $$b = \dfrac{14{,}80{,20{,}000 - 14{,}40{,}00{,}000}{66{,}60{,}000 - 64{,}00{,}000}$$ $$b = \dfrac{40{,}20{,}000}{2{,}60{,}000} = ₹15.46 \text{ per hour (approx.)}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate Intercept $a$:** $$a = \dfrac{\sum y - b \sum x}{n} = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000 - (15.46)(8{,}000)}{6}$$ $$a = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}23{,}680}{6} = \dfrac{56{,}320}{6} \approx ₹9{,}387$$ **Step 4 — Cost Equation:** $$C(x) = 9{,}387 + 15.46 \cdot x$$ **Step 5 — Verify by computing a sample prediction:** For $x = 1{,}200$ hours (Feb): $$C(1{,}200) = 9{,}387 + 15.46 \times 1{,}200 = 9{,}387 + 18{,}552 = ₹27{,}939$$ Actual: ₹28,000 → Error is minimal ✓ ### Example 3: Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Tie-In > A company sells a product at ₹50/unit. Variable cost is ₹30/unit and fixed cost is ₹40,000. **Required:** Find Break-Even point in units and sales ₹. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute Contribution Margin per unit:** $$CM = p - b = 50 - 30 = ₹20 \text{ per unit}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Break-Even Volume:** $$x_{BE} = \dfrac{a}{p - b} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{20} = 2{,}000 \text{ units}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Break-Even Sales (₹):** $$\text{BE Sales} = x_{BE} \times p = 2{,}000 \times 50 = ₹1{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 4 — Compute P/V Ratio:** $$\text{P/V Ratio} = \dfrac{CM}{p} = \dfrac{20}{50} = 0.40 \text{ or } 40\%$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Tip:** A 40% P/V ratio means every ₹100 of sales contributes ₹40 towards covering fixed costs and generating profit — a critical number for management decisions. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Classification of Costs by Behaviour ```mermaid graph TD A[Cost Behaviour] --> B[Fixed Cost] A --> C[Variable Cost] A --> D[Semi-Variable Cost] A --> E[Step-Fixed Cost] A --> F[Curvilinear Cost] B --> B1["Constant in total

DECREASES with volume"] C --> C1["Varies in total

approximated linearly"] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style C fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000 style D fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000 style E fill:#f4b084,stroke:#000 style F fill:#c9c9c9,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Cost-Behaviour Patterns (Schematic Block Architecture) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph BEHAVIOUR_TYPES P1[Fixed Cost Pattern]:::fc P2[Variable Cost Pattern]:::vc P3[Semi-Variable Pattern]:::sv P4[Step-Fixed Pattern]:::sf end A1[Activity Volume Increases] --> P1 A1 --> P2 A1 --> P3 A1 --> P4 P1 --> R1["Total Cost: HORIZONTAL LINE
