# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between Cost Allocation and Cost Apportionment with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

**Cost Allocation** is the process of charging the **whole** of an identifiable cost to a **single** cost centre or cost unit. The expense has a direct, exclusive relationship with one department.
- *Example:* The salary of the foreman of the Assembly Department is allocated in full to the Assembly Department.

**Cost Apportionment** is the process of distributing common costs over **two or more** cost centres in proportion to the estimated benefit each receives.
- *Example:* The rent of a factory building (₹40,000) is apportioned among departments based on floor area occupied.

> **Key Distinction:** Allocation = One-to-One (Whole charge). Apportionment = One-to-Many (Shared charge).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State any THREE differences between the Direct Method and the Step Method of overhead apportionment.*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Direct Method | Step Method |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Inter-Service Flow** | Ignored completely | Considered partially (one-way) |
| 2. **Service Department Ranking** | Not required | Required (highest service giver distributed first) |
| 3. **Accuracy** | Least accurate | More accurate than Direct Method |
| 4. **Complexity** | Very simple | Moderately complex |
| 5. **Re-distribution** | Service dept costs go only to production depts | First service dept's cost is distributed to all remaining (incl. other service) |

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Question]**
>
> A factory has **two production departments (A and B)** and **two service departments (X and Y)**. The following are the expenses for the year ended 31st March 2024:

| Particulars | Total (₹) |
|---|---:|
| Indirect Material | 20,000 |
| Indirect Labour | 30,000 |
| Rent & Taxes | 24,000 |
| Power | 18,000 |
| Depreciation of Machinery | 16,000 |
| Insurance of Building | 12,000 |
| Sundry Expenses | 8,000 |

**Additional Data:**

| Particulars | A | B | X | Y |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Floor Area (sq. ft) | 2,000 | 3,000 | 1,000 | 1,000 |
| Machine Hours | 6,000 | 4,000 | — | — |
| Light Points | 30 | 20 | 10 | 10 |
| Direct Wages (₹) | 40,000 | 30,000 | 10,000 | 5,000 |

> **(a)** Prepare a statement showing the **apportionment of overheads using the Step Method**. Distribute service department costs in the ratio of their direct wages. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Compute the **total cost and cost per unit** for Department A and B, given that direct material consumed is ₹1,00,000 in A and ₹80,000 in B, and units produced are 1,000 in A and 500 in B. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a): Step Method Apportionment**

**Step 1 — Total Overheads: ₹1,28,000**

**Step 2 — Identify and Apportion Common Overheads:**

- **Rent & Taxes (₹24,000) — Floor Area basis (7,000 sq. ft total)**
  - Rate = ₹24,000 / 7,000 ≈ ₹3.4286 per sq. ft
  - A = 2,000 × 3.4286 = ₹6,857
  - B = 3,000 × 3.4286 = ₹10,286
  - X = 1,000 × 3.4286 = ₹3,429
  - Y = 1,000 × 3.4286 = ₹3,429

- **Power (₹18,000) — Machine Hours basis (A:6,000, B:4,000, no X/Y consumption assumed)**
  - A = 18,000 × 6/10 = ₹10,800
  - B = 18,000 × 4/10 = ₹7,200

- **Insurance of Building (₹12,000) — Floor Area basis**
  - A = 12,000 × 2/7 = ₹3,429
  - B = 12,000 × 3/7 = ₹5,143
  - X = 12,000 × 1/7 = ₹1,714
  - Y = 12,000 × 1/7 = ₹1,714

- **Sundry Expenses (₹8,000) — apportioned by direct wages (85,000 total)**
  - A = 8,000 × 40/85 = ₹3,765
  - B = 8,000 × 30/85 = ₹2,824
  - X = 8,000 × 10/85 = ₹941
  - Y = 8,000 × 5/85 = ₹471

**Indirect Material, Indirect Labour, Depreciation** — already directly given to A, B, X, Y (as per problem). Assume allocation as in Step 2 below.

**Step 3 — Initial Sub-Totals:**

| Overhead | A (₹) | B (₹) | X (₹) | Y (₹) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Indirect Material | 8,000 | 7,000 | 2,500 | 2,500 |
| Indirect Labour | 12,000 | 10,000 | 5,000 | 3,000 |
| Rent & Taxes | 6,857 | 10,286 | 3,429 | 3,429 |
| Power | 10,800 | 7,200 | — | — |
| Depreciation | 6,000 | 6,000 | 2,000 | 2,000 |
| Insurance | 3,429 | 5,143 | 1,714 | 1,714 |
| Sundry | 3,765 | 2,824 | 941 | 471 |
| **Sub-Total** | **50,851** | **48,453** | **15,584** | **13,114** |

**Step 4 — Re-apportion Service Dept X first (ratio of direct wages: A:40, B:30, Y:5 → 40:30:5)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X\text{ distributed} &= 15{,}584 \\
A &= 15{,}584 \times \frac{40}{75} = \text{₹8,311} \\
B &= 15{,}584 \times \frac{30}{75} = \text{₹6,234} \\
Y &= 15{,}584 \times \frac{5}{75} = \text{₹1,039}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Update Y and re-distribute to A and B only (ratio 40:30)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Updated Y} &= 13{,}114 + 1{,}039 = \text{₹14,153} \\
A &= 14{,}153 \times \frac{40}{70} = \text{₹8,087} \\
B &= 14{,}153 \times \frac{30}{70} = \text{₹6,066}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Departmental Overheads (Step Method)**

| Department | Final Overheads (₹) |
|---|---:|
| A | 50,851 + 8,311 + 8,087 = **₹67,249** |
| B | 48,453 + 6,234 + 6,066 = **₹60,753** |

**Part (b): Total Cost and Cost per Unit**

| Particulars | A (₹) | B (₹) |
|---|---:|---:|
| Direct Material | 1,00,000 | 80,000 |
| Direct Wages | 40,000 | 30,000 |
| Overheads (from Part a) | 67,249 | 60,753 |
| **Total Cost** | **2,07,249** | **1,70,753** |
| Units Produced | 1,000 | 500 |
| **Cost per Unit** | **₹207.25** | **₹341.51** |

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Question]**
>
> M/s Kerala Steel Castings Ltd. provides the following information for the month of April 2024:
>
> - Opening Stock of Raw Material: ₹60,000
> - Purchases of Raw Material: ₹4,80,000
> - Closing Stock of Raw Material: ₹50,000
> - Carriage Inward: ₹10,000
> - Wages (Direct): ₹3,60,000
> - Indirect Wages: ₹40,000
> - Factory Rent & Rates: ₹30,000
> - Factory Power: ₹25,000
> - Depreciation on Plant: ₹15,000
> - Office Salaries: ₹30,000
> - Office Rent: ₹10,000
> - Selling Expenses: ₹20,000
> - Commission to Salesmen: ₹10,000
> - Production during the month: 5,000 units
> - Sales: 4,500 units @ ₹200 each
> - Closing Stock of Finished Goods: to be valued at cost
>
> **(a)** Prepare a detailed **Cost Sheet** showing Prime Cost, Works Cost, Cost of Production, Cost of Sales, and Net Profit. **(10 Marks)**
> **(b)** Calculate the **Total Cost per Unit** and the **Profit Percentage on Cost and Sales**. **(4 Marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — Cost Sheet**

| S. No. | Particulars | Amount (₹) | Total (₹) |
|---:|---|---:|---:|
| 1 | Opening Stock of Raw Material | 60,000 | |
| 2 | Add: Purchases of Raw Material | 4,80,000 | |
| 3 | Less: Closing Stock of Raw Material | (50,000) | |
| 4 | **Raw Material Consumed** | | 4,90,000 |
| 5 | Add: Carriage Inward | | 10,000 |
| 6 | Add: Direct Wages | | 3,60,000 |
| 7 | **PRIME COST** | | **8,60,000** |
| 8 | Add: Indirect Wages | 40,000 | |
| 9 | Add: Factory Rent & Rates | 30,000 | |
| 10 | Add: Factory Power | 25,000 | |
| 11 | Add: Depreciation on Plant | 15,000 | |
| 12 | **Total Factory Overheads** | 1,10,000 | 1,10,000 |
| 13 | **WORKS / FACTORY COST** | | **9,70,000** |
| 14 | Add: Office Salaries | 30,000 | |
| 15 | Add: Office Rent | 10,000 | |
| 16 | **Administration Overheads** | 40,000 | 40,000 |
| 17 | **COST OF PRODUCTION** | | **10,10,000** |
| 18 | Add: Opening Stock of FG (assumed NIL) | | 0 |
| 19 | Less: Closing Stock of FG (1,000 units × ₹202) | | (2,02,000) |
| 20 | **COST OF GOODS SOLD** | | **8,08,000** |
| 21 | Add: Selling Expenses | 20,000 | |
| 22 | Add: Commission to Salesmen | 10,000 | |
| 23 | **Selling & Distribution Overheads** | 30,000 | 30,000 |
| 24 | **COST OF SALES** | | **8,38,000** |
| 25 | Add: Net Profit (balancing figure) | | 62,000 |
| 26 | **SALES (4,500 × ₹200)** | | **9,00,000** |

**Part (b) — Per Unit Calculations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost per Unit} &= \frac{10{,}10{,}000}{5{,}000} = \text{₹202} \\
\text{Profit \% on Sales} &= \frac{62{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 6.89\% \\
\text{Profit \% on Cost} &= \frac{62{,}000}{8{,}38{,}000} \times 100 = 7.40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Closing Stock of FG** → overstates COGS and understates profit (loses 2 marks).
> 2. **Treating Office Expenses as Factory OH** → misclassifies the Works Cost and Office Cost heads (loses 1–2 marks).
> 3. **Not showing the Closing Stock of Raw Material as a deduction** in the material section (loses 1 mark).
> 4. **Forgetting to convert Profit % on Cost and Profit % on Sales** clearly — KTU asks both (loses 1 mark).
> 5. **In Step Method problems, re-distributing cost BACK to an already-cleared service department** → fundamental error (loses 2–3 marks).
> 6. **In Reciprocal Method problems, not solving the simultaneous equation correctly** → cascading errors (loses 3–4 marks).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4**

- **Overhead accounting is a 3-stage waterfall:** Allocation → Apportionment → Absorption. Memorise the order.

- **Direct Method ignores inter-service flow** — service dept costs go straight to production depts only.

- **Step Method is one-way** — distribute the highest-service-rendering service dept first; never re-distribute back to it.

- **Reciprocal Method is two-way (most accurate)** — always set up simultaneous equations; service depts may serve each other.

- **Pre-determined OAR uses estimated figures**; difference between absorbed and actual = **over/under-absorption** (reconcile via P&L Adjustment Account).

- **Actual OAR uses actual figures** — known only at year-end → not useful for timely pricing decisions.

- **Machine Hour Rate** is used when the production is highly automated (low labour content).

- **Unit Costing formula sequence:** Prime Cost + Factory OH = Works Cost + Admin OH = Cost of Production ± Stock = COGS + Selling OH = Cost of Sales.

- **Closing Stock of RM is deducted** while computing material consumed; **Closing Stock of FG is deducted** while computing COGS.

- **Profit % on Sales = (Profit/Sales) × 100**; **Profit % on Cost = (Profit/Cost of Sales) × 100** — both are different and KTU expects both.

- **Tender Price = Estimated Cost + Profit Margin + Contingency Provision**.

- **Composite Costing** = Unit + Job + Process + Operating Costing combined (used in automobile, electronics).

- **Operating Costing** is for service industries (cost unit = per passenger-km, per tonne-km, per patient-day, per kWh).

- **For Step Method:** always update the next service dept's total before re-distributing.

- **For Reciprocal Method:** use either substitution method or matrix method (KTU accepts both).

- **Default bases to remember:** Rent → Floor Area; Power → Machine Hours or kWh; Lighting → Light Points; Insurance → Value of Assets or Floor Area; Depreciation → Book Value or Usage Hours; Canteen → Number of Employees.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT523 - FINANCIAL AND COST ACCOUNTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Job Costing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Job Costing** is a fundamental method of **Cost Accumulation** in Cost Accounting where manufacturing costs are collected, recorded, and allocated to **specific jobs**, **batches**, or **contracts** executed as per the distinct requirements of individual customers. Each job is treated as a separate cost unit, and a unique **Job Cost Sheet** is maintained for every job to ascertain its profitability.

According to the **Institute of Cost and Management Accountants (ICMA, London)**, Job Costing is defined as:

> *"That form of specific order costing which applies where work is undertaken to customer's special requirements, and each order is of a comparatively short duration."*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Job Costing forms the bedrock of **Specific Order Costing** and is widely applied in industries where production is non-repetitive, heterogeneous, and customized.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Job Costing as a "Tailor's Bill" for every individual garment.**

Imagine you walk into a tailor's shop. The tailor measures *your* body, selects fabric *specifically* for you, and stitches *your* shirt from scratch. At the end, the tailor hands you a bill listing:
- Cost of fabric used (Direct Material)
- Wages of the tailor (Direct Labor)
- Buttons, thread, lining (Direct Expenses)
- A share of the shop's rent and electricity (Overheads)

Similarly, in **Job Costing**:
- Every customer order = a **"Job"**
- The bill prepared = a **"Job Cost Sheet"**
- The total of all costs = the **"Job Cost"**

If another tailor mass-produces identical shirts in a factory, that would be **Process Costing** (the opposite approach). Job Costing is therefore the *bespoke*, *custom-made* cousin of cost accounting.

## 1.3 Key Features of Job Costing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Salient Characteristics of Job Costing (KTU High-Yield Points):**
> 1. **Each job is unique and separate** – no two jobs are identical.
> 2. **Costs are accumulated job-wise**, not period-wise.
> 3. A **separate Job Cost Sheet** is opened for each job.
> 4. **Direct costs** (materials, labor, expenses) are charged directly to the job.
> 5. **Overheads** are apportioned to jobs on a suitable basis (e.g., direct labor hours).
> 6. Jobs are undertaken as per **customer's special requirements**.
> 7. **Profit/Loss is determined job-wise**, not in aggregate.
> 8. The **Job Cost Sheet acts as the primary document** for cost control.

## 1.4 Industries Where Job Costing is Applied

| **Industry** | **Example of a "Job"** |
|---|---|
| Printing Press | Printing wedding invitations, business cards |
| Ship-building | Construction of a single ship |
| Auto Repair Workshop | Repairing one specific car |
| Interior Design | Designing a single office interior |
| Heavy Engineering | Manufacturing a custom turbine |
| Construction | Building a bridge, dam, or specific structure |
| Furniture Making | Crafting a custom dining table |

## 1.5 The Job Cost Sheet — The Heart of Job Costing

> [!NOTE]
> A **Job Cost Sheet** (also called **Job Card** or **Production Order**) is a document prepared for each job to record all costs incurred in completing that specific job. It is the **fundamental record** in the entire job costing system.

**Standard Format of a Job Cost Sheet:**

| **Particulars** | **Amount (₹)** | **Total (₹)** |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Direct Materials** (Material issued from stores) | XXX | |
| Less: Return of Materials | (XX) | XXX |
| **B. Direct Wages / Labor** | XXX | |
| Add: Overtime Premium | XX | XXX |
| **C. Direct / Chargeable Expenses** | XXX | |
| (Royalties, hire charges, special tools) | | XXX |
| **D. Prime Cost** (A + B + C) | | XXX |
| **E. Add: Factory Overheads** (apportioned) | | XXX |
| **F. Works / Factory Cost** (D + E) | | XXX |
| **G. Add: Administrative Overheads** | | XXX |
| **H. Cost of Production** (F + G) | | XXX |
| **I. Add: Selling & Distribution Overheads** | | XXX |
| **J. Total Cost / Sales Cost** (H + I) | | XXX |
| **K. Add: Profit Margin** | | XXX |
| **L. Selling Price / Job Price** | | XXX |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Total Job Cost — Deep Dive

### Step 1: Direct Material Cost
- Materials **specifically purchased** for a particular job and **directly identifiable** with it.
- Issued from stores against a **Material Requisition Note**.
- Any material returned is **credited** to the job.
- Includes both **prime cost material** and **cost of special parts** bought specifically for the job.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples:** Timber for a specific table, fabric for a tailored dress, steel plates for a boiler.

### Step 2: Direct Wages (Direct Labor Cost)
- Wages paid to workers who are **directly engaged** in manufacturing the product on the job.
- Includes **basic wages, dearness allowance, overtime premium** (only the portion attributable to the job).
- Recorded via **Time Tickets / Job Tickets** prepared daily.

### Step 3: Direct / Chargeable Expenses
- Expenses that are **incurred specifically** for a particular job and **not on a regular basis**.
- Cannot be classified under Direct Materials or Direct Labor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples:** Royalties paid on a special design, hire charges of special equipment, cost of blueprints, traveling expenses of a consultant, cost of a patent used.

### Step 4: Prime Cost
- Sum of Direct Materials + Direct Wages + Direct Expenses.
- Called **Prime Cost** because it is the **primary, foundational cost** of producing the job.

### Step 5: Overheads — The Tricky Part
- **Indirect costs** that cannot be directly traced to a single job.
- **Must be apportioned** using a logical, equitable basis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Categories of Overheads in Job Costing:**

| **Category** | **Definition** | **Apportionment Basis (Common)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Factory / Works Overheads** | Indirect costs of the factory (rent, power, depreciation, supervisor's salary) | Direct Labor Hours, Machine Hours, Direct Wages |
| **Administrative Overheads** | Office and management costs (office rent, MD's salary, stationery) | Works Cost, Direct Wages, Labor Hours |
| **Selling & Distribution Overheads** | Costs of selling and delivering the product | Total Cost, Works Cost |

### Step 6: Total Cost
- Prime Cost + All Three Overheads = **Total Cost / Sales Cost of the Job**.

### Step 7: Profit Margin
- A pre-determined percentage of cost added to arrive at the **Selling Price**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Job Costing — Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do engineers and managers NEED Job Costing?**
> 1. **Accurate Pricing** – Especially in custom orders (e.g., a shipbuilder needs to quote a price for a vessel).
> 2. **Profitability Analysis** – KTU students must know how to compute job-wise profit.
> 3. **Cost Control** – Wastage and inefficiencies are highlighted job-wise.
> 4. **Decision Making** – Whether to accept or reject a quotation depends on the cost estimate.
> 5. **Legal/Contractual Evidence** – In government contracts, the Job Cost Sheet is a legally admissible document.
> 6. **Engineering Project Management** – B.Tech students dealing with project costing, R&D, or construction will directly use these principles.

## 2.3 Overhead Absorption Rates (OAR) — The Key Engine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overhead Absorption** means charging the appropriate share of overheads to each job. This is done using a **predetermined Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR)**.

### Formulae for OAR:

| **Type of OAR** | **Formula** | **When Used** |
|---|---|---|
| **Percentage of Direct Material Cost** | $\text{OAR} = \frac{\text{Estimated Factory Overheads}}{\text{Estimated Direct Material Cost}} \times 100$ | Material-intensive jobs |
| **Percentage of Direct Labor Cost** | $\text{OAR} = \frac{\text{Estimated Factory Overheads}}{\text{Estimated Direct Wages}} \times 100$ | Labor-intensive industries |
| **Percentage of Prime Cost** | $\text{OAR} = \frac{\text{Estimated Overheads}}{\text{Estimated Prime Cost}} \times 100$ | Mixed nature jobs |
| **Per Direct Labor Hour** | $\text{OAR} = \frac{\text{Estimated Factory Overheads}}{\text{Estimated Direct Labor Hours}}$ | When labor hours are easily tracked |
| **Per Machine Hour** | $\text{OAR} = \frac{\text{Estimated Factory Overheads}}{\text{Estimated Machine Hours}}$ | Machine-dominated production |
| **Per Unit of Output** | $\text{OAR} = \frac{\text{Estimated Factory Overheads}}{\text{Estimated Units of Output}}$ | When units are standard |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Prime Cost} = \text{Direct Materials} + \text{Direct Wages} + \text{Direct Expenses}$ | Foundational cost |
| 2 | $\text{Works Cost} = \text{Prime Cost} + \text{Factory Overheads}$ | Cost at factory gate |
| 3 | $\text{Cost of Production} = \text{Works Cost} + \text{Administrative Overheads}$ | Cost ready for sale |
| 4 | $\text{Total Cost} = \text{Cost of Production} + \text{Selling \& Distribution Overheads}$ | Final cost |
| 5 | $\text{Profit} = \text{Selling Price} - \text{Total Cost}$ | Absolute profit |
| 6 | $\text{Profit \%} = \frac{\text{Profit}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100$ | Profit percentage on cost |
| 7 | $\text{Selling Price} = \text{Total Cost} + \text{Profit}$ | Quotations, invoices |
| 8 | $\text{OAR (Labor Hrs)} = \frac{\text{Estimated Overheads}}{\text{Estimated Labor Hours}}$ | Overhead recovery per hour |
| 9 | $\text{Overhead Absorbed} = \text{OAR} \times \text{Actual Base}$ | Amount charged to a job |
| 10 | $\text{Under/Over Absorption} = \text{Absorbed Overheads} - \text{Actual Overheads}$ | Variance identification |

## 2.5 Treatment of Special Items in Job Costing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Treatment Points:**
> - **Spoilage (Normal):** Cost of normal spoilage is added to the job cost.
> - **Spoilage (Abnormal):** Treated as a loss and charged to the Costing P&L Account.
> - **Scrap (Saleable):** Credited to the specific job that produced it.
> - **Defective Work:** Cost of rectification is charged to the job; if unrepairable, treated as scrap.
> - **Overtime Premium:** Charged directly to the specific job that caused it.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Comprehensive Analytical Example — Single Job

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem (KTU Board Style) — Read Every Step Carefully**

### **Question:**
A factory uses **Job Costing**. The following details relate to **Job No. 105** for the month of March:

| **Item** | **Amount (₹)** |
|---|---|
| Materials issued from stores | 25,000 |
| Materials returned to stores | 1,500 |
| Wages paid to workers (40 hours @ ₹50/hr) | 2,000 |
| Special drawings purchased for the job | 500 |
| Hire charges of a special machine | 1,000 |
| Royalties paid | 800 |
| **Estimated Factory Overheads** for the year | 1,20,000 |
| **Estimated Direct Wages** for the year | 1,50,000 |
| **Estimated Direct Labor Hours** for the year | 30,000 hrs |
| Administrative Overheads = 10% of Works Cost | — |
| Selling & Distribution Overheads = 5% of Cost of Production | — |
| Profit Margin = 12.5% on Total Cost | — |

**Required:** Prepare the **Job Cost Sheet** for Job No. 105.

---

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

#### **Step 1: Identify Direct Material**
$$
\text{Direct Material} = \text{Material Issued} - \text{Material Returned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct Material} &= 25{,}000 - 1{,}500 \\
&= 23{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Material returned to stores belongs back to inventory, not to the job, so it is deducted.

---

#### **Step 2: Identify Direct Wages**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct Wages} &= 40 \text{ hrs} \times \text{₹}50 \text{ per hour} \\
&= 2{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Step 3: Identify Direct / Chargeable Expenses**
All three are **directly attributable** to the job, hence added:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct Expenses} &= 500 + 1{,}000 + 800 \\
&= 2{,}300
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Step 4: Calculate Prime Cost**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prime Cost} &= \text{Direct Material} + \text{Direct Wages} + \text{Direct Expenses} \\
&= 23{,}500 + 2{,}000 + 2{,}300 \\
&= 27{,}800
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Step 5: Compute Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR)**

Since we have **both** wage and labor hour data, KTU standard practice is to verify which method is more appropriate. The most commonly used base in board questions is **% of Direct Wages** when labor hours are also given but a more direct % exists.

**Method A — Percentage of Direct Wages:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OAR} &= \frac{\text{Estimated Factory Overheads}}{\text{Estimated Direct Wages}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 80\% \text{ of Direct Wages}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Method B — Per Direct Labor Hour:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OAR} &= \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{30{,}000} \\
&= \text{₹}4 \text{ per direct labor hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic Check:* ₹4/hr × 40 hrs = ₹160, but 80% of ₹2,000 = ₹1,600. These give different answers.

**Method B gives ₹160, Method A gives ₹1,600.** We must choose based on the question's intent. Since the question provides both, the **% of Direct Wages method (80%)** is the most commonly tested form in KTU board papers. We will adopt **Method A** as the primary, but note **Method B** as an alternative.

---

#### **Step 6: Absorb Factory Overheads**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Factory Overheads Absorbed} &= 80\% \times 2{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Step 7: Calculate Works Cost**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Works Cost} &= \text{Prime Cost} + \text{Factory Overheads} \\
&= 27{,}800 + 1{,}600 \\
&= 29{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Step 8: Add Administrative Overheads (10% of Works Cost)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Administrative Overheads} &= 10\% \times 29{,}400 \\
&= 2{,}940 \\
\\
\text{Cost of Production} &= \text{Works Cost} + \text{Admin OH} \\
&= 29{,}400 + 2{,}940 \\
&= 32{,}340
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Step 9: Add Selling & Distribution Overheads (5% of Cost of Production)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Selling Overheads} &= 5\% \times 32{,}340 \\
&= 1{,}617 \\
\\
\text{Total Cost} &= \text{Cost of Production} + \text{Selling OH} \\
&= 32{,}340 + 1{,}617 \\
&= 33{,}957
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Step 10: Add Profit Margin (12.5% of Total Cost)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Profit} &= 12.5\% \times 33{,}957 \\
&= 4{,}244.625 \approx 4{,}245 \\
\\
\text{Selling Price} &= 33{,}957 + 4{,}245 \\
&= 38{,}202
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Final Job Cost Sheet — Job No. 105**

| **Particulars** | **Amount (₹)** | **Total (₹)** |
|---|---|---|
| Direct Materials (25,000 − 1,500) | 23,500 | |
| Direct Wages (40 hrs × 50) | 2,000 | |
| Direct Expenses (500 + 1,000 + 800) | 2,300 | |
| **A. Prime Cost** | | **27,800** |
| Add: Factory Overheads (80% of 2,000) | 1,600 | |
| **B. Works Cost** | | **29,400** |
| Add: Admin Overheads (10% of 29,400) | 2,940 | |
| **C. Cost of Production** | | **32,340** |
| Add: Selling Overheads (5% of 32,340) | 1,617 | |
| **D. Total Cost** | | **33,957** |
| Add: Profit (12.5% of 33,957) | 4,245 | |
| **E. Selling Price** | | **38,202** |

---

## 3.2 Multi-Job Problem — With Over/Under Absorption

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern: Two jobs, actual vs. absorbed overheads**

### **Question:**
A company undertakes two jobs — **Job A** and **Job B** during a quarter. The following data is given:

| **Particulars** | **Job A (₹)** | **Job B (₹)** |
|---|---|---|
| Direct Materials | 8,000 | 12,000 |
| Direct Wages | 6,000 | 10,000 |
| Direct Expenses | 1,000 | 1,500 |
| Direct Labor Hours | 300 hrs | 500 hrs |

- Estimated Factory Overheads for the quarter: **₹ 16,000**
- Estimated Direct Wages: **₹ 40,000**
- Actual Factory Overheads incurred: **₹ 17,500**

**Required:** (a) Calculate the OAR. (b) Prepare Job Cost Sheets. (c) Find the amount of **under/over absorption** of overheads.

---

### **Solution:**

#### **(a) Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OAR} &= \frac{\text{Estimated Overheads}}{\text{Estimated Direct Wages}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{16{,}000}{40{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 40\% \text{ of Direct Wages}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **(b) Job Cost Sheets**

**Job A:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prime Cost}_A &= 8{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 1{,}000 = 15{,}000 \\
\text{Factory OH}_A &= 40\% \times 6{,}000 = 2{,}400 \\
\text{Total Cost}_A &= 15{,}000 + 2{,}400 = 17{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Job B:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prime Cost}_B &= 12{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 1{,}500 = 23{,}500 \\
\text{Factory OH}_B &= 40\% \times 10{,}000 = 4{,}000 \\
\text{Total Cost}_B &= 23{,}500 + 4{,}000 = 27{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **(c) Under/Over Absorption**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overheads Absorbed} &= \text{OH}_A + \text{OH}_B = 2{,}400 + 4{,}000 = 6{,}400 \\
\text{Actual Overheads} &= 17{,}500 \\
\text{Overhead Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Absorbed}}{\text{Actual}} = \frac{6{,}400}{17{,}500} = 0.366 \\
\\
\text{Apportionment of Under-Absorption:} \\
\text{To Job A} &= (17{,}500 - 6{,}400) \times \frac{2{,}400}{6{,}400} \\
&= 11{,}100 \times 0.375 = 4{,}162.50 \\
\text{To Job B} &= 11{,}100 \times \frac{4{,}000}{6{,}400} \\
&= 11{,}100 \times 0.625 = 6{,}937.50
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Actual overheads (₹17,500) > Absorbed (₹6,400), so the difference of **₹11,100 is under-absorbed**. It is apportioned to jobs in the ratio of absorbed overheads.

**Corrected Costs:**
- **Job A Total Cost** = 17,400 + 4,162.50 = **₹ 21,562.50**
- **Job B Total Cost** = 27,500 + 6,937.50 = **₹ 34,437.50**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Job Costing vs. Process Costing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a frequently asked 14-mark KTU question. Memorize the differences:**

| **Basis** | **Job Costing** | **Process Costing** |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic Nature** | Costs accumulated per job | Costs accumulated per process/department |
| **Production Type** | Custom, non-standard | Standardized, mass production |
| **Cost Unit** | A single job or batch | A process or stage of production |
| **Cost Determination** | After completion of each job | At the end of a specific period (week/month) |
| **Transfer of Cost** | No transfer between jobs | Costs transfer from one process to the next |
| **Overheads** | Apportioned job-wise | Apportioned process-wise |
| **Industries** | Printing, shipbuilding, repair | Oil refining, chemical, cement |
| **Work-in-Progress** | Only partially completed jobs | Always present in every process |
| **Costing Method** | Specific Order Costing | Operation Costing / Continuous Costing |
| **Identification** | Easy to identify costs with jobs | Difficult to identify; averaging required |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow of Costs in Job Costing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Direct Materials\nIssued from Stores"] --> D["Job Cost Sheet\nof Particular Job"]
    B["Direct Wages\nfrom Time Tickets"] --> D
    C["Direct Expenses\nRoyalties, Hire Charges"] --> D
    D --> E["PRIME COST\nDM + DW + DE"]
    E --> F["+ Factory Overheads\n(Apportioned via OAR)"]
    F --> G["WORKS COST"]
    G --> H["+ Administrative Overheads"]
    H --> I["COST OF PRODUCTION"]
    I --> J["+ Selling & Distribution Overheads"]
    J --> K["TOTAL COST"]
    K --> L["+ Profit Margin"]
    L --> M["SELLING PRICE\nof the Job"]
```

## 4.2 Architecture of a Job Costing System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT SOURCES"]
        MR["Material\nRequisition Note"]
        JT["Job Time\nTicket"]
        SR["Special\nRequisition"]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer["JOB COST SHEET - THE CENTRAL ENGINE"]
        DM["Direct\nMaterial"]
        DW["Direct\nWages"]
        DE["Direct\nExpenses"]
        PC["Prime\nCost"]
        FO["Factory\nOverheads"]
        WC["Works\nCost"]
        AO["Admin\nOverheads"]
        CP["Cost of\nProduction"]
        SO["Selling\nOverheads"]
        TC["Total\nCost"]
        PM["Profit\nMargin"]
        SP["Selling\nPrice"]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUTS"]
        REP["Job Profitability\nReport"]
        INV["Customer\nInvoice"]
        DB["Cost Database\nfor Future Tenders"]
    end

    MR --> DM
    JT --> DW
    SR --> DE
    DM --> PC
    DW --> PC
    DE --> PC
    PC --> FO --> WC
    WC --> AO --> CP
    CP --> SO --> TC
    TC --> PM --> SP
    SP --> REP
    SP --> INV
    SP --> DB
```

## 4.3 Overhead Absorption Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Estimated Factory Overheads\nfor the Period"] --> C{"Choose Basis?"}
    B["Estimated Base\nWages / Hours / Material"] --> C
    C -->|"% of Wages"| D["OAR = Overheads / Wages × 100"]
    C -->|"Per Labor Hour"| E["OAR = Overheads / Labor Hours"]
    C -->|"% of Material"| F["OAR = Overheads / Material × 100"]
    D --> G["Apply OAR to Actual Base of Each Job"]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["Amount of Overheads Absorbed\ncharged to that Job"]
    H --> I{"Compare with\nActual Overheads"}
    I -->|"Absorbed < Actual"| J["UNDER-ABSORBED\n(Loss)"]
    I -->|"Absorbed > Actual"| K["OVER-ABSORBED\n(Gain)"]
    I -->|"Absorbed = Actual"| L["PERFECTLY ABSORBED\n(Ideal)"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

### **Question 1: Define Job Costing. State any four features of it.**
> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar Pattern]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** Job Costing is a method of costing where costs are accumulated and worked out for each job, work order, or project separately, as per the customer's special requirements.

**Four Features:**
1. **Job-wise Accumulation:** Costs are collected for each job independently using a Job Cost Sheet.
2. **Non-repetitive Production:** Each job is unique; no two jobs are exactly the same.
3. **Customer Specific:** Production is driven by customer orders, not stock.
4. **Separate Profit/Loss:** Profitability is ascertained for each job, not for the period as a whole.

---

### **Question 2: What is a Job Cost Sheet? List its main components.**
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Job Cost Sheet** is a document prepared for each job to record all costs — direct and indirect — incurred in completing that job. It serves as the **primary record** of job costing.

**Main Components:**
1. Direct Material Cost
2. Direct Wages
3. Direct / Chargeable Expenses
4. Prime Cost
5. Factory Overheads (apportioned)
6. Works Cost
7. Administrative Overheads
8. Cost of Production
9. Selling & Distribution Overheads
10. Total Cost + Profit = Selling Price

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting to deduct materials returned to stores** — Direct Material must be NET (issued minus returned).
> 2. **Confusing OAR bases** — If two bases are given (wages and hours), the question usually expects the **% of Direct Wages** method. Read carefully!
> 3. **Forgetting to add Overtime Premium** when the question states "wages include overtime."
> 4. **Adding profit to cost** but using the **wrong base** — profit is always on **cost**, not on sales, unless specified.
> 5. **Not rounding off consistently** — Most KTU solutions round to the nearest rupee in the final answer.
> 6. **Skipping the "Selling Price"** — Many students stop at Total Cost. The 14-mark question always wants the **final price**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** 
> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]`

**(a)** Define Job Costing. Explain its salient features and state the industries where it is applied. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** From the following data relating to **Job No. 207**, prepare a **Job Cost Sheet** showing the prime cost, works cost, cost of production, total cost, and selling price. **(7 Marks)**

| **Particulars** | **Amount (₹)** |
|---|---|
| Materials issued | 18,000 |
| Materials returned to stores | 2,000 |
| Wages: 200 hours @ ₹30/hour | 6,000 |
| Special patterns purchased | 1,200 |
| Hire charges of a special crane | 800 |
| Royalties paid | 600 |

Additional Information:
- Factory Overheads: 100% of Direct Wages
- Administrative Overheads: 15% of Works Cost
- Selling & Distribution Overheads: 8% of Cost of Production
- Profit: 20% on Total Cost

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition:** Job Costing is a basic method of cost accounting under which the cost of each job, work order, or project is ascertained separately. The work is carried out as per the special requirements of the customer, and each job is treated as a distinct cost unit. *(2 Marks)*

**Salient Features:** *(4 Marks)*
- Each job is unique and treated as a separate cost unit.
- Production is non-repetitive and customer-specific.
- A Job Cost Sheet is maintained for each job.
- Direct costs are charged directly; overheads are apportioned.
- Profit is determined job-wise.
- The Job Cost Sheet serves as the basis for quotation pricing.

**Industries Where Applied:** *(1 Mark)*
Printing press, ship-building, heavy engineering, automobile repair, construction, interior decoration, custom furniture.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks — Job Cost Sheet**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct Material} &= 18{,}000 - 2{,}000 = 16{,}000 \\
\text{Direct Wages} &= 200 \times 30 = 6{,}000 \\
\text{Direct Expenses} &= 1{,}200 + 800 + 600 = 2{,}600 \\
\text{Prime Cost} &= 16{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 2{,}600 = 24{,}600 \\
\text{Factory OH} &= 100\% \times 6{,}000 = 6{,}000 \\
\text{Works Cost} &= 24{,}600 + 6{,}000 = 30{,}600 \\
\text{Admin OH} &= 15\% \times 30{,}600 = 4{,}590 \\
\text{Cost of Production} &= 30{,}600 + 4{,}590 = 35{,}190 \\
\text{Selling OH} &= 8\% \times 35{,}190 = 2{,}815.20 \approx 2{,}815 \\
\text{Total Cost} &= 35{,}190 + 2{,}815 = 38{,}005 \\
\text{Profit} &= 20\% \times 38{,}005 = 7{,}601 \\
\text{Selling Price} &= 38{,}005 + 7{,}601 = 45{,}606
\end{aligned}
$$

| **Particulars** | **₹** | **Total ₹** |
|---|---|---|
| Direct Material (18,000 − 2,000) | 16,000 | |
| Direct Wages | 6,000 | |
| Direct Expenses (1,200+800+600) | 2,600 | |
| **Prime Cost** | | **24,600** |
| Factory Overheads (100% of Wages) | 6,000 | |
| **Works Cost** | | **30,600** |
| Admin Overheads (15%) | 4,590 | |
| **Cost of Production** | | **35,190** |
| Selling Overheads (8%) | 2,815 | |
| **Total Cost** | | **38,005** |
| Profit (20%) | 7,601 | |
| **Selling Price** | | **45,606** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a)** Distinguish between **Job Costing** and **Process Costing** on any seven bases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A factory uses Job Costing. The following data relates to **Job No. 312** for the month of January:

| **Item** | **Amount (₹)** |
|---|---|
| Materials issued | 12,000 |
| Materials returned | 1,000 |
| Wages: 150 hours @ ₹40/hour | 6,000 |
| Overtime premium (specifically for the job) | 500 |
| Royalties | 1,500 |
| **Estimated Annual Factory Overheads** | 2,40,000 |
| **Estimated Annual Direct Wages** | 3,00,000 |
| Administrative Overheads | 12% of Works Cost |
| Selling Overheads | 10% of Cost of Production |
| Profit | 25% on Total Cost |

**Required:** Prepare the **Job Cost Sheet** for Job No. 312. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks — Distinguish Job vs. Process Costing (7 points)**

| **Basis** | **Job Costing** | **Process Costing** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Nature of Production | Custom, non-standard | Standardized, continuous |
| 2. Cost Unit | A job or batch | A process or department |
| 3. Cost Determination | After job completion | At the end of an accounting period |
| 4. Cost Transfer | No transfer between jobs | Costs flow from one process to the next |
| 5. Work-in-Progress | Only for incomplete jobs | Always present in each process |
| 6. Industries | Printing, shipbuilding, repair | Chemical, oil, cement |
| 7. Costing Method | Specific order costing | Continuous/operation costing |

*(Each point with a 1-mark weightage, well-explained for full 7 marks.)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks — Job Cost Sheet for Job No. 312**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct Material} &= 12{,}000 - 1{,}000 = 11{,}000 \\
\text{Direct Wages} &= 150 \times 40 = 6{,}000 \\
\text{Overtime Premium} &= 500 \\
\text{Total Direct Labor} &= 6{,}000 + 500 = 6{,}500 \\
\text{Direct Expenses} &= 1{,}500 \text{ (Royalties)} \\
\text{Prime Cost} &= 11{,}000 + 6{,}500 + 1{,}500 = 19{,}000 \\
\text{OAR} &= \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 80\% \text{ of Direct Wages} \\
\text{Factory OH} &= 80\% \times 6{,}500 = 5{,}200 \\
\text{Works Cost} &= 19{,}000 + 5{,}200 = 24{,}200 \\
\text{Admin OH} &= 12\% \times 24{,}200 = 2{,}904 \\
\text{Cost of Production} &= 24{,}200 + 2{,}904 = 27{,}104 \\
\text{Selling OH} &= 10\% \times 27{,}104 = 2{,}710.40 \approx 2{,}710 \\
\text{Total Cost} &= 27{,}104 + 2{,}710 = 29{,}814 \\
\text{Profit} &= 25\% \times 29{,}814 = 7{,}453.50 \approx 7{,}454 \\
\text{Selling Price} &= 29{,}814 + 7{,}454 = 37{,}268
\end{aligned}
$$

| **Particulars** | **₹** | **Total ₹** |
|---|---|---|
| Direct Material | 11,000 | |
| Direct Wages (incl. OT) | 6,500 | |
| Direct Expenses (Royalties) | 1,500 | |
| **Prime Cost** | | **19,000** |
| Factory Overheads (80% of 6,500) | 5,200 | |
| **Works Cost** | | **24,200** |
| Admin Overheads (12%) | 2,904 | |
| **Cost of Production** | | **27,104** |
| Selling Overheads (10%) | 2,710 | |
| **Total Cost** | | **29,814** |
| Profit (25%) | 7,454 | |
| **Selling Price** | | **37,268** |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Job Costing:**

### 🔹 **Core Concepts**
- ☐ Job Costing = **Specific Order Costing** for non-repetitive, custom production.
- ☐ The **Job Cost Sheet** is the heart of the entire system.
- ☐ Each job is a **distinct cost unit**; profitability is computed **per job**.

### 🔹 **Cost Build-Up (Memorize This Sequence)**
- ☐ **Prime Cost** = DM + DW + DE
- ☐ **Works Cost** = Prime Cost + Factory Overheads
- ☐ **Cost of Production** = Works Cost + Admin Overheads
- ☐ **Total Cost** = Cost of Production + Selling Overheads
- ☐ **Selling Price** = Total Cost + Profit

### 🔹 **Overhead Absorption — The Most Tested Area**
- ☐ OAR is **predetermined** using estimated figures.
- ☐ Most common bases: **% of Direct Wages**, **Per Labor Hour**, **% of Prime Cost**.
- ☐ **Under-absorption** = Absorbed < Actual (Loss)
- ☐ **Over-absorption** = Absorbed > Actual (Gain)

### 🔹 **Special Treatments (Don't Lose Marks Here)**
- ☐ **Material Returned** = ALWAYS deducted from issued material.
- ☐ **Overtime Premium** = Added to the specific job causing it.
- ☐ **Scrap** (from a job) = Credited to that job.
- ☐ **Abnormal Spoilage** = Charged to Costing P&L, NOT to the job.
- ☐ **Direct Expenses** = All expenses directly attributable to the job (royalties, hire, drawings).

### 🔹 **KTU-Specific Tips**
- ☐ When a question gives **wages AND hours**, default to **% of Direct Wages** unless the question explicitly asks for per-hour rate.
- ☐ Always state the **basis of overhead absorption** clearly before computing.
- ☐ Profit is **on cost** unless the question specifies "on sales" — read carefully.
- ☐ Round to the nearest rupee only in the **final selling price**.
- ☐ **Job Costing vs. Process Costing** is a guaranteed 7-mark question — memorize the 7 differences.

### 🔹 **Common Industries to Mention**
- ☐ Printing, ship-building, construction, custom furniture, automobile repair, interior design, heavy engineering, R&D projects.

### 🔹 **Golden Formula Reminder**
$$
\text{Selling Price} = \text{Prime Cost} + \text{All Overheads} + \text{Profit Margin}
$$

> *"In Job Costing, every job tells a story — and the Job Cost Sheet is its autobiography."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Costing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT523 - FINANCIAL AND COST ACCOUNTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Costing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Costing** is a method of cost accounting used to assign production costs to units of output when goods are produced in a **continuous, mass-production flow** where individual units are largely **homogeneous (identical)** and cannot be easily distinguished from one another. Costs are accumulated **process-wise** (department-wise) for a given accounting period and then averaged over the total units produced.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Process Costing is positioned as the **inverse counterpart of Job Costing**. While Job Costing traces costs to a *specific batch or order*, Process Costing **averages costs over a large volume of identical units passing through a sequence of standardized operations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECBT523 – Module 4):** Process costing is treated as a foundational cost-determination technique. The examiner expects mastery over **Equivalent Units, Normal/Abnormal Loss/Gain, Joint Products, and By-Products** — all of which are interlinked derivatives of this topic.

### Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | KTU Definition | Industry Alias |
|------|----------------|----------------|
| **Process** | A continuous, repetitive operation that adds value to a product | Department / Stage |
| **Process Cost Centre** | The location (e.g., a department) where costs are accumulated before being transferred | Cost Object |
| **Normal Loss** | The unavoidable, expected loss inherent to the process (e.g., evaporation, shrinkage) | Expected Spoilage |
| **Abnormal Loss** | Loss *beyond* the normal limit — caused by accidents, negligence, or inefficiencies | Exceptional Waste |
| **Abnormal Gain** | Actual output *exceeding* expected output — a credit to the process account | Yield Surplus |
| **Scrap Value** | Realisable value of the loss units (resale or salvage) | Salvage Proceeds |
| **Equivalent Units (EUP)** | The number of *complete* units that could have been produced given the work done on incomplete units | Work Units |
| **Transferred-In Cost** | Cost accumulated in a previous process that is carried forward | Prior Dept. Cost |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Bottled Water Factory" Analogy

Imagine a **mineral water bottling plant** in Kerala. Every single 1-litre bottle of "Crystal Clear" is *identical* in size, shape, and contents. There is no way to tell which specific drop of water came from which specific well on which specific day. The plant operates continuously — 24/7.

So how does the accountant calculate the cost of *one* bottle?

> **The Realization:** The accountant does NOT track each bottle individually. Instead, she does the following:
> 1. Totals up **all costs** for the entire plant for the month (water, electricity, labour, depreciation).
> 2. Totals up **all bottles produced** (and adjusts for partially-filled ones).
> 3. **Divides the lot** — Cost per bottle = Total Cost ÷ Total Equivalent Bottles.

That is the **essence of Process Costing** in one breath. It is the "averaging" or "blending" of costs across a continuous stream of indistinguishable products.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter to engineers?** Many real engineering industries — petroleum refining, cement manufacturing, paper mills, semiconductor fabrication, sugar processing, paint mixing — use Process Costing. Understanding the cost flow helps in **make-or-buy decisions, bottleneck identification, and yield-improvement projects**.

---

## 1.3 Industries That Use Process Costing

| Industry | Why Process Costing Fits |
|----------|--------------------------|
| Petroleum / Oil Refining | Continuous distillation; identical output |
| Cement & Steel | Homogeneous granular output from blast furnaces |
| Paper & Pulp Mills | Continuous web production |
| Beverages & Bottling | Identical units in a continuous flow |
| Chemicals & Paints | Standardized batch reactions in reactors |
| Food Processing (rice, sugar, flour) | Indistinguishable end products |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Trap:** Students often confuse **Process Costing with Job Costing**. The decisive test is: *Can you identify the cost of a single, specific unit?* If **YES → Job Costing**. If **NO, only an average → Process Costing**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Process Costing — Step by Step

Process Costing operates on a **five-stage sequential logic**. Every KTU numerical problem can be deconstructed into these five stages.

### Stage 1: Identify the Process and Cost Elements
- A product passes through a defined sequence of **processes** (e.g., Process I → Process II → Process III).
- In each process, three cost elements accumulate: **Direct Materials (DM), Direct Labour (DL), and Direct Expenses / Overheads (DE)**.
- Plus, **Transferred-In Costs** from the previous process for processes II onwards.

### Stage 2: Determine Units Processed
- **Units Introduced (or Received from Previous Process)**
- Less: **Normal Loss** (pre-determined by technical estimate)
- Less: **Abnormal Loss** (computed as: Actual Output + Normal Loss − Units Introduced)
- Add: **Abnormal Gain** (if output > Units Introduced − Normal Loss)
- = **Expected Output / Good Units**

### Stage 3: Ascertain Scrap / Realisable Value
- The sale value of one unit of loss (scrap) is added back to the **credit side** of the Process Account, reducing the net cost.

### Stage 4: Compute Cost per Unit
- For *no opening/closing WIP* problems:

$$\text{Cost per Unit} = \frac{\text{Total Cost (Debit Side)} - \text{Scrap Value of Normal Loss}}{\text{Expected Output}}$$

- For *WIP* problems (using **Equivalent Units** or **Average / FIFO Method**), the formula extends — see Section 3.

### Stage 5: Value the Outputs
- **Good Units Completed** → transferred at Cost per Unit × Units.
- **Normal Loss** → credited with scrap value, balance (if any) written off to **Costing Profit & Loss A/c**.
- **Abnormal Loss** → valued at **Cost per Unit** (or full cost including share of scrap) and debited to **Abnormal Loss A/c**.
- **Abnormal Gain** → credited to Process A/c at **Cost per Unit** and shown as income / credited to **Abnormal Gain A/c**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-day survival kit** for any Process Costing question. Memorize it as a single, inseparable block.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Cost per Unit (No WIP) | $$\text{CPU} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost} - \text{Scrap of Normal Loss}}{\text{Expected Good Units}}$$ | ₹/unit |
| 2 | Expected Output | $$\text{Expected Output} = \text{Input} - \text{Normal Loss}$$ | units |
| 3 | Abnormal Loss (units) | $$\text{Abnormal Loss} = \text{Input} - \text{Normal Loss} - \text{Actual Output}$$ | units |
| 4 | Abnormal Gain (units) | $$\text{Abnormal Gain} = \text{Actual Output} - (\text{Input} - \text{Normal Loss})$$ | units |
| 5 | Equivalent Units (FIFO, Material added at start) | $$\text{EUP}_{\text{Mat}} = \text{Completed} + \text{Closing WIP} + \text{Opening WIP (if mat added at start)}$$ | units |
| 6 | Equivalent Units (Weighted Avg) | $$\text{EUP} = \text{Completed} + \text{Closing WIP} \times \text{\% completion}$$ | units |
| 7 | Cost per Equivalent Unit (Weighted Avg) | $$\text{CPU} = \dfrac{\text{Opening WIP Cost} + \text{Current Period Cost}}{\text{EUP Completed + EUP Closing WIP}}$$ | ₹/unit |
| 8 | Cost per Equivalent Unit (FIFO) | $$\text{CPU} = \dfrac{\text{Current Period Cost Only}}{\text{EUP (this period's work only)}}$$ | ₹/unit |
| 9 | Total Cost of Completed Units | $$\text{Value} = \text{Units Completed} \times \text{CPU}$$ | ₹ |
| 10 | Value of Closing WIP | $$\text{Value}_{\text{WIP}} = \text{EUP}_{\text{Closing WIP}} \times \text{CPU}$$ | ₹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER** write absolute value bars `|x|` inside markdown tables. The KTU Formula Sheet uses $\vert x \vert$ or $x$ to avoid formatting collapse.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Process Costing

| Decision Area | How Process Costing Helps |
|---------------|---------------------------|
| **Pricing** | Provides a reliable average cost per unit, vital for commodity-like products with thin margins |
| **Cost Control** | Process-wise cost accumulation highlights the *most expensive stage* (e.g., which process is bleeding cash) |
| **Inventory Valuation** | Closing WIP and Finished Goods are valued consistently using equivalent units |
| **Performance Benchmarking** | Cost per process can be compared across plants, quarters, and competitor benchmarks |
| **Yield Management** | The Normal vs. Abnormal Loss split isolates the controllable from the uncontrollable |
| **Transfer Pricing** | Transferred-in cost acts as the internal price between divisions (e.g., refinery crude → petrochem) |

> [!TIP]
> In **throughput accounting** (used in Theory of Constraints), the principles of Process Costing are extended to identify the **bottleneck process** that limits the rate of value creation.

---

## 2.4 The Two Major Methodologies — Weighted Average vs. FIFO

| Aspect | Weighted Average Method | FIFO Method |
|--------|--------------------------|-------------|
| **Opening WIP Treatment** | Its cost is *blended* with current period costs | Its cost is *kept separate* |
| **Cost Distortion** | Slightly distorts current period performance | More accurate reflection of current period |
| **Computation Complexity** | Lower — fewer line items | Higher — opening WIP split into "to complete" and "started & completed" |
| **KTU Board Preference** | Most numericals use this method | Increasingly asked in advanced problems |
| **Best for** | Stable, long-running processes | Processes with rapidly changing input costs |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a problem states *"opening WIP is given separately and you are asked to value current period output distinctly,"* the examiner is **signalling FIFO**. Otherwise, default to **Weighted Average**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Simple Two-Process Problem (No WIP)

### Problem Statement

> **A chemical company passes raw material through two sequential processes. The following data is available for April 2024:**
>
> | Particulars | Process A (₹) | Process B (₹) |
> |-------------|---------------|---------------|
> | Materials (added) | 1,20,000 | 60,000 |
> | Labour | 80,000 | 70,000 |
> | Direct Expenses | 40,000 | 30,000 |
>
> - **Input to Process A:** 10,000 units @ ₹20 each.
> - **Normal Loss in Process A:** 10% of input. Scrap value ₹4 per unit.
> - **Normal Loss in Process B:** 5% of input. Scrap value ₹6 per unit.
> - **Actual Output of Process A:** 9,000 units transferred to Process B.
> - **Actual Output of Process B:** 8,500 units transferred to Finished Stock.
>
> **Required:** Prepare Process A and Process B Accounts.

---

### Step 1 — Compute Inputs and Losses for Process A

Material Cost of Inputs:

$$\text{Material Input Cost} = 10{,}000 \times 20 = 2{,}00{,}000$$

Total Cost Debited to Process A:

$$\text{Total Cost} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 4{,}40{,}000$$

Normal Loss (units):

$$\text{Normal Loss} = 10{,}000 \times 10\% = 1{,}000 \text{ units}$$

Expected Output (Good Units):

$$\text{Expected Output} = 10{,}000 - 1{,}000 = 9{,}000 \text{ units}$$

Since actual output = 9,000 units, there is **no abnormal loss or gain** in Process A. 

> **Logic Note:** When actual output equals expected output, the entire loss is "normal" and the process ran exactly to design specifications.

---

### Step 2 — Cost per Unit for Process A

Scrap Value of Normal Loss:

$$\text{Scrap Value} = 1{,}000 \times 4 = 4{,}000$$

Net Cost:

$$\text{Net Cost} = 4{,}40{,}000 - 4{,}000 = 4{,}36{,}000$$

Cost per Unit:

$$\text{CPU}_{\text{A}} = \frac{4{,}36{,}000}{9{,}000} = 48.444... \approx 48.44 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

> **Logic Note:** The scrap value is deducted because those 1,000 units are NOT part of the good output — they are sold as scrap, so the process should not bear their cost.

---

### Step 3 — Process A Account (Tabular Form)

| **Dr. (Debit Side)** | Units | ₹ | | **Cr. (Credit Side)** | Units | ₹ |
|----------------------|-------|---|---|------------------------|-------|---|
| To Materials (Input) | 10,000 | 2,00,000 | | By Normal Loss (Scrap) | 1,000 | 4,000 |
| To Materials (Added) | — | 1,20,000 | | By Process B A/c (Output) | 9,000 | 4,36,000 |
| To Labour | — | 80,000 | | | | |
| To Direct Expenses | — | 40,000 | | | | |
| **Total** | **10,000** | **4,40,000** | | **Total** | **10,000** | **4,40,000** |

---

### Step 4 — Compute Process B Inputs and Losses

Transferred-In Cost from Process A:

$$\text{Transferred-In} = 9{,}000 \times 48.44 = 4{,}36{,}000$$

Total Cost Debited to Process B:

$$\text{Total Cost}_{\text{B}} = 4{,}36{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 70{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 5{,}96{,}000$$

Normal Loss in Process B (5% of input to B):

$$\text{Normal Loss}_{\text{B}} = 9{,}000 \times 5\% = 450 \text{ units}$$

Expected Output of Process B:

$$\text{Expected Output}_{\text{B}} = 9{,}000 - 450 = 8{,}550 \text{ units}$$

---

### Step 5 — Identify Abnormal Loss in Process B

$$\text{Abnormal Loss} = \text{Expected Output} - \text{Actual Output} = 8{,}550 - 8{,}500 = 50 \text{ units}$$

> **Logic Note:** The plant was expected to deliver 8,550 good units but produced only 8,500. The missing 50 units is an *abnormal* loss — likely due to inefficiency or accident. This is shown separately and NOT blended into normal cost.

---

### Step 6 — Cost per Unit for Process B

Scrap Value of Normal Loss:

$$\text{Scrap Value}_{\text{B}} = 450 \times 6 = 2{,}700$$

Net Cost:

$$\text{Net Cost}_{\text{B}} = 5{,}96{,}000 - 2{,}700 = 5{,}93{,}300$$

Cost per Unit:

$$\text{CPU}_{\text{B}} = \frac{5{,}93{,}300}{8{,}550} = 69.39 \text{ ₹/unit (approx.)}$$

---

### Step 7 — Value of Abnormal Loss

Abnormal Loss is valued at the **full cost per unit** (including its share of scrap — but since the scrap is only for *normal* loss, the abnormal loss is valued at the simple CPU):

$$\text{Value of Abnormal Loss} = 50 \times 69.39 = 3{,}469.50 \approx 3{,}470$$

---

### Step 8 — Process B Account

| **Dr. (Debit Side)** | Units | ₹ | | **Cr. (Credit Side)** | Units | ₹ |
|----------------------|-------|---|---|------------------------|-------|---|
| To Process A A/c | 9,000 | 4,36,000 | | By Normal Loss (Scrap) | 450 | 2,700 |
| To Materials (Added) | — | 60,000 | | By Abnormal Loss A/c | 50 | 3,470 |
| To Labour | — | 70,000 | | By Finished Stock A/c | 8,500 | 5,89,830 |
| To Direct Expenses | — | 30,000 | | | | |
| **Total** | **9,000** | **5,96,000** | | **Total** | **9,000** | **5,96,000** |

**Verification:** 2,700 + 3,470 + 5,89,830 = 5,96,000 ✓

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — Equivalent Units (Weighted Average Method)

### Problem Statement

> **Process X receives raw material at the start of the process. The following data is available for March 2024:**
> - **Opening WIP:** 200 units, 60% complete for materials, labour, and overheads. Cost incurred so far: ₹8,000.
> - **Units Introduced:** 1,800 units.
> - **Materials Added:** ₹54,000 (added at the start of process).
> - **Labour:** ₹36,000.
> - **Overheads:** ₹18,000.
> - **Closing WIP:** 300 units, 40% complete.
> - **Completed Units:** 1,700 units.
>
> **Required:** Compute Equivalent Units, Cost per Equivalent Unit, and value the outputs using the **Weighted Average Method**.

---

### Step 1 — Verify Unit Reconciliation

$$\text{Opening WIP} + \text{Introduced} = \text{Completed} + \text{Closing WIP} + \text{Abnormal Loss}$$

$$200 + 1{,}800 = 1{,}700 + 300 + 0 = 2{,}000 \text{ units} \checkmark$$

No abnormal loss — clean process run.

---

### Step 2 — Compute Equivalent Units (Weighted Average)

Under Weighted Average, **opening WIP is NOT split** — its work is already embedded in the completed units. So we simply compute:

**Material EUP** (material added at start → all units in process have 100% material):

$$\text{EUP}_{\text{Mat}} = 1{,}700 + (300 \times 100\%) = 1{,}700 + 300 = 2{,}000$$

**Conversion EUP** (labour + overheads):

$$\text{EUP}_{\text{Conv}} = 1{,}700 + (300 \times 40\%) = 1{,}700 + 120 = 1{,}820$$

> **Logic Note:** The completed units are 100% complete by definition. The closing WIP units are *partially* complete — multiplying by the percentage completion converts them into "equivalent" fully-completed units.

---

### Step 3 — Total Costs (Opening + Current)

| Cost Element | Opening (₹) | Current (₹) | Total (₹) |
|--------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| Material | (assumed in 8,000) | 54,000 | 54,000* |
| Labour | (assumed in 8,000) | 36,000 | 36,000 |
| Overheads | (assumed in 8,000) | 18,000 | 18,000 |

*For this KTU-level problem, we treat the ₹8,000 opening WIP cost as a **single combined lump sum** (typical in basic problems) and assume material was added at start so the entire opening cost is allocated against conversion.

**Simplified Combined Approach:**

$$\text{Total Cost} = 8{,}000 + 54{,}000 + 36{,}000 + 18{,}000 = 1{,}16{,}000$$

---

### Step 4 — Cost per Equivalent Unit

**Material CPU:**

$$\text{CPU}_{\text{Mat}} = \frac{54{,}000}{2{,}000} = 27 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Conversion CPU:**

$$\text{CPU}_{\text{Conv}} = \frac{8{,}000 + 36{,}000 + 18{,}000}{1{,}820} = \frac{62{,}000}{1{,}820} = 34.07 \text{ ₹/unit (approx.)}$$

**Total CPU:**

$$\text{CPU}_{\text{Total}} = 27 + 34.07 = 61.07 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

---

### Step 5 — Value the Outputs

**Completed Units (1,700):**

$$\text{Value} = 1{,}700 \times 61.07 = 1{,}03{,}819$$

**Closing WIP (300 units, 40% complete):**

- Material: $300 \times 100\% \times 27 = 8{,}100$
- Conversion: $300 \times 40\% \times 34.07 = 4{,}088.40$

$$\text{Total WIP Value} = 8{,}100 + 4{,}088.40 = 12{,}188.40$$

**Verification:**

$$1{,}03{,}819 + 12{,}188.40 = 1{,}16{,}007.40 \approx 1{,}16{,}000 \text{ ✓ (rounding tolerance)}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Rules for KTU Problems

| Clue in the Problem | Method to Apply | Why |
|---------------------|-----------------|-----|
| No mention of WIP, no opening/closing inventory | Simple Average Cost (No WIP formula) | All units completed within the period |
| "Opening WIP given, no instruction to separate" | Weighted Average Method | Opening WIP cost blends with current |
| "Opening WIP, value current period output separately" | FIFO Method | Need to isolate current period performance |
| Material added at "beginning" or "start" | 100% material in all units in process | Material cannot be extracted back |
| Material added at "end" or "at 50% stage" | Use stage-completion % for material | Only units past that stage have material |
| Loss exceeds normal limit | Abnormal Loss → debit Abnormal Loss A/c | Isolate the controllable from uncontrollable |
| Output exceeds expected output | Abnormal Gain → credit Abnormal Gain A/c | Surplus yield from efficiency or input error |
| Two or more products from one process | Joint Products / By-Products | Use split-off point valuation methods |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Process Cost Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete cost flow architecture** of a multi-process manufacturing system, including loss, gain, and scrap treatment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Raw Material Store]):::store --> matIssue[Materials Issued to Process A]
    matIssue --> procA[Process A: Mixing and Blending]
    procA --> normalLossA{Normal Loss 10 percent Scrap Rs 4}:::lossnode
    procA --> abLossA{Abnormal Loss Units}:::abnode
    procA --> transB[Transfer to Process B at CPU A]
    normalLossA --> scrapSale[Scrap Sale Realisation]:::scrap
    abLossA --> abLossAcct[Abnormal Loss Account Profit Loss]:::abnode
    transB --> procB[Process B: Refining and Shaping]
    procB --> normalLossB{Normal Loss 5 percent Scrap Rs 6}:::lossnode
    procB --> abGainB{Abnormal Gain Units}:::gainnode
    procB --> finStock[Transfer to Finished Stock at CPU B]
    normalLossB --> scrapSale
    abGainB --> abGainAcct[Abnormal Gain Account Credit Income]:::gainnode
    finStock --> costingPL[Costing Profit and Loss Account]
    scrapSale --> costingPL
    abLossAcct --> costingPL
    abGainAcct --> costingPL
    costingPL --> finalReport[Management Cost Report]:::store

    classDef store fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lossnode fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef abnode fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gainnode fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef scrap fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Blue boxes** = cost flow and storage points.
> - **Red boxes** = loss treatment paths.
> - **Green boxes** = gain/income paths.
> - **Amber box** = scrap realisation that reduces net process cost.
> - All paths ultimately terminate at the **Costing Profit & Loss Account** — the central ledger where the *true* cost of production is reconciled.

---

## 4.2 Equivalent Units Computation — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    openingWIP[Opening WIP Units]:::input --> opWork[Work to be Done on Opening WIP]
    currentInput[Units Introduced This Period]:::input --> totalUnits[Total Units to Account For]
    openingWIP --> totalUnits
    totalUnits --> startComplete[Started and Completed]
    totalUnits --> closingWIP[Closing WIP Units]:::wip
    totalUnits --> normalLossCalc[Normal Loss Expected]:::loss
    opWork --> fifoSep{FIFO Method Split}:::method
    fifoSep --> opComplete[Opening WIP Completion]
    fifoSep --> startComplete
    startComplete --> euCalc[Equivalent Unit Calculation]
    closingWIP --> euCalc
    opComplete --> euCalc
    totalCosts[Total Costs Material Labour Overhead]:::cost --> cpu[Cost per Equivalent Unit]
    euCalc --> cpu
    cpu --> valueOutputs[Value Completed Units]
    cpu --> valueWIP[Value Closing WIP]
    valueOutputs --> finalRecon[Reconciliation Check]:::check
    valueWIP --> finalRecon
    normalLossCalc --> finalRecon

    classDef input fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wip fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef method fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cost fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef check fill:#cffafe,stroke:#155e75,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow separates **physical units** (left) from **cost flows** (right), converging at the **Reconciliation Check** block. This mirrors the KTU board's two-sided Process Account format: units on one axis, costs on the other.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — When to Use Which Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Process Costing Problem]):::root --> q1{Opening WIP or Closing WIP present?}
    q1 -- No --> simple[Simple Average Costing No WIP]:::simple
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Method specified in question?}
    q2 -- No --> wavg[Weighted Average Method]:::wavg
    q2 -- Yes FIFO --> fifo[FIFO Method]:::fifo
    q2 -- Yes Standard --> std[Standard Costing Method]:::std
    simple --> formula1[Use formula Net Cost divided by Expected Output]
    wavg --> formula2[Use formula Opening Plus Current divided by EUP Completed Plus EUP Closing WIP]
    fifo --> formula3[Use formula Current Period Cost Only divided by EUP current work only]
    std --> formula4[Use pre-set standard cost adjusted for variances]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef simple fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wavg fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fifo fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef std fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#86198f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Distinguish between Job Costing and Process Costing. Under what circumstances is Process Costing preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Job Costing | Process Costing |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Product Nature** | Heterogeneous, custom-made | Homogeneous, standardized |
| **Cost Object** | Individual job / batch | Process / department |
| **Cost Computation** | Actual cost per job | Average cost per unit |
| **WIP Treatment** | Job-specific WIP | Process-wide WIP using EUP |
| **Suitability** | Custom furniture, printing, shipbuilding | Oil refining, chemicals, cement |

**Circumstances favouring Process Costing (1 Mark):**
1. Continuous, mass production of homogeneous goods.
2. Output of one process becomes input of the next.
3. Cost of individual unit tracing is uneconomical.
4. Standardization is the operational norm.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the concept of "Equivalent Units" with a suitable illustration. Why is it necessary in Process Costing?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Concept (1 Mark):** Equivalent Units (EUP) represent the number of *complete* units that could have been produced given the amount of work performed on partially completed units during a period. It converts partial work into a standardised "full-unit" measure.

**Illustration (1 Mark):**
If 200 units are 50% complete in a process, they are equivalent to **100 fully completed units**. Thus 800 fully completed + 200 × 50% = **900 Equivalent Units** for that period.

**Necessity (1 Mark):**
- Closing WIP is never 100% complete.
- To average costs meaningfully, partial units must be converted to a common base.
- Without EUP, comparing per-unit costs across periods becomes distorted.
- It is the **mathematical bridge** between physical units and monetary costs.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU's standard End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern for Module 4, **internal choice** is provided. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**The XY Chemicals Ltd. operates two consecutive processes. The following information is extracted for the month of February 2024:**

| Particulars | Process I (₹) | Process II (₹) |
|-------------|---------------|----------------|
| Materials consumed | 80,000 | 40,000 |
| Labour | 50,000 | 45,000 |
| Manufacturing Overheads | 30,000 | 25,000 |

Additional details:
- **1,000 units** were issued to Process I at ₹60 per unit.
- **Normal Loss** in Process I = 5% of input. Scrap value = ₹5 per unit.
- **Normal Loss** in Process II = 10% of input. Scrap value = ₹8 per unit.
- **Actual Output** of Process I = 950 units (transferred to Process II).
- **Actual Output** of Process II = 820 units (transferred to Finished Stock).
- There is **no opening or closing WIP**.

**Required:**
- **(a)** Prepare **Process I Account** and **Process II Account**, clearly showing normal loss, abnormal loss/gain, and the value of output transferred. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** Prepare the **Finished Stock Account** and pass the **Abnormal Loss / Gain entries to the Costing Profit & Loss Account** through a single reconciliation statement. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

##### Part (a) — Process Accounts (7 Marks)

**Process I Account:**

| **Dr.** | Units | ₹ | | **Cr.** | Units | ₹ |
|---------|-------|---|---|---------|-------|---|
| To Materials (Input) | 1,000 | 60,000 | | By Normal Loss (Scrap) | 50 | 250 |
| To Materials (Added) | — | 80,000 | | By Process II A/c (Output) | 950 | 1,59,250 |
| To Labour | — | 50,000 | | | | |
| To Overheads | — | 30,000 | | | | |
| **Total** | **1,000** | **2,20,000** | | **Total** | **1,000** | **1,59,500** |

**Working — Process I:**

- Normal Loss units = 1,000 × 5% = 50 units. Scrap value = 50 × 5 = ₹250.
- Expected Output = 1,000 − 50 = 950 units. **Actual Output = 950 → No Abnormal Loss/Gain.**
- Net Cost = 2,20,000 − 250 = 2,19,750.
- $$\text{CPU}_{\text{I}} = \frac{2{,}19{,}750}{950} = 231.32 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Process II Account:**

| **Dr.** | Units | ₹ | | **Cr.** | Units | ₹ |
|---------|-------|---|---|---------|-------|---|
| To Process I A/c | 950 | 2,19,750 | | By Normal Loss (Scrap) | 95 | 760 |
| To Materials (Added) | — | 40,000 | | By Abnormal Loss A/c | 35 | 8,567 |
| To Labour | — | 45,000 | | By Finished Stock A/c | 820 | 2,00,723 |
| To Overheads | — | 25,000 | | | | |
| **Total** | **950** | **3,29,750** | | **Total** | **950** | **2,10,050** |

**Working — Process II:**

- Normal Loss = 950 × 10% = 95 units. Scrap value = 95 × 8 = ₹760.
- Expected Output = 950 − 95 = 855 units. Actual Output = 820.
- $$\text{Abnormal Loss} = 855 - 820 = 35 \text{ units}$$
- Net Cost = 3,29,750 − 760 = 3,28,990.
- $$\text{CPU}_{\text{II}} = \frac{3{,}28{,}990}{855} = 384.78 \text{ ₹/unit}$$
- Value of Abnormal Loss = 35 × 384.78 = ₹13,467 *(corrected; reconciled below)*

**Reconciliation Adjustment:**
For accounting balance, recompute precisely using exact CPU:

$$\text{CPU}_{\text{II}} = \frac{3{,}28{,}990}{855} = 384.78$$

- Normal Loss credit: 95 × 384.78... = **95 × (3,28,990/855)** → Use **proportionate scrap methodology**:

A more practical KTU approach: Abnormal Loss is valued at full CPU including proportionate scrap:

$$\text{Value of Abnormal Loss} = 35 \times \frac{3{,}28{,}990}{855} = 13{,}466$$

- Finished Stock = 820 × 384.78 = **3,15,524**
- Sum of credits: 760 + 13,466 + 3,15,524 = **3,29,750** ✓

##### Part (b) — Finished Stock Account and Reconciliation (7 Marks)

**Finished Stock Account:**

| **Dr.** | Units | ₹ | | **Cr.** | Units | ₹ |
|---------|-------|---|---|---------|-------|---|
| To Process II A/c (Balance b/d) | 820 | 3,15,524 | | By Costing P&L A/c (Sales) | 820 | (at selling price) |
| | | | | **By Balance c/d** | 0 | 0 |

> **Note:** Since no sale price is mentioned, the credit side shows transfer to Costing P&L at the same cost for balance confirmation, leaving no closing stock.

**Costing Profit & Loss Account (Abnormal Items Only):**

| **Dr.** | ₹ | | **Cr.** | ₹ |
|---------|---|---|---------|---|
| To Normal Loss (Process II, written off) | 760 | | By Abnormal Loss A/c | 13,466 |
| | | | | |
| **Net Credit to P&L** | | | | **12,706** |

> **Logic Note:** Normal Loss scrap value of ₹760 from Process I was already netted into Process I's CPU. The ₹760 from Process II is the *net* scrap value that was *not* covered in CPU — it represents the balancing figure. The Abnormal Loss is treated as an expense of the period and charged to P&L.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**ABC Manufacturing Ltd. produces a chemical "Z" through a single process. Data for the month of January 2024:**

| Particulars | Units | ₹ |
|-------------|-------|---|
| Opening WIP (60% complete for materials; 40% for labour & overheads) | 400 | 6,000 |
| Units introduced during the month | 2,600 | — |
| Materials added (introduced at the start of process) | — | 78,000 |
| Labour incurred | — | 48,000 |
| Overheads absorbed | — | 24,000 |
| Closing WIP (40% complete for materials; 30% for labour & overheads) | 500 | — |
| Units completed and transferred to Finished Stock | 2,500 | — |
| Normal Loss (by evaporation) | Expected | Scrap = ₹2/unit |

**Required:**
- **(a)** Compute the **Equivalent Units of Production** under the **Weighted Average Method**, separating material and conversion. **(4 Marks)**
- **(b)** Compute the **Cost per Equivalent Unit** for material and conversion separately, and determine the **total value of output transferred to finished stock** and the **value of closing WIP**. **(7 Marks)**
- **(c)** Prepare a **Process Account** and show the **Abnormal Loss / Gain treatment**. **(3 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

##### Part (a) — Equivalent Units (4 Marks)

**Unit Reconciliation:**

$$400 (\text{Op WIP}) + 2{,}600 (\text{Input}) = 2{,}500 (\text{Completed}) + 500 (\text{Cl. WIP}) = 3{,}000 \text{ ✓}$$

**Material EUP** (Material added at start → 100% in all units present):

$$\text{EUP}_{\text{Mat}} = 2{,}500 + 500 \times 100\% = 2{,}500 + 500 = 3{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Conversion EUP** (Labour + Overheads):

$$\text{EUP}_{\text{Conv}} = 2{,}500 + (500 \times 30\%) = 2{,}500 + 150 = 2{,}650 \text{ units}$$

##### Part (b) — Cost per Equivalent Unit and Valuation (7 Marks)

**Total Costs:**

| Element | Opening (₹) | Current (₹) | Total (₹) |
|---------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| Material | 6,000 (assumed lump) | 78,000 | 84,000* |
| Labour + Overhead | (incl. above) | 48,000 + 24,000 = 72,000 | 72,000 |

> *For this KTU-level problem, we use the **combined approach** where the opening WIP cost is treated as a conversion-related balance (since material was added at start and 60% complete on materials implies the previous period's material cost is embedded).

**Refined Split (assuming opening ₹6,000 is fully conversion-related since 40% conversion was incomplete):**

- Total Material Cost = ₹78,000
- Total Conversion Cost = ₹6,000 + ₹72,000 = ₹78,000

**Cost per Equivalent Unit:**

$$\text{CPU}_{\text{Mat}} = \frac{78{,}000}{3{,}000} = 26.00 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

$$\text{CPU}_{\text{Conv}} = \frac{78{,}000}{2{,}650} = 29.43 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

$$\text{CPU}_{\text{Total}} = 26.00 + 29.43 = 55.43 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Value of Completed Units (2,500):**

$$\text{Value}_{\text{Completed}} = 2{,}500 \times 55.43 = 1{,}38{,}575$$

**Value of Closing WIP (500 units):**

- Material: $500 \times 100\% \times 26.00 = 13{,}000$
- Conversion: $500 \times 30\% \times 29.43 = 4{,}415$

$$\text{Value}_{\text{Closing WIP}} = 13{,}000 + 4{,}415 = 17{,}415$$

**Reconciliation:**

$$1{,}38{,}575 + 17{,}415 = 1{,}55{,}990$$

Actual total cost = 6,000 + 78,000 + 48,000 + 24,000 = **1,56,000**

Difference (₹10) is rounding tolerance. ✓

##### Part (c) — Process Account and Abnormal Treatment (3 Marks)

**No abnormal loss/gain** since units balance (2,500 + 500 = 3,000).

**Process Account:**

| **Dr.** | Units | ₹ | | **Cr.** | Units | ₹ |
|---------|-------|---|---|---------|-------|---|
| To Opening WIP A/c | 400 | 6,000 | | By Finished Stock A/c | 2,500 | 1,38,575 |
| To Materials (Input) | 2,600 | — | | By Closing WIP A/c | 500 | 17,415 |
| To Materials (Added) | — | 78,000 | | | | |
| To Labour | — | 48,000 | | | | |
| To Overheads | — | 24,000 | | | | |
| **Total** | **3,000** | **1,56,000** | | **Total** | **3,000** | **1,55,990** |

> **Note:** Closing WIP value is ₹17,415 (rounded). Difference of ₹10 is absorbed as rounding adjustment in the P&L.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Process Costing Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting Scrap Value Deduction (1–2 marks lost):** Students forget to subtract the scrap value of normal loss from the total cost *before* computing CPU. This inflates the per-unit cost artificially.
> 2. **Confusing Abnormal Loss Valuation (2–3 marks lost):** Abnormal loss is valued at the *full cost per unit of good output* — NOT at scrap value. The scrap value belongs only to normal loss.
> 3. **Mixing Opening WIP Cost in FIFO without Adjustment (1–2 marks lost):** In FIFO, opening WIP cost is kept separate and only the *work done this period* on opening WIP is added to current period EUP. The original cost of opening WIP is added back to the value of completed units separately.
