> 4. **Unit Reconciliation Error (0.5–1 mark lost):** Every Process Account must have **debit units = credit units**. The reconciliation statement must balance — examiners specifically check this.
> 5. **Forgetting to Credit Scrap on the Credit Side of Process Account (1 mark lost):** The credit side must explicitly show "By Normal Loss A/c — Scrap Value" as a separate line item.
> 6. **Wrong Equivalent Unit Percentage for Material (2 marks lost):** If material is added at the *end* of the process, only the *completed* units get 100% material. The closing WIP has 0% material. Many students blindly apply 100% to everything.
> 7. **Not Showing Abnormal Gain Properly (1–2 marks lost):** When output exceeds expected output, abnormal gain is *credited* to Process A/c, NOT shown as a deduction. It also gets transferred to Abnormal Gain A/c in the P&L.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Process Costing (Module 4, PECBT523):**

- **Definition:** Process Costing = cost accumulation for continuous, homogeneous production. Costs averaged over equivalent units.
- **5 Stages:** Identify Process → Determine Units → Ascertain Scrap → Compute CPU → Value Outputs.
- **CPU (No WIP):** $\dfrac{\text{Total Cost} - \text{Scrap of Normal Loss}}{\text{Expected Good Units}}$
- **CPU (Weighted Avg):** $\dfrac{\text{Opening WIP Cost} + \text{Current Period Cost}}{\text{EUP Completed} + \text{EUP Closing WIP}}$
- **CPU (FIFO):** $\dfrac{\text{Current Period Cost Only}}{\text{EUP for Current Period's Work}}$
- **Normal Loss:** Expected, unavoidable → valued at scrap, balance (if any) written off to P&L.
- **Abnormal Loss:** Beyond normal limit → valued at full CPU, debited to Abnormal Loss A/c, then to P&L.
- **Abnormal Gain:** Actual > Expected → valued at full CPU, credited to Abnormal Gain A/c, then to P&L.
- **Equivalent Units:** Convert partial units to "fully completed" units. Material at start = 100% in all units present. Conversion = apply % completion.
- **Transferred-In Cost:** Cost from previous process is treated as material cost of the current process.
- **Two Account Format:** Process A/c and Process B/c run sequentially. The output of one becomes the input of the next.
- **Reconciliation:** Total debits = Total credits (units AND ₹) — examiners always check this.
- **Methods Preference:** KTU defaults to **Weighted Average** unless explicitly stated **FIFO**.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme Weightage:** Process Costing typically carries **14 marks** in ESE (one full Part B question) and **3 marks** in Part A (definition/concept).
- **Real-World Mapping:** Petroleum, cement, chemicals, food processing, paper, paint, beverages — all use Process Costing principles.

> **Final Examiner Mantra:** *"Master the five-stage logic, never forget the scrap deduction, balance the Process Account to the last rupee, and you will own the 14 marks."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marginal Costing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT523 - FINANCIAL AND COST ACCOUNTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Marginal Costing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marginal Costing** is a fundamental managerial accounting technique of cost ascertainment and managerial decision-making in which **only variable costs (marginal costs)** are charged to products, operations, processes, or services as product costs, while **fixed costs are written off in full against the contribution** of the period in which they are incurred.

According to the **Institute of Cost and Management Accountants (ICMA)**, London, *"Marginal Costing is the ascertainment of marginal costs and of the effect on profit of changes in volume or type of output by differentiating between fixed costs and variable costs."*

### The Two Cornerstone Definitions You Must Memorize

| Term | KTU Board Definition |
|------|---------------------|
| **Marginal Cost** | The amount at any given volume of output by which aggregate costs are changed if the volume of output is increased or decreased by one unit. |
| **Contribution** | The difference between Sales and Marginal Cost (Variable Cost). It represents the amount of money left to cover Fixed Costs and generate Profit. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Stall" Model

Imagine you run a tea stall. For every cup of tea you sell:
- You spend **₹10** on milk, sugar, tea leaves, and the cup → this is your **Variable Cost** (changes with every extra cup).
- You pay **₹5,000/month** as rent to the stall owner → this is your **Fixed Cost** (pays you whether you sell 10 cups or 1,000 cups).

If you sell a cup for **₹25**:
- **Contribution per cup** = ₹25 − ₹10 = **₹15**
- This ₹15 first **pays off the rent** (₹5,000), and whatever remains is **pure profit**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pearl:** Under Marginal Costing, we first recover Variable Costs → then Fixed Costs → then Profit is what is left. This sequencing is the single most tested concept in Module 4.

---

## 1.3 Why Marginal Costing is Essential in Engineering Business Decisions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Marginal Costing forms the backbone of **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis** which directly helps managers in:
> - Pricing decisions
> - Make-or-buy decisions
> - Profit planning
> - Determining break-even production levels
> - Evaluating profitability of different product lines
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind Marginal Costing

Under traditional **Absorption Costing**, fixed overheads are *absorbed* into product cost, often distorting profitability at different output levels (e.g., over-absorption during high production). Marginal Costing solves this by:

1. **Strictly segregating costs** into Fixed and Variable components.
2. **Charging only Variable Cost** as the cost of a product.
3. **Treating Fixed Cost as a Period Cost** — charged to the Profit & Loss Account in full.
4. **Valuing Inventory** of finished goods at Marginal Cost only (this is the KTU-favourite numerical question).

### The Three Logical Pillars of Marginal Costing

- **Pillar 1 — Cost Behavior:** Costs are classified by behavior (Fixed vs. Variable) and not by function.
- **Pillar 2 — Contribution Focus:** The key performance metric is *Contribution*, not *Gross Profit*.
- **Pillar 3 — Marginal Equation:** The identity *Sales − Variable Cost = Fixed Cost + Profit* is the foundation of all CVP math.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula tested in KTU ESE** for Marginal Costing. Memorize the symbol, the meaning, and the unit.

| # | KTU Formula | Symbols Explained | Unit / Output |
|---|-------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{Marginal Cost per unit} = \text{Variable Cost per unit}$ | Direct Material + Direct Labour + Direct Expenses + Variable Overheads | ₹/unit |
| 2 | $\text{Contribution} = \text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost}$ | Sales = Selling Price × Units Sold | ₹ |
| 3 | $\text{Contribution per unit} = \text{SP} - \text{VC per unit}$ | SP = Selling Price | ₹/unit |
| 4 | $\text{Profit} = \text{Contribution} - \text{Fixed Cost}$ | Standard identity | ₹ |
| 5 | $\text{P/V Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$ | P/V = Profit Volume | % |
| 6 | $\text{BEP (units)} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Contribution per unit}}$ | BEP = Break-Even Point | Units |
| 7 | $\text{BEP (₹)} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$ | BEP in sales value | ₹ |
| 8 | $\text{Margin of Safety} = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}$ | MoS | ₹ |
| 9 | $\text{MoS Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Margin of Safety}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$ | — | % |
| 10 | $\text{Required Sales (units)} = \dfrac{\text{FC} + \text{Target Profit}}{\text{Contribution per unit}}$ | — | Units |
| 11 | $\text{Required Sales (₹)} = \dfrac{\text{FC} + \text{Target Profit}}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$ | — | ₹ |
| 12 | $\text{Profit} = \text{Contribution} \times \text{P/V Ratio / 100}$ | When Contribution is known | ₹ |
| 13 | $\text{Change in Profit} = \text{Change in Contribution}$ | Because Fixed Cost remains constant | ₹ |

---

## 2.3 Practical Utility in the Engineering Business World

For an engineering manager in a manufacturing firm (say, a CNC machine shop in Kerala), Marginal Costing answers:
- *"If I take a special order at a lower price, will I be profitable?"* (Decision: as long as price > variable cost, it adds to contribution).
- *"What is the minimum number of components I must produce this month to avoid a loss?"* (Decision: BEP calculation).
- *"How much safer is our current production than the break-even point?"* (Decision: Margin of Safety).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Marginal Costing Equation

Let:
- $S$ = Sales Revenue
- $V$ = Total Variable Cost
- $C$ = Total Contribution
- $F$ = Fixed Cost
- $P$ = Profit
- $x$ = Number of units sold
- $p$ = Selling price per unit
- $v$ = Variable cost per unit
- $f$ = Total Fixed Cost
- $c$ = Contribution per unit

**Step 1:** Write the basic profit equation.

$$
P = S - V - F
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that Sales minus Variable Cost equals Contribution.

$$
C = S - V
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Contribution into the profit equation.

$$
P = C - F
$$

**Step 4:** Expand the terms using per-unit variables.

$$
S = p \cdot x, \quad V = v \cdot x
$$

**Step 5:** Express Contribution at the unit level.

$$
c = p - v
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into the contribution equation.

$$
C = c \cdot x
$$

**Step 7:** Final, KTU-favourite form of the Marginal Costing equation.

$$
P = (p - v) \cdot x - f
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Every KTU ESE problem in Marginal Costing is a permutation of this single equation. Memorize it, and you can solve 90% of Module 4 questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Break-Even Point (BEP) Formula

**Step 1:** At Break-Even, Profit ($P$) is **zero**.

$$
P = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Marginal Costing identity.

$$
0 = C - F
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange — Contribution equals Fixed Cost at BEP.

$$
C = F
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $C = c \cdot x_{BE}$ (where $x_{BE}$ = BEP in units).

$$
c \cdot x_{BE} = F
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $x_{BE}$.

$$
x_{BE} = \frac{F}{c}
$$

**Step 6:** For BEP in sales rupees, divide Fixed Cost by the P/V Ratio.

$$
\text{BEP (₹)} = \frac{F}{\text{P/V Ratio}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Comprehensive Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Problem Statement:** A manufacturing company produces a single product. From the following data, calculate **(i)** P/V Ratio, **(ii)** Break-Even Point (in units and ₹), **(iii)** Margin of Safety, **(iv)** Profit when sales are ₹ 6,00,000.
>
> Data:
> - Selling Price per unit = ₹ 50
> - Variable Cost per unit = ₹ 30
> - Fixed Cost = ₹ 4,00,000
> - Actual Sales = 12,000 units

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute Contribution per unit.**

$$
c = p - v = 50 - 30 = 20 \text{ ₹/unit}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Total Sales Revenue.**

$$
S = p \cdot x = 50 \times 12{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Contribution.**

$$
C = c \cdot x = 20 \times 12{,}000 = 2{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4: (i) Compute the P/V Ratio.**

$$
\text{P/V Ratio} = \frac{C}{S} \times 100 = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

**Step 5: (ii-a) Compute BEP in units.**

$$
x_{BE} = \frac{F}{c} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{20} = 20{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 6: (ii-b) Compute BEP in ₹.**

$$
\text{BEP (₹)} = \frac{F}{\text{P/V Ratio}} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{0.40} = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 7: (iii) Compute Margin of Safety (MoS) in ₹.**

$$
\text{MoS} = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales} = 6{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Notice MoS came out **negative**! This means the company is operating **below break-even** and incurring a loss. The correct interpretation is that MoS is the deficit, not a positive safety margin. In KTU valuation, students often ignore the sign and lose 1 mark.

**Step 7 (Corrected Interpretation):** Since Actual Sales (₹6,00,000) < BEP Sales (₹10,00,000), the company is in a loss zone, and the "Margin of Safety" is technically negative ₹4,00,000, indicating a deficit of safety.

**Step 8: (iv) Compute Profit when sales are ₹ 6,00,000.**

$$
P = C - F = 2{,}40{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = -1{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

The company is incurring a **loss of ₹ 1,60,000**.

### Final Summary Table (Board-Answer Style)

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|-------|------|
| Contribution per unit | $c$ | **20** | ₹/unit |
| P/V Ratio | $\rho$ | **40** | % |
| BEP in units | $x_{BE}$ | **20,000** | units |
| BEP in ₹ | $S_{BE}$ | **10,00,000** | ₹ |
| Margin of Safety | MoS | **(4,00,000)** | ₹ |
| Profit / (Loss) | $P$ | **(1,60,000)** | ₹ |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to Choose the Right Formula in the Exam

| Exam Question Wording | Formula to Apply | Expected Output |
|-----------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| "How many units to break even?" | $F \div c$ | Units |
| "What is the break-even sales value?" | $F \div \text{P/V Ratio}$ | ₹ |
| "Compute P/V ratio" | $C / S \times 100$ | % |
| "Sales to earn a target profit of ₹X" | $(F + X) \div c$ (units) or $(F + X) \div \rho$ (₹) | Units or ₹ |
| "Margin of safety" | Actual Sales − BEP Sales | ₹ or % |
| "Inventory value under marginal costing" | Closing Stock × Variable Cost per unit | ₹ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cost Flow under Marginal Costing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Sales Revenue SP times Units Sold"] --> B["Total Variable Cost VC times Units"]
    A --> C["Total Contribution C equals S minus V"]
    B --> C
    C --> D["Fixed Cost Period Cost"]
    C --> E["Operating Profit P equals C minus F"]
    D --> E
    D --> F["Inventory Valuation Closing Stock times VC per unit"]
    B --> F
```

## 4.2 Break-Even Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BELOW["BELOW BREAK-EVEN: LOSS ZONE"]
        direction TB
        L1["Actual Sales less than BEP Sales"] --> L2["Contribution less than Fixed Cost"]
        L2 --> L3["Company Incurs a LOSS"]
    end
    subgraph AT["AT BREAK-EVEN"]
        direction TB
        A1["Actual Sales equals BEP Sales"] --> A2["Contribution equals Fixed Cost"]
        A2 --> A3["Company breaks even: NO PROFIT NO LOSS"]
    end
    subgraph ABOVE["ABOVE BREAK-EVEN: PROFIT ZONE"]
        direction TB
        P1["Actual Sales greater than BEP Sales"] --> P2["Contribution greater than Fixed Cost"]
        P2 --> P3["Company earns a PROFIT equals Contribution minus Fixed Cost"]
    end
    BELOW --> AT --> ABOVE
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The CVP Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1["INPUT: Selling Price per unit p"] --> CORE
    IN2["INPUT: Variable Cost per unit v"] --> CORE
    IN3["INPUT: Total Fixed Cost F"] --> CORE
    IN4["INPUT: Volume of Output x"] --> CORE
    IN5["INPUT: Target Profit P_target optional"] --> CORE
    CORE["CORE ENGINE: Marginal Costing Equation P equals p minus v times x minus F"] --> OUT1["OUTPUT: Profit or Loss in rupees"]
    CORE --> OUT2["OUTPUT: P/V Ratio percentage"]
    CORE --> OUT3["OUTPUT: Break Even Point units and rupees"]
    CORE --> OUT4["OUTPUT: Margin of Safety rupees"]
    CORE --> OUT5["OUTPUT: Required Sales for Target Profit units"]
```

## 4.4 Comparison: Marginal Costing vs Absorption Costing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MC["MARGINAL COSTING"]
        direction LR
        M1["Product Cost = Variable Cost only"] --> M2["Fixed Cost = Period Cost fully written off"]
        M2 --> M3["Inventory valued at VC only"]
        M3 --> M4["Profit = Contribution minus Fixed Cost"]
    end
    subgraph AC["ABSORPTION COSTING"]
        direction LR
        A1["Product Cost = VC plus Absorbed Fixed Overhead"] --> A2["Fixed Cost recovered via overhead recovery rate"]
        A2 --> A3["Inventory valued at full absorption cost"]
        A3 --> A4["Profit based on full cost"]
    end
    MC -.- AC
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define Marginal Costing. List any four limitations of Marginal Costing.**

**Model Answer (Board-Standard):**

> **Marginal Costing** is a method of cost accounting in which only variable costs (marginal costs) are considered as the cost of the product, while fixed costs are treated as period costs and written off against the contribution of the period.

**Four Limitations:**
1. It is difficult to **classify costs strictly** as fixed or variable; most costs are semi-variable.
2. It **ignores the time element** and the principle of matching costs with revenue under accrual accounting.
3. **Long-term pricing decisions** cannot be based on marginal costing alone, as fixed costs are excluded.
4. In the **valuation of inventory**, only variable cost is considered, which may understate the true cost and is not acceptable under financial reporting / tax laws.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Explain the concept of 'P/V Ratio' and its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Profit/Volume (P/V) Ratio** is the ratio of Contribution to Sales, expressed as a percentage.
>
> $$\text{P/V Ratio} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$$
>
> **Significance:**
> 1. It measures the **rate at which contribution is generated per rupee of sales**.
> 2. It helps in **computing break-even sales** by the formula $\text{BEP (₹)} = \dfrac{F}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$.
> 3. It helps in **computing the required sales to earn a target profit**.
> 4. A **higher P/V ratio** indicates greater profitability and lower risk; a **falling P/V ratio** warns of reduced profitability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**A company manufactures and sells a single product. The following data is given for the year ended 31st March 2024:**

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|-------------|------------|
| Selling Price per unit | 200 |
| Variable Cost per unit | 120 |
| Total Fixed Cost | 8,00,000 |
| Actual Sales (units) | 10,000 |

**Required (in 7-mark sub-parts):**

**(a)** Calculate the **P/V Ratio**, **Break-Even Point in units and in rupees**, and the **Margin of Safety in ₹ and as a percentage**.

**(b)** If the management desires a **profit of ₹ 6,00,000**, calculate the **number of units to be sold** and the **required sales revenue**. Also compute the **profit at the actual sales level of 10,000 units**.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Contribution per unit.**

$$
c = p - v = 200 - 120 = 80 \text{ ₹/unit}
$$

**Step 2: Total Sales Revenue.**

$$
S = 200 \times 10{,}000 = 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3: Total Contribution.**

$$
C = 80 \times 10{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4: P/V Ratio.**

$$
\text{P/V Ratio} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

**Step 5: BEP in units.**

$$
x_{BE} = \frac{F}{c} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{80} = 10{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 6: BEP in ₹.**

$$
\text{BEP (₹)} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{0.40} = 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 7: Margin of Safety in ₹.**

$$
\text{MoS} = 20{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000 = 0 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 8: MoS Ratio.**

$$
\text{MoS Ratio} = \frac{0}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 0\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The company is operating **exactly at break-even** — neither profit nor loss.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Required units to earn ₹ 6,00,000 profit.**

$$
x_{\text{required}} = \frac{F + P_{\text{target}}}{c} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000}{80} = \frac{14{,}00{,}000}{80} = 17{,}500 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2: Required sales revenue.**

$$
S_{\text{required}} = \frac{F + P_{\text{target}}}{\text{P/V Ratio}} = \frac{14{,}00{,}000}{0.40} = 35{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3: Profit at actual sales of 10,000 units.**

$$
P = (p - v) \cdot x - f = (200 - 120) \times 10{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
P = 80 \times 10{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 0 \text{ ₹}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Zero profit** confirms that 10,000 units is exactly the BEP volume.

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**A firm provides the following data for two consecutive years:**

| Particulars | Year 1 (₹) | Year 2 (₹) |
|-------------|------------|------------|
| Sales | 10,00,000 | 12,00,000 |
| Profit | 1,00,000 | 2,00,000 |

**Required (in 7-mark sub-parts):**

**(a)** Compute the **P/V Ratio**, **Fixed Cost**, and **Break-Even Point in ₹** for each year. **Comment** on the relationship.

**(b)** If the firm expects to achieve **sales of ₹ 15,00,000** in Year 3, what **profit** is expected? Also find the **Margin of Safety** and **profit at 80% capacity utilization** if full capacity sales = ₹ 15,00,000.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute the change in Sales and Profit.**

$$
\Delta S = 12{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\Delta P = 2{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: P/V Ratio (same in both years since cost structure is unchanged).**

$$
\text{P/V Ratio} = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta S} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

**Step 3: Fixed Cost (using Year 1 data: P = C − F).**

$$
C_{\text{Year 1}} = 50\% \times 10{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
F = C - P = 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4: Break-Even Point in ₹.**

$$
\text{BEP} = \frac{F}{\text{P/V Ratio}} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{0.50} = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Comment:** P/V Ratio (50%) and Fixed Cost (₹ 4,00,000) remain constant, confirming that the cost structure has not changed. Only sales volume has increased, leading to a proportional increase in profit at the same P/V ratio.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Expected profit at sales of ₹ 15,00,000 (Year 3).**

$$
P = S \times \text{P/V Ratio} - F = 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 2: Margin of Safety (full capacity = ₹ 15,00,000).**

$$
\text{MoS} = 15{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3: Sales at 80% capacity utilization.**

$$
S_{80\%} = 0.80 \times 15{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4: Profit at 80% capacity utilization.**

$$
P_{80\%} = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up units and rupees:** Always clearly label whether BEP is in *units* or in *₹*. A frequent 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting the P/V ratio unit:** It is always a **percentage**, not a decimal, in the displayed answer.
> 3. **Negative MoS:** If actual sales < BEP sales, explicitly state the company is "operating below BEP" rather than reporting a meaningless "negative safety".
> 4. **P/V Ratio from two periods:** Use $\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$, **not** the simple ratio in either year, when you are given two years' data.
> 5. **Inventory valuation trap:** Under marginal costing, closing stock is valued at **variable cost only**, NOT total cost — this is a recurring 2-mark trap in KTU ESE.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Marginal Costing (Module 4) — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- **Definition:** Marginal Costing charges **only variable cost** to the product; fixed costs are **period costs**.
- **The Golden Equation:** $\text{Profit} = \text{Contribution} - \text{Fixed Cost}$.
- **Contribution = Sales − Variable Cost** (this is the heart of marginal costing).
- **P/V Ratio = Contribution / Sales × 100%** — measures profitability per rupee of sales.
- **BEP (units) = Fixed Cost / Contribution per unit.**
- **BEP (₹) = Fixed Cost / P/V Ratio.**
- **MoS = Actual Sales − BEP Sales**; **MoS Ratio = MoS / Actual Sales × 100.**
- **Required Sales (₹) = (Fixed Cost + Target Profit) / P/V Ratio.**
- **When sales increase by ₹X, profit increases by ₹(X × P/V Ratio)** — because fixed cost remains constant.
- **Two-year P/V ratio trick:** $\text{P/V Ratio} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S} \times 100$.
- **Inventory under Marginal Costing** is valued at **Variable Cost only** — this creates the difference from Absorption Costing profits.
- **Key Limitation:** Difficult to classify costs strictly as fixed or variable in real-world industries.
- **Engineering Relevance:** Used in **make-or-buy, pricing, breakeven planning, and product-mix** decisions in manufacturing firms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Absorption Costing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT523 - FINANCIAL AND COST ACCOUNTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Absorption Costing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absorption Costing (also known as Full Costing or Total Costing)** is a cost accounting method in which **all manufacturing costs** — both **variable** and **fixed** — are absorbed/charged to the cost units (products) irrespective of their level of activity. Under this method, the cost of a product includes: **Direct Materials + Direct Labour + Direct Expenses + Variable Manufacturing Overhead + Fixed Manufacturing Overhead.**

According to **CIMA (Chartered Institute of Management Accountants)**, absorption costing attributes both fixed and variable production overheads to products/services using a predetermined overhead absorption rate (OAR).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Absorption Costing is the **default method prescribed under Indian Accounting Standard (AS-2)** and **Ind AS-2** for valuation of inventories. It is therefore **mandatory for financial reporting** and **statutory valuation of stock**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you run a **small bakery** producing bread loaves.

- **Direct Materials** → Flour, yeast, salt (₹10 per loaf)
- **Direct Labour** → Baker's wages (₹5 per loaf)
- **Variable Overhead** → Electricity used by the oven that runs only when baking (₹2 per loaf)
- **Fixed Overhead** → Rent of the bakery, salaried manager, depreciation of the oven (₹30,000 per month) — this is paid **whether you bake 1 loaf or 10,000 loaves**.

Under **Absorption Costing**, the rent of the bakery (₹30,000) is *sliced* and *spread* across every loaf produced. If you bake **10,000 loaves** in a month, then each loaf absorbs **₹3** of rent (₹30,000 ÷ 10,000). If you bake only **5,000 loaves**, each loaf absorbs **₹6** of rent.

> [!TIP]
> Think of **Absorption Costing as a "shared-ride taxi"** — the fixed cab fare is split among all passengers. The fewer the passengers, the more each one pays; the more the passengers, the less each one pays. The fixed cost (rent of the cab) is **absorbed** by the riders (products).

The key insight: **In Absorption Costing, the cost of a single unit fluctuates with the production volume** because fixed costs get re-distributed.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology Block

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary You Must Memorize for KTU Exams:**

- **Cost Unit:** The unit of product/service for which cost is ascertained (e.g., one bread loaf, one machine-hour).
- **Cost Centre:** A location, function, or item of equipment in respect of which costs may be ascertained (e.g., the Bakery Department).
- **Overhead Absorption:** The process of charging/attributing overheads to cost units via a logical basis.
- **Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR):** The rate at which overheads are charged per unit of a chosen activity base.
- **Under-Absorption:** When overheads actually incurred > overheads absorbed.
- **Over-Absorption:** When overheads actually incurred < overheads absorbed.
- **Normal Costing:** A variant of absorption costing where OAR is based on *normal* (long-run average) capacity.
- **Actual Costing:** A variant where actual overheads and actual activity are used.
- **Standard Absorption Costing:** A variant where OAR is based on *standard* costs.

---

## 1.4 Standards and Real-World Application

| Standard / Framework | Stance on Absorption Costing |
|---|---|
| **AS-2 (India)** | **Mandatory** for inventory valuation in financial statements. |
| **Ind AS-2** | **Mandatory** — fixed and variable production overheads must be absorbed. |
| **IFRS** | Requires **absorption of fixed overheads** based on normal capacity. |
| **CIMA Terminology** | Defines it formally as a method of cost ascertainment. |
| **Management Decision-Making** | **Marginal Costing** is preferred (for break-even, decision-making), but **Absorption Costing** is mandatory for **external financial reporting**. |

---

## 1.5 The Three Components of Product Cost

| Cost Component | Behaviour | Treatment under Absorption Costing |
|---|---|---|
| **Prime Cost** (DM + DL + Direct Exp.) | Variable | Fully charged to product |
| **Variable Mfg. Overhead** | Variable | Fully charged to product |
| **Fixed Mfg. Overhead** | **Fixed** | **Absorbed** to product via OAR |

> [!WARNING]
> **Only MANUFACTURING overheads (fixed) are absorbed.** Selling, distribution, and administration overheads are **period costs** — they are written off to the **Profit & Loss A/c** in the period they are incurred, NOT absorbed into product cost. This is a very common KTU exam pitfall.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — How Absorption Costing Works

The methodology follows a precise sequential logic:

### Step 1: Classification of Costs
Separate every cost into one of three buckets:
- **Direct Costs** (Direct Materials, Direct Labour, Direct Expenses)
- **Variable Manufacturing Overheads** (e.g., indirect materials, indirect labour tied to output)
- **Fixed Manufacturing Overheads** (e.g., factory rent, depreciation, factory supervisor salary)

### Step 2: Selection of an Absorption Base
A base (also called *cost driver* or *activity base*) is selected. Common bases:
- **Units produced** (suitable for single-product firms)
- **Direct Labour Hours (DLH)**
- **Machine Hours (MH)**
- **Direct Labour Cost (₹)**
- **Prime Cost (₹)**
- **Standard Hours**

### Step 3: Computation of Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OAR** is the *per-unit* rate at which overheads are loaded onto the product. There are **three variants** of OAR — all are high-yield KTU questions.

### Step 4: Charging to Cost Units
Apply the OAR to actual production activity to compute the **absorbed overhead**.

### Step 5: Reconciliation
Compare *absorbed overhead* with *actual overhead incurred* to identify **under/over-absorption**, and adjust the difference in the P&L account.

---

## 2.2 Methods of Computing Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR)

| Method | Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual (Historical) Costing** | $\text{OAR} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Overhead}}{\text{Actual Base}}$ | Post-mortem; **not for pricing** (after-the-fact) |
| **Normal Costing** | $\text{OAR} = \dfrac{\text{Estimated Overhead}}{\text{Normal Capacity}}$ | Most common in practice |
| **Standard Costing** | $\text{OAR} = \dfrac{\text{Standard Overhead}}{\text{Standard Hours}}$ | Used in standard costing systems |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — Every formula below has appeared in KTU exams.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Product Cost (per unit)** | $\text{Cost} = DM + DL + \text{Var. MOH} + \text{Fixed MOH absorbed}$ | — |
| 2 | **Fixed OAR (per unit)** | $\text{OAR} = \dfrac{\text{Estimated Fixed Overhead}}{\text{Estimated Base}}$ | — |
| 3 | **Absorbed Overhead** | $\text{Absorbed OH} = \text{OAR} \times \text{Actual Base}$ | — |
| 4 | **Under-Absorption** | $\text{Under-abs.} = \text{Actual OH} - \text{Absorbed OH} \quad (\text{positive})$ | $U > 0$ |
| 5 | **Over-Absorption** | $\text{Over-abs.} = \text{Absorbed OH} - \text{Actual OH} \quad (\text{positive})$ | $O > 0$ |
| 6 | **Profit (Absorption)** | $\text{Profit} = \text{Sales} - \text{COGS} - \text{Selling \& Admin Exp.}$ | COGS uses **full** cost |
| 7 | **Reconciliation Adjustment** | $\text{Adj.} = \pm (\text{Over/Under-absorption of OH})$ | Added to / deducted from MC profit |
| 8 | **Stock Valuation** | $\text{Closing Stock} = \text{Units} \times \text{Full Unit Cost}$ | — |
| 9 | **Fixed OH in Closing Stock** | $\text{Fixed OH carry forward} = \text{Units unsold} \times \text{Fixed OH per unit}$ | Deferred to next period |
| 10 | **Profit Reconciliation** | $\text{AP} = \text{MP} + \text{Fixed OH in Cl. Stock} - \text{Fixed OH in Op. Stock}$ | AP = Absorption Profit, MP = Marginal Profit |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM RULE:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ inside markdown tables will break the table. The formula sheet above uses NO pipes inside formulas — strictly safe.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why does an engineering manager (or B.Tech student) need Absorption Costing?**

- **Inventory Valuation:** Mandated by law for stock valuation in financial statements. Companies cannot show products in the balance sheet at "marginal cost" — that would understate asset value.
- **Product Pricing:** For long-term pricing decisions, management must know the **full cost** to ensure all costs (including fixed) are recovered.
- **Cost Control:** Comparing absorbed vs. actual overheads reveals efficiency (or inefficiency) in production.
- **Tender / Quotation Pricing:** Government contracts and industrial tenders require full-cost quotations, not just marginal cost.
- **Engineering Economics:** Projects, equipment replacement, and make-or-buy decisions often use full cost as the baseline.
- **Job-Costing in Manufacturing:** Machine shops, foundries, fabrication units — all use OAR (per machine-hour) to quote prices.

---

## 2.5 Difference Between Absorption Costing and Marginal Costing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **two-mark guaranteed KTU question** every year.

| Basis | Absorption Costing | Marginal Costing |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Overhead Treatment** | Absorbed into product cost | Treated as period cost |
| **Stock Valuation** | Includes fixed OH | Excludes fixed OH |
| **Profit Fluctuation** | Fluctuates with **production** | Fluctuates with **sales** |
| **Decision-Making Suitability** | Unsuitable (carries inventory distortion) | **Highly suitable** |
| **Reporting Mandate** | Mandatory (AS-2 / Ind AS-2) | Not permitted for external reporting |
| **Break-Even Analysis** | Not feasible (fixed OH per unit varies) | **Ideal** |
| **Treatment of Inventory** | Carry forward of fixed OH deferred to next period | Inventory valued at variable cost only |

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Full Income Statement under Absorption Costing

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the standard 14-mark KTU question pattern. Master this numerical.**

### Problem Statement

A manufacturing company furnishes the following data for the year ending 31st March 2024:

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Sales (10,000 units @ ₹50) | 5,00,000 |
| Direct Materials | 1,00,000 |
| Direct Labour | 50,000 |
| Variable Manufacturing Overhead | 30,000 |
| Fixed Manufacturing Overhead | 1,20,000 |
| Selling & Distribution Overhead (Variable) | 20,000 |
| Selling & Distribution Overhead (Fixed) | 40,000 |
| Opening Stock | NIL |
| Closing Stock | 2,000 units |

**Required:** Prepare an **Absorption Costing Income Statement**.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the Fixed OAR and Full Unit Cost**

Since opening stock is NIL, total production = sales + closing stock = 10,000 + 2,000 = **12,000 units**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fixed OAR (per unit)} &= \frac{\text{Total Fixed MOH}}{\text{Units Produced}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{12{,}000} = \text{₹ 10 per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Full Unit Cost (per unit)} &= \text{DM} + \text{DL} + \text{Var. MOH} + \text{Fixed MOH per unit} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{12{,}000} + \frac{50{,}000}{12{,}000} + \frac{30{,}000}{12{,}000} + 10 \\[4pt]
&= 8.333 + 4.167 + 2.500 + 10.000 \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹ 25 per unit (rounded)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Prepare the Income Statement**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Absorption Costing Income Statement for the year ended 31.03.2024} \\[4pt]
\hline
\text{Sales (10,000 units @ ₹50)} \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad & = \text{₹ } 5{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Less: Cost of Goods Sold:} \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad & \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Opening Stock} \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad & = \text{₹ } \;\; \text{Nil} \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Add: Production Cost (12,000 units @ ₹25)} \quad & = \text{₹ } 3{,}00{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Less: Closing Stock (2,000 units @ ₹25)} \quad & = \text{₹ } \;\; 50{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\quad \textbf{Cost of Goods Sold} \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad & = \text{₹ } 2{,}50{,}000 \\[4pt]
\hline
\textbf{Gross Profit} \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad & = \text{₹ } 2{,}50{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Less: Selling & Distribution OH (Variable)} \quad & = \text{₹ } \;\; 20{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Selling & Distribution OH (Fixed)} \quad\quad\quad & = \text{₹ } \;\; 40{,}000 \\[4pt]
\hline
\textbf{Net Profit} \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad & = \text{₹ } 1{,}90{,}000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** Always present your solution in a **vertical statement format** with clear sub-totals. Examiners award step-marks for layout.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Treatment of Under/Over-Absorption of Overheads

### Problem Statement

A company's estimated annual overheads are **₹4,00,000**, and the company operates for **50,000 normal direct labour hours**. During the year, the actual overheads incurred were **₹4,20,000**, and the actual hours worked were **48,000 hours**.

**Required:** Compute the OAR, absorbed overhead, and the under/over-absorption.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the OAR (using normal costing)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OAR} &= \frac{\text{Estimated Overhead}}{\text{Normal Hours}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹ 8 per direct labour hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Absorbed Overhead**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Absorbed OH} &= \text{OAR} \times \text{Actual Hours Worked} \\[4pt]
&= 8 \times 48{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹ 3{,}84{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Identify Under/Over-Absorption**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual OH Incurred} &= \text{₹ } 4{,}20{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Absorbed OH} &= \text{₹ } 3{,}84{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Difference} &= 4{,}20{,}000 - 3{,}84{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹ 36{,}000 (Under-Absorbed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Remember the direction of the arrow:** If **Actual > Absorbed** → **Under-Absorbed** (we did not charge enough). If **Absorbed > Actual** → **Over-Absorbed** (we charged too much). The formula is:
> $$\text{Under-Absorption} = \text{Actual} - \text{Absorbed} \quad (\text{if positive})$$

**Step 4: Accounting Treatment of Under-Absorption**

In the **Costing P&L Account**, the under-absorption of ₹36,000 is **added to** the cost of sales (since cost was understated) — thereby **reducing profit** by ₹36,000.

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative Methods of Disposal of Under/Over-Absorption:**
> 1. **Carry Forward** to next period (added/deducted from next year's overhead)
> 2. **Write-off to P&L** in the same period (most common — KTU preferred)
> 3. **Use Supplementary Rate** (adjust OAR for the next period)

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Profit Reconciliation (Absorption vs. Marginal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This 14-mark question is the MOST FREQUENT in KTU Module 4. Master the reconciliation logic.**

### Problem Statement

A company provides the following data:

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Sales (20,000 units @ ₹40) | 8,00,000 |
| Direct Material | 1,60,000 |
| Direct Labour | 80,000 |
| Variable Production Overhead | 1,20,000 |
| Fixed Production Overhead | 2,40,000 |
| Variable Selling Overhead | 40,000 |
| Fixed Selling Overhead | 60,000 |
| Opening Stock (units) | 4,000 |
| Closing Stock (units) | 6,000 |

**Required:** (a) Prepare **Marginal Costing** Income Statement. (b) Prepare **Absorption Costing** Income Statement. (c) **Reconcile** the two profits.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute Production Volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production} &= \text{Sales} + \text{Closing Stock} - \text{Opening Stock} \\[4pt]
&= 20{,}000 + 6{,}000 - 4{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \text{22,000 units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Fixed OAR (Absorption Costing)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fixed OAR} &= \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{22{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹ 10.9091 per unit (≈ ₹10.91)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Variable Cost per Unit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Variable Production Cost per unit} &= \frac{1{,}60{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000}{22{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3{,}60{,}000}{22{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹ 16.3636 per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Full Cost per Unit (for Opening and Closing Stock in Absorption)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Full Cost per unit} &= 16.3636 + 10.9091 \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹ 27.2727 per unit (≈ ₹27.27)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For simplicity, KTU exams usually round to integers. Let us use **₹27 per unit (approx.)** for the closing stock.

---

**Part (a) — Marginal Costing Income Statement**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales (20,000 @ ₹40)} &= 8{,}00{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Variable Cost of Sales:} \quad & \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Opening Stock (4,000 @ ₹16.36)} &= 65{,}454 \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Add: Variable Production Cost} &= 3{,}60{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Less: Closing Stock (6,000 @ ₹16.36)} &= 98{,}182 \\[-2pt]
\quad \textbf{Variable COGS} &= 3{,}27{,}272 \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Add: Variable Selling OH} &= 40{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\quad \textbf{Total Variable Cost} &= 3{,}67{,}272 \\[-2pt]
\hline
\textbf{Contribution} &= 4{,}32{,}728 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Fixed Production OH} &= 2{,}40{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Fixed Selling OH} &= 60{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\hline
\textbf{Marginal Costing Profit} &= \text{₹ 1{,}32{,}728} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Absorption Costing Income Statement**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales} &= 8{,}00{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Full Cost of Sales:} \quad & \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Opening Stock (4,000 @ ₹27.27)} &= 1{,}09{,}091 \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Add: Full Production Cost (22,000 @ ₹27.27)} &= 6{,}00{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Less: Closing Stock (6,000 @ ₹27.27)} &= 1{,}63{,}636 \\[-2pt]
\quad \textbf{COGS} &= 5{,}45{,}455 \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Add: Selling OH (Variable + Fixed)} &= 1{,}00{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\hline
\textbf{Absorption Costing Profit} &= \text{₹ 1{,}54{,}545} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (c) — Reconciliation Statement**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Profit Reconciliation} \\[4pt]
\hline
\text{Marginal Costing Profit} &= 1{,}32{,}728 \\[-2pt]
\text{Add: Fixed OH absorbed in Closing Stock} \quad & \\[-2pt]
\quad (6{,}000 \text{ units} \times ₹10.91) &= 65{,}455 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Fixed OH in Opening Stock} \quad & \\[-2pt]
\quad (4{,}000 \text{ units} \times ₹10.91) &= 43{,}636 \\[-2pt]
\hline
\textbf{Absorption Costing Profit} &= \text{₹ 1{,}54{,}547} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Reconciliation Identity to Memorize:**
> $$\boxed{\text{Absorption Profit} = \text{Marginal Profit} + \text{Fixed OH in Cl. Stock} - \text{Fixed OH in Op. Stock}}$$
>
> This **single identity** lets you reconcile any KTU problem in under 60 seconds.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When Does Absorption Profit Exceed Marginal Profit?

| Condition | Effect on Inventory | Effect on Absorption vs. Marginal Profit |
|---|---|---|
| Production > Sales | Inventory **increases** | **AP > MP** (fixed OH is deferred in closing stock) |
| Production < Sales | Inventory **decreases** | **AP < MP** (fixed OH released from opening stock) |
| Production = Sales | Inventory **unchanged** | **AP = MP** (no timing difference) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a classic 3-mark conceptual question.** Memorize: *"If production exceeds sales, absorption costing shows higher profit because some fixed overheads are 'hidden' in closing inventory and are not charged to the current period."*

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cost Classification Flow — Inputs to Absorption Costing System

This diagram maps how raw cost data is classified and routed into the absorption costing pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Costs Incurred in the Period]:::startNode
    A --> B[Direct Costs]
    A --> C[Production Overheads]
    A --> D[Selling, Distribution and Admin Overheads]
    B --> B1[Direct Materials]
    B --> B2[Direct Labour]
    B --> B3[Direct Expenses]
    C --> C1[Variable Production Overheads]
    C --> C2[Fixed Production Overheads]
    D --> D1[Variable SDA]
    D --> D2[Fixed SDA]
    B1 --> E1[Product Cost per Unit]
    B2 --> E1
    B3 --> E1
    C1 --> E1
    C2 --> F[Compute OAR]
    F --> G[Apply OAR to Actual Base]
    G --> E1
    E1 --> H1[Closing Stock Valuation]
    E1 --> H2[Cost of Goods Sold]
    H2 --> I[Profit and Loss Account]
    D1 --> I
    D2 --> I
    H1 --> J[Balance Sheet Inventory]
    K[Actual Overhead Incurred]:::compareNode
    G --> L[Compare Absorbed vs Actual]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Under or Over Absorption?}
    M -->|Actual greater| N[Under-Absorption Charge to P and L]
    M -->|Absorbed greater| O[Over-Absorption Credit to P and L]
    N --> I
    O --> I
    classDef startNode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#1f2937
    classDef compareNode fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1f2937
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram top-to-bottom:** Raw costs enter at the top, get classified, get absorbed via OAR, and are then routed to (1) Closing Stock, (2) COGS, and (3) P&L Account. The under/over-absorption reconciliation loop runs in parallel.
