# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define Absorption Costing. State two of its advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Absorption Costing is a method of cost accounting in which all manufacturing costs — both **fixed and variable** — are charged to cost units/products through a predetermined overhead absorption rate. The cost of a product = Direct Materials + Direct Labour + Variable Manufacturing Overhead + Fixed Manufacturing Overhead (absorbed).

> **Advantages (1 mark for any two):**
> 1. It is **mandated by accounting standards (AS-2 / Ind AS-2)** for stock valuation in financial statements, ensuring compliance.
> 2. It **captures full cost**, which is essential for long-term pricing decisions and ensuring all costs are recovered.
> 3. It helps in **preparing realistic tender quotations** that include all overheads.
> 4. It enables **accurate inventory valuation** as fixed overheads are included in the closing stock.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**What is meant by Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR)? List any two bases used for absorbing overheads.**

**Model Answer:**

> **OAR (1.5 marks):** Overhead Absorption Rate is the rate at which manufacturing overheads are charged/burdened to cost units. It is computed by dividing the estimated overhead by the estimated absorption base. Formula: $\text{OAR} = \dfrac{\text{Estimated Overhead}}{\text{Estimated Base}}$.

> **Two Bases (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
> 1. **Direct Labour Hours (DLH)** — most common in labour-intensive industries.
> 2. **Machine Hours (MH)** — preferred in capital-intensive/process industries.
> 3. **Direct Labour Cost (₹)** — used when labour rates vary widely.
> 4. **Units Produced** — simple, single-product scenarios.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must answer **either** the full Question A **or** the full Question B.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

A manufacturing company provides the following data for the year 2023-24:

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Sales (8,000 units @ ₹80) | 6,40,000 |
| Direct Material (per unit) | 15 |
| Direct Labour (per unit) | 10 |
| Variable Production Overhead (per unit) | 5 |
| Fixed Production Overhead (total) | 1,60,000 |
| Variable Selling Overhead (per unit sold) | 4 |
| Fixed Selling Overhead (total) | 80,000 |
| Opening Stock (units) | 1,000 |
| Closing Stock (units) | 3,000 |

**(a) Prepare an Absorption Costing Income Statement showing the net profit for the year. (7 marks)**

**(b) Prepare a Marginal Costing Income Statement and reconcile the profits of the two methods. (7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — Absorption Costing Income Statement (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Production Volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production} &= \text{Sales} + \text{Closing} - \text{Opening} \\[4pt]
&= 8{,}000 + 3{,}000 - 1{,}000 = \text{10,000 units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Per Unit Costs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct Material} &= ₹15.00 \\[-2pt]
\text{Direct Labour} &= ₹10.00 \\[-2pt]
\text{Variable MOH} &= ₹5.00 \\[-2pt]
\text{Fixed MOH (₹1,60,000 / 10,000)} &= ₹16.00 \\[-2pt]
\hline
\textbf{Full Cost per unit} &= \text{₹ 46.00}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Income Statement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales (8,000 @ ₹80)} &= 6{,}40{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: COGS} \quad & \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Opening Stock (1,000 @ ₹46)} &= \;\; 46{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Add: Production (10,000 @ ₹46)} &= 4{,}60{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Less: Closing Stock (3,000 @ ₹46)} &= 1{,}38{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\quad \textbf{COGS} &= 3{,}68{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\hline
\textbf{Gross Profit} &= 2{,}72{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Variable Selling OH (8,000 @ ₹4)} &= \;\; 32{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Fixed Selling OH} &= \;\; 80{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\hline
\textbf{Net Profit (Absorption)} &= \text{₹ 1{,}60{,}000} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — Marginal Costing + Reconciliation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Variable Cost per Unit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Variable Cost per unit} &= 15 + 10 + 5 = \text{₹ 30}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Marginal Costing Income Statement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales (8,000 @ ₹80)} &= 6{,}40{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Variable COGS} \quad & \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Opening Stock (1,000 @ ₹30)} &= \;\; 30{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Add: Variable Prod. Cost (10,000 @ ₹30)} &= 3{,}00{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Less: Closing Stock (3,000 @ ₹30)} &= \;\; 90{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\quad \textbf{Variable COGS} &= 2{,}40{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\quad \text{Add: Variable Selling OH (8,000 @ ₹4)} &= \;\; 32{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\quad \textbf{Total Variable Cost} &= 2{,}72{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\hline
\textbf{Contribution} &= 3{,}68{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Fixed Production OH} &= 1{,}60{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Fixed Selling OH} &= \;\; 80{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\hline
\textbf{Net Profit (Marginal)} &= \text{₹ 1{,}28{,}000} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Profit Reconciliation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Marginal Profit} &= 1{,}28{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Add: Fixed OH in Cl. Stock (3,000 @ ₹16)} &= \;\; 48{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Fixed OH in Op. Stock (1,000 @ ₹16)} &= \;\; 16{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\hline
\textbf{Absorption Profit} &= \text{₹ 1{,}60{,}000} \;\; \checkmark \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** ₹1,60,000 (Absorption) = ₹1,28,000 (Marginal) + ₹48,000 − ₹16,000 = ₹1,60,000 ✓

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Set]

The estimated overheads of a factory for the year are **₹6,00,000** for a normal capacity of **30,000 direct labour hours**. The actual data for the year are as follows:

- Actual Overheads Incurred: **₹5,80,000**
- Actual Hours Worked: **28,000 hours**
- Sales: 25,000 units @ ₹60
- Variable Cost per unit: ₹25
- Opening Stock: 2,000 units (valued at ₹40 each under absorption)

**(a) Compute the OAR, Absorbed Overhead, and the amount of under/over-absorption. (7 marks)**

**(b) Prepare a Marginal Costing P&L Account assuming closing stock is 5,000 units and reconcile it with the absorption profit. (7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — OAR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OAR} &= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{30{,}000} = \text{₹ 20 per direct labour hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Absorbed Overhead:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Absorbed OH} &= 20 \times 28{,}000 = \text{₹ 5{,}60{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Under/Over-Absorption:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual OH} &= 5{,}80{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Absorbed OH} &= 5{,}60{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Difference} &= 5{,}80{,}000 - 5{,}60{,}000 = \text{₹ 20{,}000 (Under-Absorbed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

> Since Actual > Absorbed, overheads are **under-absorbed by ₹20,000**. The cost of sales was understated by ₹20,000; hence, the P&L must be debited by ₹20,000 to write off the under-absorption.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Production:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production} &= \text{Sales} + \text{Closing} - \text{Opening} \\[4pt]
&= 25{,}000 + 5{,}000 - 2{,}000 = \text{28,000 units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Marginal Costing P&L:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales (25,000 @ ₹60)} &= 15{,}00{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Variable COGS (28,000 @ ₹25)} &= 7{,}00{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Op. Stock adj. (2,000 @ ₹25 - 2,000 @ ₹40 already in COGS)} &= \text{Note: revalue}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *For clarity, treat the variable P&L directly:*
>
> **Contribution = Sales − Variable Cost of Sales − Variable Selling OH (assumed nil)**
> = 15,00,000 − [(2,000 × 25) + (28,000 × 25) − (5,000 × 25)]
> = 15,00,000 − (50,000 + 7,00,000 − 1,25,000) = 15,00,000 − 6,25,000 = **₹8,75,000**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Contribution} &= 8{,}75{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Fixed Production OH (actual)} &= 5{,}80{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\hline
\textbf{Marginal Profit} &= \text{₹ 2{,}95{,}000} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Reconciliation with Absorption Profit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Marginal Profit} &= 2{,}95{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Add: Fixed OH in Cl. Stock (5,000 @ ₹20)} &= 1{,}00{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Less: Fixed OH in Op. Stock (2,000 @ ₹20)} &= \;\; 40{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\text{Add: Under-absorption adjustment} &= \;\; 20{,}000 \\[-2pt]
\hline
\textbf{Absorption Profit (Adjusted)} &= \text{₹ 3{,}75{,}000} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the under-absorption adjustment?** The reconciliation uses the *absorbed* overhead in stock; the under-absorption of ₹20,000 must be added to MC profit to arrive at the true absorption profit.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes KTU Students Make in Absorption Costing Problems:**
>
> 1. **Treating Selling/Distribution/Admin overheads as product cost** — these are PERIOD costs and must be written off in the P&L. Only *manufacturing* fixed overheads are absorbed.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to adjust under/over-absorption** — examiners specifically test this. The unadjusted profit is *not* the final answer.
>
> 3. **Using sales units instead of production units** to compute Fixed OAR — OAR is always Production ÷ Production, NOT Sales ÷ Production.
>
> 4. **Ignoring opening stock in the reconciliation** — the formula is: $\text{AP} = \text{MP} + \text{FOH in Cl. Stock} - \text{FOH in Op. Stock}$. Missing opening stock = -3 marks.
>
> 5. **Not presenting the Income Statement vertically with sub-totals** — KTU examiners award layout marks. Use a clear **vertical format** with proper headings and sub-headings.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ **Absorption Costing = Full Costing.** All manufacturing costs (variable + fixed) are absorbed into products.
- ✅ **Only manufacturing fixed OHs are absorbed**; SDA overheads are period costs.
- ✅ **OAR** = Estimated Overhead ÷ Estimated Base (use Normal Capacity for normal costing).
- ✅ **Under-Absorption** occurs when Actual OH > Absorbed OH (write off to P&L, debit).
- ✅ **Over-Absorption** occurs when Absorbed OH > Actual OH (credit to P&L).
- ✅ **Reconciliation Identity:** $\text{AP} = \text{MP} + \text{FOH}_{\text{Cl. Stock}} - \text{FOH}_{\text{Op. Stock}}$
- ✅ **Production > Sales** → AP > MP (fixed OH deferred in stock).
- ✅ **Production < Sales** → AP < MP (fixed OH released from opening stock).
- ✅ **Production = Sales** → AP = MP.
- ✅ **Mandatory for financial reporting** under AS-2 and Ind AS-2.
- ✅ **Unsuitable for short-term decision-making** — Marginal Costing is preferred for CVP/BEP analysis.
- ✅ **Common bases for OAR:** DLH, Machine Hours, Direct Labour Cost, Units Produced.
- ✅ **Disposal methods of under/over-absorption:** (i) Write-off to P&L, (ii) Carry forward, (iii) Supplementary rate.
- ✅ **Income Statement Format (Absorption):** Sales − COGS = Gross Profit − Selling OH = Net Profit.
- ✅ **Income Statement Format (Marginal):** Sales − Variable Cost = Contribution − Fixed Cost = Net Profit.
- ✅ **CIMA definition** of absorption costing is the gold standard for KTU exam answers.
- ✅ **Remember the difference between "Normal Costing" and "Standard Costing"** OAR.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Absorption Costing.** These notes align with the **KTU 2024 Scheme, Course PECBT523, Module 4**. For maximum marks in KTU 2024 ESE: (1) Present vertical statements with sub-totals, (2) State definitions using CIMA terminology, (3) Show all formula derivations, (4) Always reconcile profits when comparing methods, (5) Mention statutory compliance (AS-2 / Ind AS-2) for context marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Cost Sheet (Simple Problems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT523 - FINANCIAL AND COST ACCOUNTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Cost Sheet (Simple Problems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost Sheet (KTU Definition):** A **Cost Sheet** is a formal, comprehensive, and classified statement or document that presents in a tabular/analytical form the total cost of production, the cost of goods sold, and the cost of sales for a particular accounting period, after aggregating, grouping, and apportioning the various elements of cost (Material, Labour, and Expenses) into homogeneous cost pools such as Prime Cost, Works Cost, Cost of Production, Cost of Goods Sold, and Cost of Sales.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECBT523 / Module 4):** The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to *prepare* a cost sheet from given transactions, identify the *classification* of each cost item, and compute the *Selling Price / Profit* using absorption costing principles. Numerical problems typically involve 8–14 transactions to be categorised correctly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cake Factory"

Imagine you run a home-based **cake-baking business**. To bake one cake, you need:

| Real-World Ingredient | Accounting Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Flour, sugar, eggs (directly traceable to ONE cake) | **Direct Material** |
| The baker's wages for baking that specific cake | **Direct Labour (Wages)** |
| Fuel for the oven, cake-box wrapper (used specifically for that cake) | **Direct Expenses** |
| Factory rent, supervisor's salary, electricity of the bakery | **Factory / Production Overheads** |
| Office rent, accountant's salary, stationery | **Administrative / Office Overheads** |
| Delivery van, salesman commission, advertisement | **Selling & Distribution Overheads** |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Just as you add up the cost of every ingredient and labour involved, layer by layer, to find the *true cost* of one cake, a cost sheet **adds (and adjusts stock of)** each cost element layer-by-layer to arrive at the **true cost of the product**. Without it, you cannot price your cake correctly or know your profit.

### 1.3 Standard Cost Elements (As per KTU / CIMA Terminology)

The three **Primary Elements of Cost** are:
1. **Material** — the physical inputs that become part of the product.
2. **Labour** — the human effort directly applied to convert material into the product.
3. **Expenses** — all other costs incurred (excluding Material and Labour) for operating the business.

These are further classified as **Direct** (traceable) or **Indirect** (overheads, untraceable).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Structure of a Cost Sheet — The Cost-Building Logic

The cost sheet is built in **ascending layers**. Each layer adds a new category of indirect cost (overhead) to the running total. This logic is the **single most important concept** for solving KTU problems.

**Layer-by-Layer Build-Up:**

- **Step 1 — Direct Material Consumed**
  Opening Stock of Raw Material + Purchases of Raw Material − Closing Stock of Raw Material − Purchase Returns = **Direct Material Consumed**.
  *Why?* Because only the material that is *actually used* in production forms part of cost.

- **Step 2 — Direct Labour / Wages**
  All wages paid to workers who *physically convert* raw material into finished product.
  *Why?* Without direct labour there is no conversion of material.

- **Step 3 — Direct / Chargeable Expenses**
  Special expenses that are 100 % attributable to the product (e.g., hire of a special machine, royalty, patterns, dies).

- **Step 4 — PRIME COST (D + D + D)**
  Prime Cost = Direct Material Consumed + Direct Labour + Direct Expenses.
  *Why?* This is the *bare-minimum* cost that *must* be incurred to produce one unit, ignoring factory rent, supervision, etc.

- **Step 5 — Add: Factory / Works / Manufacturing Overheads**
  Indirect material, indirect labour, and indirect expenses incurred *inside the factory* up to the point of production.
  *Why?* A factory cannot run without supervisors, electricity, depreciation of plant — these are necessary to convert material.

- **Step 6 — WORKS / FACTORY COST**
  Works Cost = Prime Cost + Factory Overheads.

- **Step 7 — Add: Office / Administrative Overheads**
  Expenses of the *head office* (general manager, accountants, stationery of accounts dept., office rent).
  *Why?* Even a factory needs centralised administration.

- **Step 8 — COST OF PRODUCTION (Office Cost)**
  Cost of Production = Works Cost + Administrative Overheads.

- **Step 9 — Adjust: Stock of Finished Goods**
  Add Opening Stock of Finished Goods − Closing Stock of Finished Goods.
  *Why?* Not all units produced in the period may be sold; we must match cost with units *sold*.

- **Step 10 — COST OF GOODS SOLD**
  Cost of Goods Sold = Cost of Production + Opening FG Stock − Closing FG Stock.

- **Step 11 — Add: Selling & Distribution Overheads**
  Cost of getting the product *from the warehouse to the buyer's hands* — packing, carriage outward, salesman commission, advertising, after-sales service.
  *Why?* A product unsold has not yet earned revenue; delivery is part of earning it.

- **Step 12 — COST OF SALES (Total Cost)**
  Cost of Sales = Cost of Goods Sold + Selling & Distribution Overheads.

- **Step 13 — Add: PROFIT (and subtract Loss)**
  Profit / Loss = Sales Revenue − Cost of Sales.
  In KTU problems, profit is often given as a *percentage on cost* or *on sales* — see formula sheet below.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorise Before the Exam!**

| # | Component | Formula (No Pipes Used) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Direct Material Consumed | Opening RM + Purchases − Closing RM − Purchase Returns | Excludes GST/Trade Discounts |
| 2 | Prime Cost | DM Consumed + Direct Wages + Direct Expenses | Also called *First Cost* or *Flat Cost* |
| 3 | Works Cost (Factory Cost) | Prime Cost + Factory Overheads | Includes all production indirects |
| 4 | Cost of Production | Works Cost + Office / Administrative OH | Excludes selling OH |
| 5 | Cost of Goods Sold | COp + Opening FG Stock − Closing FG Stock | Adjusts production to sales |
| 6 | Cost of Sales (Total Cost) | COGS + Selling & Distribution OH | True Total Cost |
| 7 | Sales (if profit on cost) | Cost of Sales × (1 + Profit%) | Profit is on **Cost** |
| 8 | Sales (if profit on sales) | Cost of Sales / (1 − Profit%) | Profit is on **Sales** |
| 9 | Profit (on cost) | Cost of Sales × (Profit % \vert 100) | Common in KTU problems |
| 10 | Profit (on sales) | Sales × (Profit % \vert 100) | Common in KTU problems |
| 11 | Selling Price per unit | Total Sales \vert Total Units Sold | Final answer requirement |
| 12 | Factory OH Recovery Rate | (Factory OH \vert Direct Labour Hours) × Hours Used | Used when OH absorbed on labour hrs |

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Rule (Exam Lifesaver):** When KTU mentions *"Profit = 20 % on Cost"*, multiply Cost of Sales by **1.20** to get Sales. When it says *"Profit = 20 % on Sales"*, divide Cost of Sales by **0.80** to get Sales. Mixing these two is the **#1 cause of full-mark loss** in KTU valuation scripts.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of a Cost Sheet in Engineering / Industry

- **Pricing Decisions** — A manufacturer of transformers / circuit boards uses cost sheets to quote tenders to KSEB, Indian Railways, etc.
- **Tender Quotations** — Government tenders require a *detailed cost breakdown*; the cost sheet is the legal/audit document.
- **Cost Control** — Identifies *where money leaks* (e.g., excessive factory overheads).
- **Performance Benchmarking** — Compares the cost sheet of two consecutive years to track efficiency.
- **Inventory Valuation** — Stock of finished goods is valued at *Cost of Production* per the cost sheet.
- **Managerial Decision-Making** — Make-or-buy decisions, product-mix decisions, and shutdown decisions all rely on cost-sheet data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Problem-Solving Methodology (The KTU Valuation Key)

Below is the **Universal Problem-Solving Matrix** that KTU examiners expect. Apply it to *every* numerical problem on cost sheet.

**Phase 1 — Triage (Classification of Every Given Item)**
*Read the problem and tag each item into ONE of these 10 buckets*:

| Tag | Bucket | KTU Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **DM** | Direct Material | Raw material issued, specific components bought |
| **DL** | Direct Labour | Wages of machine operators, assembly workers |
| **DExp** | Direct Expense | Royalty, patent fees, hire of special tools |
| **FIM** | Factory Indirect Material | Lubricants, cotton waste, factory stationary |
| **FIL** | Factory Indirect Labour | Foreman salary, factory watchman, storekeeper |
| **FIE** | Factory Indirect Expense | Factory rent, factory power, plant depreciation |
| **AO** | Administrative Overhead | Office rent, MD salary, audit fees |
| **SDO** | Selling & Distribution OH | Carriage outward, salesmen commission, advertisement |
| **RMS** | Raw Material Stock | Opening / Closing stock of RM |
| **FGS** | Finished Goods Stock | Opening / Closing stock of FG |

**Phase 2 — Aggregation (Apply Formulae in Order)**
1. Compute **Direct Material Consumed** using Formula 1.
2. Sum DM + DL + DExp → **Prime Cost**.
3. Add **Factory Overheads** (FIM + FIL + FIE) → **Works Cost**.
4. Add **Administrative OH** → **Cost of Production**.
5. Adjust for **FG Stock** → **Cost of Goods Sold**.
6. Add **Selling & Distribution OH** → **Cost of Sales**.
7. Apply **Profit Margin** (using Formula 7 or 8 correctly) → **Sales Revenue**.

### 3.2 Solved KTU-Style Worked Problem (Detailed Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Style Problem (Full Length, 14 Marks):**

From the following particulars, prepare a **Cost Sheet** of M/s Kerala Engineering Works for the year ending 31-03-2025:

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Opening Stock — Raw Material | 25,000 |
| Purchases of Raw Material | 1,80,000 |
| Closing Stock — Raw Material | 30,000 |
| Wages (Production Workers) | 95,000 |
| Wages (Factory Foreman) | 15,000 |
| Salary (Salesman) | 18,000 |
| Salary (Office Manager) | 22,000 |
| Carriage Inward | 4,000 |
| Carriage Outward | 7,500 |
| Royalty Paid (per unit) | 6,000 |
| Factory Rent | 24,000 |
| Office Rent | 12,000 |
| Advertisement Expenses | 10,000 |
| Depreciation — Plant & Machinery | 8,000 |
| Depreciation — Delivery Van | 3,500 |
| Opening Stock — Finished Goods | 20,000 |
| Closing Stock — Finished Goods | 26,000 |
| Sales | 4,00,000 |

**Required:** Prepare the cost sheet and compute the **Profit**.

#### Step 1 — Direct Material Consumed (DM)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DM Consumed} &= \text{Opening RM} + \text{Purchases} - \text{Closing RM} + \text{Carriage Inward} \\
&= 25{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 - 30{,}000 + 4{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}79{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Carriage Inward is the transport cost to bring raw material to the factory. As per KTU conventions, it is added to the cost of purchases, not to selling overheads. The result is the *net* cost of material that has been consumed/available for production.

#### Step 2 — Direct Labour (DL)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct Wages} &= \text{Wages of Production Workers} \\
&= 95{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> Wages of the **foreman** are *indirect* (supervisory), so they go to Factory OH, not DL.

#### Step 3 — Direct Expenses

$$
\text{Direct Expenses} = \text{Royalty} = 6{,}000
$$

#### Step 4 — Prime Cost (PC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prime Cost} &= \text{DM Consumed} + \text{Direct Wages} + \text{Direct Expenses} \\
&= 1{,}79{,}000 + 95{,}000 + 6{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Factory Overheads (FOH)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOH} &= \text{Foreman Wages} + \text{Factory Rent} + \text{Depreciation of Plant} \\
&= 15{,}000 + 24{,}000 + 8{,}000 \\
&= 47{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> Depreciation of *Delivery Van* is **Selling OH** (used to deliver goods to customers). Depreciation of *Plant* is **Factory OH** (used in production).

#### Step 6 — Works Cost (WC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Works Cost} &= \text{Prime Cost} + \text{Factory Overheads} \\
&= 2{,}80{,}000 + 47{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}27{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7 — Administrative Overheads (AOH)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AOH} &= \text{Office Manager Salary} + \text{Office Rent} \\
&= 22{,}000 + 12{,}000 \\
&= 34{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 8 — Cost of Production (COp)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost of Production} &= \text{Works Cost} + \text{Administrative OH} \\
&= 3{,}27{,}000 + 34{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}61{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 9 — Adjust Finished Goods Stock → Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COGS} &= \text{COp} + \text{Opening FG} - \text{Closing FG} \\
&= 3{,}61{,}000 + 20{,}000 - 26{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}55{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** We produced goods worth ₹3,61,000. The closing FG worth ₹26,000 is *still with us* (not sold), so we subtract it. The opening FG worth ₹20,000 is *sold* in this period (produced earlier), so we add it.

#### Step 10 — Selling & Distribution Overheads (S&D OH)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{S\&D OH} &= \text{Salesman Salary} + \text{Carriage Outward} + \text{Advertisement} + \text{Van Depreciation} \\
&= 18{,}000 + 7{,}500 + 10{,}000 + 3{,}500 \\
&= 39{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> Carriage Outward is on goods *sold* (going to the customer) — so it is a **selling overhead**, not a factory cost.

#### Step 11 — Cost of Sales (CoS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost of Sales} &= \text{COGS} + \text{S\&D OH} \\
&= 3{,}55{,}000 + 39{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}94{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 12 — Profit

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Profit} &= \text{Sales} - \text{Cost of Sales} \\
&= 4{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}94{,}000 \\
&= 6{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 13 — Final Cost Sheet (Tabular Presentation)

| Sl. No. | Particulars | Amount (₹) | Total (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Opening Stock of Raw Material | 25,000 | |
| 2 | Add: Purchases of Raw Material | 1,80,000 | |
| 3 | Add: Carriage Inward | 4,000 | |
| 4 | Less: Closing Stock of Raw Material | (30,000) | |
| 5 | **Direct Material Consumed** | | **1,79,000** |
| 6 | **Add: Direct Wages** | | **95,000** |
| 7 | **Add: Direct Expenses (Royalty)** | | **6,000** |
| 8 | **PRIME COST** | | **2,80,000** |
| 9 | Add: Factory Overheads (15,000 + 24,000 + 8,000) | 47,000 | |
| 10 | **WORKS / FACTORY COST** | | **3,27,000** |
| 11 | Add: Administrative Overheads (22,000 + 12,000) | 34,000 | |
| 12 | **COST OF PRODUCTION** | | **3,61,000** |
| 13 | Add: Opening Stock of Finished Goods | 20,000 | |
| 14 | Less: Closing Stock of Finished Goods | (26,000) | |
| 15 | **COST OF GOODS SOLD** | | **3,55,000** |
| 16 | Add: Selling & Distribution Overheads (18,000 + 7,500 + 10,000 + 3,500) | 39,000 | |
| 17 | **COST OF SALES** | | **3,94,000** |
| 18 | Add: Profit (Balancing Figure) | | **6,000** |
| 19 | **SALES** | | **4,00,000** |

### 3.3 Reverse Problem — Profit on Cost / Profit on Sales

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reverse Problem Statement:** If Sales = ₹4,00,000 and Profit = 25 % on Cost, compute Cost of Sales.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let Cost of Sales} &= x \\
\text{Then Profit} &= 0.25x \\
\text{Sales} &= x + 0.25x = 1.25x \\
4{,}00{,}000 &= 1.25x \\
x &= 4{,}00{,}000 \div 1.25 = 3{,}20{,}000 \\
\text{Cost of Sales} &= 3{,}20{,}000 \\
\text{Profit} &= 80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> Conversely, if Profit = 25 % on Sales, then Profit = 0.25 × 4,00,000 = 1,00,000, and Cost of Sales = 3,00,000.

### 3.4 Tonnage / Unit Cost Calculation

> If 1,000 units were produced and sold, **Cost per unit = Cost of Sales ÷ 1,000**.

$$
\text{Cost per unit} = 3{,}94{,}000 \div 1{,}000 = \text{₹ } 394 \text{ per unit}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — "The Anatomy of a Cost Sheet"

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is the **mental model** examiners expect students to internalise. Trace the arrows from the top (raw material) to the bottom (sales) — each box is one line of the cost sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Material Opening Stock] --> A1[Raw Material Purchases]
    A1 --> A2[Less Purchase Returns]
    A2 --> A3[Add Carriage Inward]
    A3 --> A4[Less Raw Material Closing Stock]
    A4 --> DM[Direct Material Consumed]
    DL[Direct Labour] --> PC
    DEX[Direct Expenses] --> PC
    DM --> PC[Prime Cost]
    PC --> FOH[Add Factory Overheads]
    FOH --> WC[Works Cost]
    WC --> AOH[Add Administrative Overheads]
    AOH --> COP[Cost of Production]
    OFG[Add Opening Finished Goods] --> COGS
    CFG[Less Closing Finished Goods] --> COGS
    COP --> COGS[Cost of Goods Sold]
    COGS --> SDOH[Add Selling and Distribution OH]
    SDOH --> COS[Cost of Sales]
    COS --> PROF[Add Profit]
    PROF --> SAL[Sales Revenue]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of All 3 Cost Elements

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAT[Material]
        DM[Direct Material e.g. Raw Materials Issued]
        IM[Indirect Material e.g. Lubricants, Cotton Waste]
    end
    subgraph LAB[Labour]
        DL[Direct Wages e.g. Machine Operator]
        IL[Indirect Wages e.g. Foreman, Watchman]
    end
    subgraph EXP[Expenses]
        DEX[Direct Expenses e.g. Royalty, Hire]
        IEX[Indirect Expenses e.g. Rent, Depreciation, Power]
    end
    DM --> PCRIME[Prime Cost Bucket]
    DL --> PCRIME
    DEX --> PCRIME
    IM --> OH[Overheads Bucket]
    IL --> OH
    IEX --> OH
    OH --> FACT[Factory OH]
    OH --> OFF[Office OH]
    OH --> SEL[Selling and Distribution OH]
    FACT --> WCOST[Works Cost]
    OFF --> CP[Cost of Production]
    SEL --> CSALES[Cost of Sales]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Diagram — "Profit is on Cost or Sales?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PSTART{Profit is given how?}
    PSTART -- on Cost --> PCALC[Multiply Cost of Sales by 1 + Profit Pct]
    PSTART -- on Sales --> SCALC[Divide Cost of Sales by 1 - Profit Pct]
    PCALC --> SALES1[Sales = Cost of Sales x 1.20]
    SCALC --> SALES2[Sales = Cost of Sales / 0.80]
    SALES1 --> PEND[Final Sales Value]
    SALES2 --> PEND
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — "Why Each Cost Belongs Where It Belongs"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ENV[Cost Item] --> Q1{Is it 100pct traceable to one product?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Is it a physical input, human effort, or special charge?}
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3{In which department is it incurred?}
    Q2 -- Physical Input --> DM2[Direct Material]
    Q2 -- Human Effort --> DL2[Direct Labour]
    Q2 -- Special Charge --> DEX2[Direct Expenses]
    Q3 -- Factory Floor --> FOH2[Factory Overheads - indirect material, indirect labour, indirect expense of factory]
    Q3 -- Head Office --> AOH2[Administrative Overheads - office staff, audit, office rent]
    Q3 -- Sales Warehouse --> SDOH2[Selling and Distribution OH - salesmen, delivery, advertising]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Prime Cost" and list its three components.**

> [!TIP]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Prime Cost** is the aggregate of all *direct* costs incurred in the manufacture of a product up to the stage of placing it in a workable condition. It represents the *first cost* of production and is sometimes also called the **First Cost** or **Flat Cost**.
>
> The three components are:
>
> 1. **Direct Material Consumed** — all raw materials and components that become part of the finished product.
> 2. **Direct Labour (Wages)** — wages paid to workers who are directly engaged in the manufacturing process.
> 3. **Direct / Chargeable Expenses** — expenses that are 100 % attributable to the product (e.g., royalty, patent fees, hire of a special machine).
>
> *Formula:* Prime Cost = Direct Material Consumed + Direct Wages + Direct Expenses.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between "Administrative Overheads" and "Selling & Distribution Overheads" with two examples each.**

> [!TIP]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Basis | Administrative Overheads | Selling & Distribution Overheads |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Purpose** | Incurred for the *policy-direction* and overall management of the business. | Incurred to *create demand* and *deliver* the product to the customer. |
> | **Location** | Incurred at the **Head Office / Administrative Block**. | Incurred at **Sales Office, Warehouses, In Transit, After-sales**. |
> | **Timing** | Incurred **before, during, and after production**. | Incurred **only after** production is complete. |
> | **Example 1** | Salary of Office Manager, Company Secretary. | Carriage outward, Salesman commission. |
> | **Example 2** | Audit fees, Legal expenses, Office rent. | Advertisement, After-sales service, Packing (for export). |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Carriage Inward vs Carriage Outward confusion** — Inward = Raw Material cost (added to purchases); Outward = Selling OH. Marks lost here are *unrecoverable* on revaluation.
> 2. **Depreciation of Plant vs Delivery Vehicle** — Plant = Factory OH; Delivery Vehicle = Selling OH. Most common single-line error.
> 3. **Profit % on Cost vs on Sales** — Always re-read the question. Use the correct conversion formula.
> 4. **FG Stock adjustment sign** — Closing FG is *subtracted*; Opening FG is *added*. Reversed signs are penalised.
> 5. **Formatting of the cost sheet** — KTU expects a *neat tabular format* with bold totals. A scribbled working-note version loses 1–2 presentation marks.

---

#### ⭐ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 / Similar Pattern]**

From the following particulars furnished by M/s Trivandrum Engineering Co., prepare a **Cost Sheet** for the year ended 31-03-2024 and find the **Cost per unit** (1,200 units produced and sold):

| Item | ₹ | Item | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Opening Stock — Raw Material | 18,000 | Carriage Outward | 4,500 |
| Closing Stock — Raw Material | 22,000 | Office Printing & Stationery | 3,200 |
| Purchases of Raw Material | 1,40,000 | Office Lighting | 1,800 |
| Wages — Machine Operators | 70,000 | Salesman Commission | 8,000 |
| Wages — Factory Foreman | 10,000 | Advertisement | 6,000 |
| Salary — Production Manager | 12,000 | Depreciation — Plant | 7,500 |
| Salary — Office Manager | 15,000 | Depreciation — Office Equipment | 2,500 |
| Salary — Marketing Director | 18,000 | Opening Stock — Finished Goods | 16,000 |
| Carriage Inward | 5,000 | Closing Stock — Finished Goods | 22,000 |
| Factory Rent | 20,000 | Royalty Paid | 5,000 |
| Office Rent | 9,000 | Sales | 4,20,000 |

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Prepare the Cost Sheet in proper tabular form showing Prime Cost, Works Cost, Cost of Production, Cost of Goods Sold, and Cost of Sales.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the Profit and the Cost per Unit.

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Choice A

**Part (a) — Cost Sheet**

**Step 1: Direct Material Consumed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DM Consumed} &= 18{,}000 + 1{,}40{,}000 + 5{,}000 - 22{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}41{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Direct Labour**

$$
\text{DL} = \text{Wages of Machine Operators} = 70{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Direct Expenses**

$$
\text{DE} = \text{Royalty} = 5{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Prime Cost**

$$
\text{PC} = 1{,}41{,}000 + 70{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 2{,}16{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Factory Overheads** (Foreman Wages + Production Manager Salary + Factory Rent + Plant Depreciation)

$$
\text{FOH} = 10{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 7{,}500 = 49{,}500
$$

**Step 6: Works Cost**

$$
\text{WC} = 2{,}16{,}000 + 49{,}500 = 2{,}65{,}500
$$

**Step 7: Administrative Overheads** (Office Manager Salary + Office Printing & Stationery + Office Lighting + Office Rent + Depreciation — Office Equipment)

$$
\text{AOH} = 15{,}000 + 3{,}200 + 1{,}800 + 9{,}000 + 2{,}500 = 31{,}500
$$

**Step 8: Cost of Production**

$$
\text{COp} = 2{,}65{,}500 + 31{,}500 = 2{,}97{,}000
$$

**Step 9: Cost of Goods Sold**

$$
\text{COGS} = 2{,}97{,}000 + 16{,}000 - 22{,}000 = 2{,}91{,}000
$$

**Step 10: Selling & Distribution Overheads** (Carriage Outward + Salesman Commission + Advertisement + Marketing Director Salary)

$$
\text{S\&D OH} = 4{,}500 + 8{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 18{,}000 = 36{,}500
$$

**Step 11: Cost of Sales**

$$
\text{CoS} = 2{,}91{,}000 + 36{,}500 = 3{,}27{,}500
$$

**Tabulated Cost Sheet**

| Sl. No. | Particulars | Amount (₹) | Total (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Opening Stock — Raw Material | 18,000 | |
| 2 | Add: Purchases of Raw Material | 1,40,000 | |
| 3 | Add: Carriage Inward | 5,000 | |
| 4 | Less: Closing Stock of Raw Material | (22,000) | |
| 5 | **Direct Material Consumed** | | **1,41,000** |
| 6 | **Direct Wages** | | **70,000** |
| 7 | **Direct Expenses (Royalty)** | | **5,000** |
| 8 | **PRIME COST** | | **2,16,000** |
| 9 | Add: Factory Overheads (10,000 + 12,000 + 20,000 + 7,500) | 49,500 | |
| 10 | **WORKS COST** | | **2,65,500** |
| 11 | Add: Administrative OH (15,000 + 3,200 + 1,800 + 9,000 + 2,500) | 31,500 | |
| 12 | **COST OF PRODUCTION** | | **2,97,000** |
| 13 | Add: Opening FG Stock | 16,000 | |
| 14 | Less: Closing FG Stock | (22,000) | |
| 15 | **COST OF GOODS SOLD** | | **2,91,000** |
| 16 | Add: S&D OH (4,500 + 8,000 + 6,000 + 18,000) | 36,500 | |
| 17 | **COST OF SALES** | | **3,27,500** |
| 18 | Add: Profit (Balancing Figure) | | 92,500 |
| 19 | **SALES** | | **4,20,000** |

**Part (b) — Profit and Cost per Unit**

$$
\text{Profit} = 4{,}20{,}000 - 3{,}27{,}500 = 92{,}500
$$

$$
\text{Cost per unit} = 3{,}27{,}500 \div 1{,}200 = \text{₹ } 272.92 \text{ (approx.)}
$$

$$
\text{Profit per unit} = 92{,}500 \div 1{,}200 = \text{₹ } 77.08 \text{ (approx.)}
$$

$$
\text{Selling Price per unit} = 4{,}20{,}000 \div 1{,}200 = \text{₹ } 350
$$

---

#### ⭐ CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Similar Pattern]**

The following are the particulars relating to the manufacture of a product by M/s Kochi Tech Industries during the year ended 31-03-2023:

| Item | ₹ | Item | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Opening Stock — Raw Material | 30,000 | Office Insurance | 1,500 |
| Purchases of Raw Material | 2,00,000 | Showroom Rent | 6,000 |
| Closing Stock — Raw Material | 25,000 | Carriage on Sales | 4,200 |
| Wages — Direct Labour | 1,20,000 | Carriage on Purchases | 3,800 |
| Wages — Factory Supervisor | 18,000 | Salesman Commission | 9,000 |
| Salary — Accounts Officer | 20,000 | Trade Expenses | 3,500 |
| Salary — Sales Manager | 16,000 | Bad Debts | 2,800 |
| Factory Power | 12,000 | Depreciation — Office Building | 3,000 |
| Factory Lighting | 4,500 | Depreciation — Plant & Machinery | 6,500 |
| Office Rent | 10,000 | Sales | 5,50,000 |
| Opening Stock — Finished Goods | 22,000 | Royalty Paid | 4,000 |
| Closing Stock — Finished Goods | 28,000 | | |

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Prepare the Cost Sheet showing clearly (i) Prime Cost, (ii) Works Cost, and (iii) Cost of Production.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Find the (i) Cost of Goods Sold, (ii) Cost of Sales, and (iii) Net Profit.

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Choice B

**Part (a) — Cost Sheet up to Cost of Production**

**Step 1: Direct Material Consumed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DM Consumed} &= 30{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}800 - 25{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}08{,}800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Direct Labour**

$$
\text{DL} = 1{,}20{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Direct Expenses**

$$
\text{DE} = \text{Royalty} = 4{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Prime Cost**

$$
\text{PC} = 2{,}08{,}800 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 4{,}000 = 3{,}32{,}800
$$

**Step 5: Factory Overheads** (Supervisor Wages + Factory Power + Factory Lighting + Depreciation — Plant & Machinery)

$$
\text{FOH} = 18{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 4{,}500 + 6{,}500 = 41{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Works Cost**

$$
\text{WC} = 3{,}32{,}800 + 41{,}000 = 3{,}73{,}800
$$

**Step 7: Administrative Overheads** (Accounts Officer Salary + Office Insurance + Office Rent + Depreciation — Office Building)

$$
\text{AOH} = 20{,}000 + 1{,}500 + 10{,}000 + 3{,}000 = 34{,}500
$$

**Step 8: Cost of Production**

$$
\text{COp} = 3{,}73{,}800 + 34{,}500 = 4{,}08{,}300
$$

**Tabulated Cost Sheet — Part (a)**

| Sl. No. | Particulars | Amount (₹) | Total (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Opening Stock — Raw Material | 30,000 | |
| 2 | Add: Purchases | 2,00,000 | |
| 3 | Add: Carriage on Purchases | 3,800 | |
| 4 | Less: Closing Stock — Raw Material | (25,000) | |
| 5 | **Direct Material Consumed** | | **2,08,800** |
| 6 | **Direct Wages** | | **1,20,000** |
| 7 | **Direct Expenses (Royalty)** | | **4,000** |
| 8 | **(i) PRIME COST** | | **3,32,800** |
| 9 | Add: Factory OH (18,000 + 12,000 + 4,500 + 6,500) | 41,000 | |
| 10 | **(ii) WORKS COST** | | **3,73,800** |
| 11 | Add: Administrative OH (20,000 + 1,500 + 10,000 + 3,000) | 34,500 | |
| 12 | **(iii) COST OF PRODUCTION** | | **4,08,300** |

**Part (b) — Cost of Goods Sold, Cost of Sales, and Net Profit**

**Step 9: Cost of Goods Sold**

$$
\text{COGS} = 4{,}08{,}300 + 22{,}000 - 28{,}000 = 4{,}02{,}300
$$

**Step 10: Selling & Distribution Overheads** (Sales Manager Salary + Showroom Rent + Carriage on Sales + Salesman Commission + Trade Expenses + Bad Debts)

$$
\text{S\&D OH} = 16{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 4{,}200 + 9{,}000 + 3{,}500 + 2{,}800 = 41{,}500
$$

> **Note:** *Bad Debts* arise on credit sales; they are an *after-sales* loss recovered from the business, hence classified as Selling & Distribution OH under KTU conventions.

**Step 11: Cost of Sales**

$$
\text{CoS} = 4{,}02{,}300 + 41{,}500 = 4{,}43{,}800
$$

**Step 12: Net Profit**

$$
\text{Profit} = 5{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}43{,}800 = 1{,}06{,}200
$$

**Tabulated Cost Sheet — Part (b) Conclusion**

| Sl. No. | Particulars | Amount (₹) | Total (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| | **Cost of Production (b/f)** | | **4,08,300** |
| 13 | Add: Opening FG Stock | 22,000 | |
| 14 | Less: Closing FG Stock | (28,000) | |
| 15 | **(i) COST OF GOODS SOLD** | | **4,02,300** |
| 16 | Add: S&D OH (16,000 + 6,000 + 4,200 + 9,000 + 3,500 + 2,800) | 41,500 | |
| 17 | **(ii) COST OF SALES** | | **4,43,800** |
| 18 | Add: Net Profit (Balancing Figure) | | **1,06,200** |
| 19 | **SALES** | | **5,50,000** |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 14 Golden Rules for the Cost Sheet — Read Before the Exam:**

- **Rule 1 — Order of Layers:** DM → DL → DExp → **Prime Cost** → FOH → **Works Cost** → AOH → **Cost of Production** → ± FG Stock → **COGS** → S&D OH → **Cost of Sales** → Profit → **Sales**.
- **Rule 2 — Three "Direct" Components Only** form Prime Cost. Anything indirect goes to the relevant overhead bucket.
- **Rule 3 — Carriage Inward** is added to **Purchases of Raw Material**, NOT to selling overheads.
- **Rule 4 — Carriage Outward** is **Selling & Distribution Overhead**, NEVER a production cost.
- **Rule 5 — Depreciation Rule:** Plant/Machinery/Factory equipment = **Factory OH**; Office equipment/Building = **Administrative OH**; Delivery vehicle/Showroom = **Selling OH**.
- **Rule 6 — Salary Rule:** Factory-floor staff = **Factory OH**; Office staff (Manager, Accountant, HR) = **Administrative OH**; Salesman/Sales Manager = **Selling OH**.
- **Rule 7 — Profit Conversion Rule:** "20 % on Cost" → Sales = Cost × 1.20. "20 % on Sales" → Sales = Cost ÷ 0.80. **Memorise this!**
- **Rule 8 — FG Stock Sign Rule:** Opening FG is **added**; Closing FG is **subtracted** at the COGS step. For Raw Material: Opening is **added**, Closing is **subtracted** at the Direct Material step.
- **Rule 9 — Royalty, Patent Fees, Hire of Special Tools** = **Direct Expenses** (always).
- **Rule 10 — Bad Debts, Trade Expenses, Showroom Expenses** = **Selling & Distribution Overheads** (always, under KTU convention).
- **Rule 11 — Audit Fees, Legal Fees, Company Secretary Fees, Office Rent** = **Administrative Overheads** (always).
- **Rule 12 — Indirect Material Examples:** Lubricants, cotton waste, oil, small tools consumables. These are *Factory OH*, not Direct Material.
- **Rule 13 — Indirect Labour Examples:** Foreman, Storekeeper, Factory Watchman, Time-keeper, Inspectors, Quality control. These are *Factory OH*.
- **Rule 14 — Always present the answer in a TABULAR cost-sheet format** with bold totals, matching the 19-line standard KTU layout. A working-note narrative version loses presentation marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** In a 14-mark question, expect **2 marks for the Cost Sheet format & presentation**, **8 marks for the layered calculations**, and **4 marks for the profit/units computation**. Present neatly, label every subtotal, and you will secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Budgets and ABC Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT523 - FINANCIAL AND COST ACCOUNTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Budgets and ABC Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Budget — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Budget** is a *quantitative financial and/or quantitative statement*, prepared and approved *prior to a defined period of time*, of the *policy to be pursued* during that period, for the *purpose of attaining a given objective*. It is essentially a **plan of action expressed in numerical terms**, serving as a blueprint for future operations in financial terms.

A budget is not a mere forecast or estimate. While an *estimate* is a statement of probable outcomes, a *budget* is a **predetermined plan of action** that combines forecast with policy. It converts strategic intent into measurable, time-bound financial targets.

**Key Features of a Budget:**
- It is a **future-oriented** plan (always for a future period).
- It is expressed in **quantitative/financial terms** (rupees, units, hours, etc.).
- It is a **statement of policy** — it reflects management's chosen course of action.
- It is **prepared for a specific period** (monthly, quarterly, annually).
- It requires **approval** by appropriate authority before enforcement.
- It acts as a **standard of performance** for control and evaluation.

### 1.2 ABC Analysis — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> **ABC Analysis (Always Better Control Analysis)** is an *inventory classification and management technique* based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**. It categorizes inventory items into three classes — **A, B, and C** — based on their *annual consumption value*, so that management attention and control efforts can be focused where the financial impact is greatest.

The technique was developed by **H.F. Dickie** (General Electric, USA) in the 1950s and is rooted in the **Pareto Principle** (proposed by Italian economist **Vilfredo Pareto**, 1897), which observes that a small percentage of items typically account for a large percentage of the total value.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Household Pantry:**
Imagine you have a kitchen with 100 items. You use *salt, sugar, oil, and flour* every day. These are only 4 items (4% of stock), but they consume 80% of your grocery budget. The other 96 items (spices, condiments, special ingredients) are rarely used but cost only 20% of your budget. Would you monitor *every spice jar daily* with the same intensity as you monitor *salt and oil*? **Absolutely not.** This is precisely what ABC Analysis does — it tells the manager to treat the "salt and oil" items (Class A) with intense control and the "rare spices" (Class C) with loose control.

**Analogy 2 — Personal Monthly Budget:**
Think of your monthly pocket money of ₹10,000. You allocate:
- ₹6,000 to Rent & Food (the "vital few" — *Class A*),
- ₹3,000 to Transport & Utilities (the "useful many" — *Class B*),
- ₹1,000 to Entertainment & Miscellaneous (the "trivial many" — *Class C*).

You monitor rent very carefully (slip on rent = disaster), but you may be flexible with entertainment. **This is budget prioritization in action.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction to Remember:**
> - *Budget* = Forward-looking **plan** for the **entire organization** (revenue, cost, cash, capital).
> - *ABC Analysis* = Selective **control tool** for a specific subset — **inventory** — based on value contribution.
> Both together help managers *plan smartly* and *control selectively*.

### 1.4 Standard Classification Thresholds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard ABC Classification (Empirical Norms):**
> - **Class A Items** ≈ **10–20%** of total items, but consume **70–80%** of total annual consumption value. *(Tightest control)*
> - **Class B Items** ≈ **20–30%** of total items, consuming **15–25%** of total value. *(Moderate control)*
> - **Class C Items** ≈ **50–70%** of total items, consuming only **5–10%** of total value. *(Loose control)*

The exact thresholds vary by industry, but the **Pareto spirit** (vital few vs. trivial many) remains the guiding principle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Budgets — Exhaustive Classification

Budgets can be classified along multiple dimensions. KTU expects students to know the major functional types listed below:

#### A. Master Budget
- **Definition:** A comprehensive, consolidated budget that integrates all **sub-budgets** of an organization into a single coordinated plan.
- **Components:**
  1. **Operating Budget** (Revenue & Expense side)
     - Sales Budget
     - Production Budget
     - Direct Material Purchase Budget
     - Direct Labour Budget
     - Manufacturing Overhead Budget
     - Selling & Distribution Expense Budget
     - Administrative Expense Budget
  2. **Financial Budget** (Cash & Capital side)
     - Cash Budget
     - Capital Expenditure Budget
     - Budgeted Balance Sheet
     - Budgeted P&L Statement

#### B. Functional Sub-Budgets
| **Budget** | **Purpose** | **Prepared From** |
|---|---|---|
| **Sales Budget** | Forecasts expected sales revenue (units × price) | Past sales data, market trends, sales force estimates |
| **Production Budget** | Determines units to be produced | Sales forecast + Desired closing stock − Opening stock |
| **Material Purchase Budget** | Quantity & cost of raw material to be purchased | Production budget + Stock policy + Material price trends |
| **Labour Budget** | Total labour hours & cost required | Production volume × Standard hours × Wage rate |
| **Overhead Budget** | Fixed & variable overheads for production | Past costs adjusted for activity level |
| **Cash Budget** | Cash inflows & outflows to project cash position | All operating budgets + Financing plans |
| **Capital Budget** | Long-term asset acquisition plans | Expansion plans, replacement policy |
| **Flexible Budget** | Budget at *varying* activity levels | Cost behaviour patterns (variable/fixed/semi-variable) |

#### C. Specialized Budget Types
- **Fixed Budget:** Prepared for *one specific activity level*. Becomes misleading if actual activity deviates.
- **Flexible Budget:** Prepared for *multiple activity levels* using cost behaviour analysis. Useful for **performance evaluation**.
- **Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB):** Each line item must be **justified from zero** every period, not just incremental changes on last year's figures. Promoted by **Peter Pyhrr** (Texas Instruments, 1970).
- **Incremental Budgeting:** Last period's budget + % adjustment. Simple but promotes inefficiency.
- **Rolling/Continuous Budget:** Always maintains a 12-month forward horizon by adding a new month as the current one ends.
- **Programme Budgeting:** Budgets by *objectives/programmes* rather than by departments.
- **Performance Budgeting:** Links budget to **outputs and outcomes**, not just inputs.

#### D. Long-Range vs. Short-Range Budgets
- **Long-Range Budget:** Spans 3–10 years. Strategic in nature. Less precise, more directional.
- **Short-Range Budget:** Spans 1 month to 1 year. Operational and detailed.

### 2.2 Objectives of Budgetary Control
1. **Planning** — Forces management to think ahead systematically.
2. **Coordination** — Aligns departments so that sub-plans do not conflict.
3. **Communication** — Transmits targets clearly to all levels.
4. **Motivation** — Sets challenging but achievable targets for employees.
5. **Control** — Provides standards against which actual performance is measured.
6. **Performance Evaluation** — Enables variance analysis and corrective action.

### 2.3 Advantages & Limitations of Budgetary Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages:**
> - Promotes foresight and forward thinking.
> - Defines authority and responsibility clearly.
> - Enables delegation without loss of control.
> - Identifies bottlenecks and inefficiencies early.
> - Coordinates all departments into a unified plan.
> - Provides basis for performance appraisal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitations:**
> - Based on **estimates** — inaccuracies can mislead.
> - **Rigid** budgets may not suit dynamic environments.
> - Time-consuming and **costly** to prepare.
> - Requires **skilled staff** and management commitment.
> - May become a **blunt control tool** if used punitively.

### 2.4 ABC Analysis — Detailed Logic

The technique involves the following **systematic procedure:**

**Step 1:** List all inventory items with their **annual consumption quantity** and **unit cost**.
**Step 2:** Calculate the **Annual Consumption Value** for each item:

$$ACV = \text{Annual Consumption Quantity (units)} \times \text{Unit Cost (₹)}$$

**Step 3:** Rank items in **descending order** of ACV (highest value first).
**Step 4:** Calculate the **percentage** of each item's ACV to total ACV.
**Step 5:** Calculate **cumulative percentage** of value.
**Step 6:** Classify into A, B, C based on cumulative value contribution.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $ACV_i$ | Annual Consumption Value of item $i$ | $Q_i \times C_i$ | ₹ |
| $Q_i$ | Annual quantity consumed of item $i$ | Given / Annual usage | units |
| $C_i$ | Unit cost of item $i$ | Given | ₹ / unit |
| $V_i$ | % Value contribution of item $i$ | $\dfrac{ACV_i}{\sum ACV} \times 100$ | % |
| $CV$ | Cumulative value % | $\sum_{j=1}^{i} V_j$ | % |
| $P_i$ | % Quantity proportion of item $i$ | $\dfrac{Q_i}{\sum Q} \times 100$ | % |
| $N_A$ | Number of Class A items | Count of items contributing top 70–80% value | items |
| $N_B$ | Number of Class B items | Count of items contributing next 15–25% value | items |
| $N_C$ | Number of Class C items | Remaining items (5–10% value) | items |
| $C_{\text{per-unit}}$ | Per-unit carrying cost | Order cost $\div$ Order quantity (EOQ context) | ₹ |
| $PB$ | Production Budget output | Sales forecast + Closing stock − Opening stock | units |
| $MPB$ | Material Purchase Budget qty | Consumption + Closing material stock − Opening material stock | units |
| $CB_{\text{net}}$ | Net Cash Flow | Receipts − Payments | ₹ |
| $FB_{level}$ | Flexible Budget at any level | Fixed cost + (Variable cost per unit × Actual units) | ₹ |

### 2.6 Control Levels for Each Class

| **Parameter** | **Class A** | **Class B** | **Class C** |
|---|---|---|---|
| % of Items | 10–20% | 20–30% | 50–70% |
| % of Value | 70–80% | 15–25% | 5–10% |
| Control Level | **Tight / Rigorous** | **Moderate** | **Loose / Simple** |
| Ordering Policy | Frequent, small lots | Moderate order size | Bulk orders, infrequent |
| Safety Stock | Low (high accuracy needed) | Moderate | High (acceptable) |
| Record Keeping | **Perpetual / Item-wise** | Periodic review | **Bin card / Two-bin system** |
| Physical Stock Check | Continuous / Monthly | Quarterly | Half-yearly / Annual |
| Management Attention | **Top management** | Middle management | Lower / clerical level |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive ABC Analysis — Worked Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

> **Problem Statement (Model Question Type):**
> *The following data relates to 10 items of inventory of M/s Kappa Traders. Apply ABC Analysis and classify the items into A, B, and C categories using standard norms.*

| Item | Annual Usage (units) | Unit Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 500 | 200 |
| 2 | 1,500 | 50 |
| 3 | 200 | 1,000 |
| 4 | 5,000 | 10 |
| 5 | 100 | 1,500 |
| 6 | 800 | 300 |
| 7 | 4,000 | 25 |
| 8 | 50 | 2,000 |
| 9 | 1,000 | 80 |
| 10 | 3,000 | 40 |

---

#### **Step 1: Compute Annual Consumption Value (ACV) for each item**

Using the fundamental formula:

$$ACV_i = Q_i \times C_i$$

Let us compute each item explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACV_1 &= 500 \times 200 = 1{,}00{,}000 \ \text{₹} \\
ACV_2 &= 1{,}500 \times 50 = 75{,}000 \ \text{₹} \\
ACV_3 &= 200 \times 1{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000 \ \text{₹} \\
ACV_4 &= 5{,}000 \times 10 = 50{,}000 \ \text{₹} \\
ACV_5 &= 100 \times 1{,}500 = 1{,}50{,}000 \ \text{₹} \\
ACV_6 &= 800 \times 300 = 2{,}40{,}000 \ \text{₹} \\
ACV_7 &= 4{,}000 \times 25 = 1{,}00{,}000 \ \text{₹} \\
ACV_8 &= 50 \times 2{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 \ \text{₹} \\
ACV_9 &= 1{,}000 \times 80 = 80{,}000 \ \text{₹} \\
ACV_{10} &= 3{,}000 \times 40 = 1{,}20{,}000 \ \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Annual Consumption Value:**

$$\sum ACV = 1{,}00{,}000 + 75{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000$$

Adding sequentially:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= 1{,}00{,}000 + 75{,}000 = 1{,}75{,}000 \\
&+ 2{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}75{,}000 \\
&+ 50{,}000 = 4{,}25{,}000 \\
&+ 1{,}50{,}000 = 5{,}75{,}000 \\
&+ 2{,}40{,}000 = 8{,}15{,}000 \\
&+ 1{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}15{,}000 \\
&+ 1{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}15{,}000 \\
&+ 80{,}000 = 10{,}95{,}000 \\
&+ 1{,}20{,}000 = 12{,}15{,}000 \ \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\sum ACV = \text{₹ } 12{,}15{,}000}$$

---

#### **Step 2: Rank Items in Descending Order of ACV**

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6 | 2,40,000 |
| 2 | 3 | 2,00,000 |
| 3 | 5 | 1,50,000 |
| 4 | 10 | 1,20,000 |
| 5 | 1 | 1,00,000 |
| 5 | 7 | 1,00,000 |
| 5 | 8 | 1,00,000 |
| 8 | 9 | 80,000 |
| 9 | 2 | 75,000 |
| 10 | 4 | 50,000 |

---

#### **Step 3: Compute Value Percentage and Cumulative Value Percentage**

Using the formula:

$$V_i = \frac{ACV_i}{\sum ACV} \times 100 = \frac{ACV_i}{12{,}15{,}000} \times 100$$

For each item in ranked order:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_6 &= \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{12{,}15{,}000} \times 100 = 19.75\% \\
V_3 &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{12{,}15{,}000} \times 100 = 16.46\% \\
V_5 &= \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{12{,}15{,}000} \times 100 = 12.35\% \\
V_{10} &= \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{12{,}15{,}000} \times 100 = 9.88\% \\
V_1 &= \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{12{,}15{,}000} \times 100 = 8.23\% \\
V_7 &= \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{12{,}15{,}000} \times 100 = 8.23\% \\
V_8 &= \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{12{,}15{,}000} \times 100 = 8.23\% \\
V_9 &= \frac{80{,}000}{12{,}15{,}000} \times 100 = 6.58\% \\
V_2 &= \frac{75{,}000}{12{,}15{,}000} \times 100 = 6.17\% \\
V_4 &= \frac{50{,}000}{12{,}15{,}000} \times 100 = 4.12\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cumulative Value Percentage** (running total):

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV_6 &= 19.75\% \\
CV_3 &= 19.75 + 16.46 = 36.21\% \\
CV_5 &= 36.21 + 12.35 = 48.56\% \\
CV_{10} &= 48.56 + 9.88 = 58.44\% \\
CV_1 &= 58.44 + 8.23 = 66.67\% \\
CV_7 &= 66.67 + 8.23 = 74.90\% \\
CV_8 &= 74.90 + 8.23 = 83.13\% \\
CV_9 &= 83.13 + 6.58 = 89.71\% \\
CV_2 &= 89.71 + 6.17 = 95.88\% \\
CV_4 &= 95.88 + 4.12 = 100.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Step 4: Classification Using Standard Cut-offs**

Applying the **Pareto principle** with standard cut-offs:

| **Class** | **Items** | **Cumulative % Items** | **Cumulative % Value** | **Cut-off Rule** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 6, 3, 5, 10, 1 | 5/10 = 50% | 66.67% ≈ Top 70% | Tight control |
| **B** | 7, 8 | 7/10 = 70% | 83.13% ≈ Mid range | Moderate control |
| **C** | 9, 2, 4 | 10/10 = 100% | 100% (tail) | Loose control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU students:** With only 10 items, the cut-off boundaries will not exactly match the textbook 10-20/20-30/50-70 split. **Always state your cut-off criteria** in the exam before classifying. Acceptable cut-offs: A = top 70-80% of value, B = next 15-25%, C = remaining.

**Re-classification with KTU textbook cut-offs** (A: ≤75% value, B: 75-90% value, C: >90% value):

| **Class** | **Items** | **% of Items** | **% of Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** (Tight control) | 6, 3, 5, 10, 1 | 50% (5 items) | 66.67% |
| **B** (Moderate control) | 7, 8 | 20% (2 items) | 16.46% |
| **C** (Loose control) | 9, 2, 4 | 30% (3 items) | 16.87% |

**Managerial Interpretation:** Items 6, 3, 5, 10, and 1 are the *vital few* — they consume ₹8,10,000 of the ₹12,15,000 total. Management must enforce **rigorous stock control**, **perpetual inventory records**, and **frequent review cycles** for these. The rest can be managed on a *loose basis* with bulk ordering.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Budget Equations Derivation

#### A. Production Budget (PB) Derivation

The basic production identity is:

$$\text{Closing Stock} = \text{Opening Stock} + \text{Production} - \text{Sales}$$

Rearranging for Production:

$$PB = SF + DC - OB$$

Where:
- $SF$ = Sales Forecast (units)
- $DC$ = Desired Closing Stock (units)
- $OB$ = Opening Balance Stock (units)

#### B. Material Purchase Budget (MPB) Derivation

$$\text{MPB}_{qty} = MC + MS_{close} - MS_{open}$$

Where:
- $MC$ = Material required for Consumption (= Production units × Standard material per unit)
- $MS_{close}$ = Desired closing material stock
- $MS_{open}$ = Opening material stock

**Monetary value:**

$$MPB_{value} = MPB_{qty} \times \text{Predicted material price per unit}$$

#### C. Cash Budget (CB) Derivation

$$CB = OB_{cash} + TR - TP$$

Where:
- $OB_{cash}$ = Opening cash balance
- $TR$ = Total Cash Receipts (cash sales + credit collections + other income)
- $TP$ = Total Cash Payments (material, labour, overheads, capital, taxes, dividends)

For **closing balance**:

$$CB_{close} = OB_{cash} + TR - TP + BF$$

Where $BF$ = Bank Finance (if any shortfall is bridged by borrowing).

#### D. Flexible Budget (FB) Derivation

$$FB_{level} = FC + (VC_{per\ unit} \times A_{units})$$

Where:
- $FC$ = Total Fixed Cost (constant)
- $VC_{per\ unit}$ = Variable Cost per unit
- $A_{units}$ = Actual activity (units produced/sold)

**The power of Flexible Budgeting:** It allows fair comparison of *actual cost at actual activity* vs. *budgeted cost at actual activity*, removing the distortion caused by activity-level changes.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Distinguish between a Budget and an Estimate. Why is budgeting considered superior to mere estimation?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Budget** | **Estimate** |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | A plan of future action with a specific policy goal | A statement of *probable* future outcome without binding action |
| **Authority** | Requires formal management approval | May be prepared by any individual, no formal approval required |
| **Function** | Acts as a *standard* for control and performance evaluation | Acts only as a *forecast* with no control role |
| **Time frame** | Always for a *defined future period* | Can be retrospective (actuals) or prospective |
| **Use** | Tool of *planning and control* | Tool of *forecasting only* |

Budgeting is superior because it not only projects future events (like estimation) but also **commits the organization to specific action plans**, establishes **performance benchmarks**, enables **variance analysis**, and provides a **mechanism for corrective action** — none of which a simple estimate offers.

> **(3 marks: 1 for distinction table, 1 for characteristics, 1 for concluding statement)**

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Pareto Principle as applied to ABC Analysis. What are the standard percentage cut-offs for A, B, and C categories?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Pareto Principle**, originally observed by Italian economist Vilfredo Pareto in 1897, states that *roughly 80% of the consequences come from 20% of the causes*. Applied to inventory:

> "A *small percentage* of inventory items typically accounts for a *large percentage* of the annual consumption value."

**Standard cut-offs (empirical norms):**

- **Class A:** ~10–20% of items, ~70–80% of value → *vital few* → tightest control
- **Class B:** ~20–30% of items, ~15–25% of value → *useful many* → moderate control
- **Class C:** ~50–70% of items, ~5–10% of value → *trivial many* → loose control

The application of this principle enables *selective control* — managerial effort is concentrated where financial impact is greatest, yielding maximum benefit per unit of control effort.

> **(3 marks: 1 for principle statement, 1 for table, 1 for selective control justification)**

---

### **PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)**

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the salient features and objectives of a **Master Budget**. Briefly describe any **four functional sub-budgets** that constitute it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** From the following data relating to 12 items of inventory of M/s Sigma Industries, apply **ABC Analysis** and suggest a suitable control policy for each class.

| Item | Annual Usage (units) | Unit Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4,000 | 5 |
| 2 | 600 | 200 |
| 3 | 200 | 1,500 |
| 4 | 3,000 | 20 |
| 5 | 100 | 1,000 |
| 6 | 1,500 | 60 |
| 7 | 80 | 2,500 |
| 8 | 2,000 | 80 |
| 9 | 500 | 150 |
| 10 | 1,200 | 100 |
| 11 | 50 | 3,000 |
| 12 | 800 | 90 |

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Salient Features of a Master Budget:**
1. It is a **comprehensive** plan covering all operations of the business.
2. It is a **consolidated** statement of all functional sub-budgets.
3. It is expressed in **financial terms** (rupees) for a defined period.
4. It is **approved** by top management and the board of directors.
5. It serves as a **blueprint** for the entire organization.
6. It includes both **operating and financial** plans.
7. It acts as a **standard** for subsequent performance evaluation.

**Objectives:**
- Ensure **coordination** among departments to prevent sub-optimization.
- Translate **strategic goals** into actionable financial plans.
- Provide a basis for **control** through variance analysis.
- Facilitate **delegation** without loss of authority.
- Optimize **resource allocation** across activities.
- Communicate **performance expectations** clearly.

**Four Functional Sub-Budgets:**

| **Sub-Budget** | **Purpose** | **Key Input** |
|---|---|---|
| **Sales Budget** | Forecasts expected sales in units and rupees | Market analysis, past data, sales force estimates |
| **Production Budget** | Determines units to be produced | Sales forecast + closing stock policy − opening stock |
| **Cash Budget** | Projects cash inflows and outflows | All operating budgets + financing plans |
| **Material Purchase Budget** | Quantity and cost of materials to buy | Production budget + stock policies + price forecast |

> **(Marks split: 3 for features/objectives, 4 for sub-budget explanation with table.)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — ABC Analysis Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Compute ACV (Annual Consumption Value) for each item**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACV_1 &= 4{,}000 \times 5 = 20{,}000 \\
ACV_2 &= 600 \times 200 = 1{,}20{,}000 \\
ACV_3 &= 200 \times 1{,}500 = 3{,}00{,}000 \\
ACV_4 &= 3{,}000 \times 20 = 60{,}000 \\
ACV_5 &= 100 \times 1{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 \\
ACV_6 &= 1{,}500 \times 60 = 90{,}000 \\
ACV_7 &= 80 \times 2{,}500 = 2{,}00{,}000 \\
ACV_8 &= 2{,}000 \times 80 = 1{,}60{,}000 \\
ACV_9 &= 500 \times 150 = 75{,}000 \\
ACV_{10} &= 1{,}200 \times 100 = 1{,}20{,}000 \\
ACV_{11} &= 50 \times 3{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000 \\
ACV_{12} &= 800 \times 90 = 72{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total ACV** = 20,000 + 1,20,000 + 3,00,000 + 60,000 + 1,00,000 + 90,000 + 2,00,000 + 1,60,000 + 75,000 + 1,20,000 + 1,50,000 + 72,000 = **₹14,67,000**

**Step 2: Rank in Descending Order**

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) | % Value | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 3,00,000 | 20.45 | 20.45 |
| 2 | 7 | 2,00,000 | 13.63 | 34.08 |
| 3 | 8 | 1,60,000 | 10.91 | 44.99 |
| 4 | 11 | 1,50,000 | 10.22 | 55.21 |
| 5 | 2 | 1,20,000 | 8.18 | 63.39 |
| 6 | 10 | 1,20,000 | 8.18 | 71.57 |
| 7 | 5 | 1,00,000 | 6.82 | 78.39 |
| 8 | 6 | 90,000 | 6.13 | 84.52 |
| 9 | 9 | 75,000 | 5.11 | 89.63 |
| 10 | 12 | 72,000 | 4.91 | 94.54 |
| 11 | 4 | 60,000 | 4.09 | 98.63 |
| 12 | 1 | 20,000 | 1.36 | 100.00 |

**Step 3: Apply Standard Cut-offs (A: ≤ 70-75% value, B: 75-90%, C: > 90%)**

| **Class** | **Items** | **No. of Items (% of total)** | **% of Total Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** (Tight control) | 3, 7, 8, 11, 2, 10 | 6 / 12 = **50%** | **71.57%** |
| **B** (Moderate control) | 5, 6, 9 | 3 / 12 = **25%** | **18.06%** |
| **C** (Loose control) | 12, 4, 1 | 3 / 12 = **25%** | **10.36%** |

**Suggested Control Policy:**

- **Class A** (Items 3, 7, 8, 11, 2, 10): Rigorous control — **perpetual inventory records**, continuous stock monitoring, low safety stock, frequent order placements with small lot sizes, daily management attention.
- **Class B** (Items 5, 6, 9): Moderate control — **periodic review** (monthly/quarterly), EOQ-based ordering, middle management supervision.
- **Class C** (Items 12, 4, 1): Loose control — **two-bin system or simple bin cards**, bulk annual purchases, high safety stock to avoid stockouts, minimal clerical attention.

> **(Marks split: 2 for ACV computation, 2 for ranking and cumulative %, 2 for classification, 1 for control policy.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB)**? Discuss its features, advantages, and limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following details are available for M/s Delta Manufacturing Co. for the quarter ending 31st March 2025:

| | **January** | **February** | **March** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Estimated Sales (units)** | 8,000 | 10,000 | 12,000 |
| **Selling Price per unit (₹)** | 100 | 100 | 100 |
| **Desired Closing Stock** | 20% of next month sales | | |
| **Opening Stock on 1st January** | 1,600 units | | |

You are required to prepare a **Sales Budget** and a **Production Budget** for the three months.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB):**

> **Definition (KTU standard):** ZBB is a budgeting method in which **every expense in every line item must be justified afresh** for each new budget period, starting from a "zero base", rather than relying on incremental changes to the previous period's budget.

It was developed by **Peter A. Pyhrr** at Texas Instruments in 1970 and was successfully implemented in the U.S. State of Georgia under Governor Jimmy Carter (1973).

**Features of ZBB:**
1. Every line item starts at **zero**, not at last year's level.
2. Each activity is evaluated as a **"decision package"** — what would be the cost and benefit?
3. Activities are **ranked in order of priority** by management.
4. Resources are allocated based on **ranking and available funds**, not on history.
5. It demands strong **managerial participation** at all levels.

**Advantages:**
- Promotes **efficient resource allocation** by eliminating obsolete or low-priority activities.
- Encourages **cost consciousness** and cost reduction (not just cost control).
- Facilitates **identification and elimination** of wasteful expenditure.
- Aligns budget with **strategic priorities** and current needs.
- Encourages **participative management** and ownership.
- Particularly useful for **discretionary and overhead costs**.

**Limitations:**
- **Time-consuming and costly** to implement; requires extensive paperwork.
- Requires **highly skilled** managers and budget officers.
- Difficult to define **decision units** and rank them objectively.
- Short-term focus may harm **long-term strategic investments**.
- Resistance from department heads who must constantly re-justify.
- May be **unsuitable for small organizations** with limited resources.

> **(Marks split: 1 for definition, 1 for features, 3 for advantages/limitations.)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b): Sales and Production Budget**

**Step 1: Sales Budget**

Sales Budget = Estimated Sales Units × Selling Price

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{January Sales Value} &= 8{,}000 \times 100 = \text{₹ } 8{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{February Sales Value} &= 10{,}000 \times 100 = \text{₹ } 10{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{March Sales Value} &= 12{,}000 \times 100 = \text{₹ } 12{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Total Sales Value (Q1)} &= \text{₹ } 30{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

| **Month** | **Sales Units** | **Selling Price (₹)** | **Sales Value (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| January | 8,000 | 100 | 8,00,000 |
| February | 10,000 | 100 | 10,00,000 |
| March | 12,000 | 100 | 12,00,000 |
| **Total** | **30,000** | | **30,00,000** |

**Step 2: Production Budget**

Using the production identity:

$$PB = \text{Sales} + \text{Desired Closing Stock} - \text{Opening Stock}$$

- **January Closing Stock** = 20% × February sales = 20% × 10,000 = 2,000 units
- **February Closing Stock** = 20% × March sales = 20% × 12,000 = 2,400 units
- **March Closing Stock** = (given as 0 / next period data not available; assume nil or 0)
- **Opening Stock** of January = 1,600 units; Opening stock of February = 2,000 (closing of Jan); Opening stock of March = 2,400 (closing of Feb).

**January Production:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PB_{\text{Jan}} &= 8{,}000 + 2{,}000 - 1{,}600 = 8{,}400 \ \text{units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**February Production:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PB_{\text{Feb}} &= 10{,}000 + 2{,}400 - 2{,}000 = 10{,}400 \ \text{units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**March Production:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PB_{\text{Mar}} &= 12{,}000 + 0 - 2{,}400 = 9{,}600 \ \text{units (assuming nil closing stock for Q1 end)}
\end{aligned}
$$

| **Month** | **Sales (units)** | **Add: Closing Stock** | **Less: Opening Stock** | **Production (units)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| January | 8,000 | 2,000 | 1,600 | 8,400 |
| February | 10,000 | 2,400 | 2,000 | 10,400 |
| March | 12,000 | 0 | 2,400 | 9,600 |
| **Total** | **30,000** | **4,400** | **6,000** | **28,400** |

**Verification (Q1 total):** Sales 30,000 + Closing 4,400 − Opening 1,600 = 32,800 ÷ Wait, recompute with consistent opening:

Using identity at Q1 level: Opening stock = 1,600; Closing stock of Q1 = 0 (if assumed). Total Production = 30,000 + 0 − 1,600 = **28,400 units** ✓ (matches column sum 8,400 + 10,400 + 9,600).

> **(Marks split: 1 for sales value, 3 for closing stock, 3 for production budget with explanation.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes & Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **In ABC Analysis:** Forgetting to rank items in *descending* order of ACV before computing cumulative percentage. ✗ Loss of 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Cumulative % rounding:** If your cumulative % exceeds 100% (e.g., 100.05%), you have a calculation error — recheck.
> 3. **Production Budget Identity:** Writing "Closing + Opening" or inverting signs. The correct identity is **Production = Sales + Closing − Opening**. Memorize this carefully.
> 4. **Cut-off declaration:** Always state the cut-off criteria (e.g., "A: top 70% value, B: next 20%, C: remaining") *before* classifying. Examiners deduct marks if you jump straight to classification.
> 5. **Control policy:** Listing *only* the names of A, B, C without describing the **control intensity** loses you 1-2 marks.
> 6. **ZBB vs Incremental:** Students often confuse ZBB with incremental budgeting. Key distinction: ZBB starts at **zero base**; Incremental adjusts last year.
> 7. **Units consistency:** In ABC Analysis, ensure quantity (units) and cost (₹/unit) are in the *same period* (annual). Mixing monthly and annual values is a common error.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 High-Yield KTU Bullet Points — Read Before Exam:**

- [ ] A **Budget** is a *predetermined, approved, quantified plan* for a *defined future period* — it is a tool of *planning AND control*, unlike a mere estimate.
- [ ] A **Master Budget** = All operating budgets + All financial budgets consolidated.
- [ ] **Production Budget** identity (most asked formula): $PB = \text{Sales Forecast} + \text{Desired Closing Stock} - \text{Opening Stock}$
- [ ] **Cash Budget** identity: $CB_{\text{close}} = \text{Opening Cash} + \text{Total Receipts} - \text{Total Payments} \pm \text{Finance}$
- [ ] **Flexible Budget** identity: $FB = \text{Fixed Cost} + (\text{Variable Cost per unit} \times \text{Actual units})$ — it is the *only* fair basis for comparing actuals at different activity levels.
- [ ] **Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB)** — every expense justified from zero each period. Promoted by **Peter Pyhrr** (1970).
- [ ] **ABC Analysis** is based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** — first applied to inventory by **H.F. Dickie** of General Electric.
- [ ] **Class A**: 10-20% of items / 70-80% of value → **vital few** → **tight control** (perpetual records, frequent review, top management attention).
- [ ] **Class B**: 20-30% of items / 15-25% of value → **moderate control** (EOQ, periodic review).
- [ ] **Class C**: 50-70% of items / 5-10% of value → **trivial many** → **loose control** (two-bin system, bulk orders, low priority).
- [ ] Annual Consumption Value formula: $ACV = \text{Annual Usage Quantity} \times \text{Unit Cost}$ — rank in *descending* order of ACV.
- [ ] Classification logic: Plot cumulative % value against cumulative % items. The *steep initial rise* = Class A. The *gentle middle* = Class B. The *flat tail* = Class C.
- [ ] **Fixed Budget** vs **Flexible Budget**: Fixed = single activity level (useless for control if activity varies); Flexible = multiple levels (the only *fair* yardstick).
- [ ] Budgetary control is a **continuous cycle**: Plan → Prepare → Coordinate → Approve → Implement → Compare → Correct → Repeat.
- [ ] Advantages of budgets: *Planning, Coordination, Motivation, Control, Performance Evaluation, Communication*.
- [ ] Limitations of budgets: *Rigid, Costly, Time-consuming, Estimated basis, May demotivate if used as a stick*.
- [ ] ZBB is best for *discretionary and overhead costs*; less suitable for *routine repetitive operations*.
